# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Time reversal of a signal
(b) Amplitude scaling of a signal

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> (a) **Time reversal** is the operation of replacing the independent variable $t$ with $-t$, producing a signal $x(-t)$ that is the **mirror image** of $x(t)$ about the vertical axis. **Example:** If $x(t) = \sin(t)$, then $x(-t) = \sin(-t) = -\sin(t)$, which is reflected about the origin.
>
> (b) **Amplitude scaling** multiplies every amplitude value of a signal by a constant $A$, producing $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$. **Example:** If $x(t) = \cos(t)$ and $A = 2$, then $y(t) = 2\cos(t)$, which has double the peak amplitude.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the condition for a system to be:**
(a) Linear
(b) Time-invariant
(c) Causal

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> (a) A system $T\{\cdot\}$ is **linear** if it satisfies the **superposition principle**:
>
> $$T\{ a x_1(t) + b x_2(t) \} = a\, T\{x_1(t)\} + b\, T\{x_2(t)\}$$
>
> (b) A system is **time-invariant** if a time-shift $t_0$ in the input produces the same time-shift in the output:
>
> $$T\{ x(t - t_0) \} = y(t - t_0) \quad \text{for all } t_0$$
>
> (c) A system is **causal** if its output at time $t$ depends only on the **present and past** values of the input, i.e., on $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \le t$, and **not on future** values.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In the 14-mark question, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to show the **graph/sketch** of the final signal.
> 2. Confusing **time scaling** ($\vert a \vert > 1$ means compression) with **amplitude scaling**.
> 3. Failing to provide the **support interval** explicitly in the final answer.
> 4. Skipping the **sketch/diagram** which usually carries 2–3 marks.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A signal $x(t)$ is defined as:**

$$
x(t) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & -1 \le t \le 1 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**(a) (7 Marks)** Sketch the signal $y(t) = x(2t - 1)$ and find its mathematical expression. Show all steps.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A new system is described by $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$. Test the system for **linearity** and **time-invariance**. Justify with proper mathematical steps.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-step derivation of $y(t) = x(2t - 1)$**

We rewrite the argument $2t - 1$ as $2\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$. This means we must perform **two operations** on $x(t)$:
- **First:** Time shift to the **right** by $\frac{1}{2}$ unit → this gives $x\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$.
- **Second:** Time compression by factor $2$ → this gives $x\left(2\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)\right) = x(2t - 1)$.

**Step 1 — Time shift by $t_0 = \frac{1}{2}$:**

The original support $[-1, 1]$ becomes $\left[-1 + \frac{1}{2},\; 1 + \frac{1}{2}\right] = \left[-\frac{1}{2},\; \frac{3}{2}\right]$.

**Step 2 — Time compression by $a = 2$:**

The new support is found by dividing each endpoint by $2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New Left End} &= -\frac{1}{2} \div 2 = -\frac{1}{4} \\
\text{New Right End} &= \frac{3}{2} \div 2 = \frac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the mathematical expression is:

$$
y(t) = x(2t - 1) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & -\frac{1}{4} \le t \le \frac{3}{4} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The sketch is a **rectangular pulse of width 1** centred at $t = \frac{1}{4}$ with height 1, occupying the interval $\left[-\frac{1}{4},\, \frac{3}{4}\right]$.

**Part (b) — Linearity and Time-Invariance Test for $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$**

**Linearity Test:**

Let $x_1(t) \to y_1(t) = t \cdot x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t) \to y_2(t) = t \cdot x_2(t)$. For scalars $a, b$:

$$
T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = t \cdot [a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)] = a\, t \cdot x_1(t) + b\, t \cdot x_2(t) = a y_1(t) + b y_2(t)
$$

Since the **superposition principle holds**, the system is **LINEAR**.

**Time-Invariance Test:**

Consider a delayed input $x(t - t_0)$:

$$
T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)
$$

The corresponding delayed output is:

$$
y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)
$$

Comparing the two:

$$
t \cdot x(t - t_0) \;\neq\; (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)
$$

The two expressions are **not equal** (they differ by the term $t_0 \cdot x(t - t_0)$). Hence the system is **TIME-VARIANT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often wrongly conclude that $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is time-invariant because it has a "simple" form. The factor $t$ multiplying $x(t)$ explicitly **breaks** time-invariance because it depends on absolute time.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**A discrete-time signal is defined as:**

$$
x[n] = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = -1, 0, 1, 2 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**(a) (7 Marks)** Sketch the signals $y_1[n] = x[2n]$ and $y_2[n] = x[-n + 1]$. Find their expressions and supports.

**(b) (7 Marks)** For a system with input-output relation $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1] - x[n-3]$, test the system for **linearity**, **time-invariance**, and **causality**. Justify each property.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Discrete-time operations**

**Sub-part (i): $y_1[n] = x[2n]$ (Time compression by 2 in discrete domain)**

For each integer $n$, we evaluate $x[2n]$:
- $n = -1 \Rightarrow 2n = -2 \Rightarrow x[-2] = 0$
- $n = 0 \Rightarrow 2n = 0 \Rightarrow x[0] = 1$
- $n = 1 \Rightarrow 2n = 2 \Rightarrow x[2] = 1$

Therefore:

$$
y_1[n] = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = 0, 1 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The non-zero samples are at $n = 0$ and $n = 1$.

**Sub-part (ii): $y_2[n] = x[-n + 1]$ (Time reversal + shift)**

Rewrite as $y_2[n] = x[-(n - 1)]$. This is equivalent to first **shifting right by 1** and then **time-reversing**.

- **Step 1:** $x[n - 1]$ has support at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.
- **Step 2:** Apply time-reversal: replace $n$ with $-n$. The support at $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ reflects to $\{-3, -2, -1, 0\}$.

Therefore:

$$
y_2[n] = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = -3, -2, -1, 0 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Part (b) — System property tests for $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1] - x[n-3]$**

**Linearity Test:**

Let $x_1[n] \to y_1[n] = x_1[n] + 2x_1[n-1] - x_1[n-3]$ and $x_2[n] \to y_2[n] = x_2[n] + 2x_2[n-1] - x_2[n-3]$.

For scalars $a, b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} &= (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + 2(a x_1[n-1] + b x_2[n-1]) - (a x_1[n-3] + b x_2[n-3]) \\
&= a[x_1[n] + 2x_1[n-1] - x_1[n-3]] + b[x_2[n] + 2x_2[n-1] - x_2[n-3]] \\
&= a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Superposition holds ⇒ System is LINEAR.**

**Time-Invariance Test:**

Delayed input: $x[n - n_0]$.

$$
T\{x[n - n_0]\} = x[n - n_0] + 2x[n - n_0 - 1] - x[n - n_0 - 3]
$$

Delayed output:

$$
y[n - n_0] = x[n - n_0] + 2x[n - n_0 - 1] - x[n - n_0 - 3]
$$

The two expressions are **identical** for **all** $n_0$. **System is TIME-INVARIANT.**

**Causality Test:**

The output $y[n]$ depends on $x[n]$ (present), $x[n-1]$ (past), and $x[n-3]$ (past). **No future values are used.** **System is CAUSAL.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common student error is to misread $x[n-3]$ as a future value. Remember: $n - 3 < n$, so $x[n-3]$ is a **past** sample.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Basic Operations & System Concepts**

- [x] A **signal** is a function of time carrying **information**; a **system** transforms an input signal into an output signal.
- [x] **Time Shifting:** $x(t - t_0)$, with $t_0 > 0$ = right shift (delay), $t_0 < 0$ = left shift (advance).
- [x] **Time Scaling:** $x(at)$, with $\vert a \vert > 1$ = **compression**, $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$ = **expansion**.
- [x] **Time Reversal:** $x(-t)$ — mirror image about the $y$-axis.
- [x] **Amplitude Scaling:** $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ — $A$ multiplies amplitude; $A < 0$ also inverts the signal.
- [x] **Addition & Multiplication** are pointwise operations; product is the basis of **modulation**.
- [x] **Differentiation** gives slope; **Integration** gives accumulated area; they are inverse operations for well-behaved signals.
- [x] **Linear System** satisfies the **superposition principle**: $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$.
- [x] **Time-Invariant System** satisfies $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ for all $t_0$.
- [x] **Causal System** output depends only on present and past inputs, never on future values.
- [x] **BIBO Stable System** produces a bounded output for every bounded input.
- [x] **Memoryless System** output at $t$ depends only on $x(t)$; a **Dynamic System** has memory (uses past/future samples).
- [x] **Invertible System** allows unique recovery of input from output.
- [x] For **composite operations**, follow the order: **shift → scale → reverse** (or as given in the problem).
- [x] For **support intervals**: shifting **adds** $t_0$; scaling by $a$ **divides** endpoints by $a$; reversal **negates** endpoints.
- [x] Always include a **sketch** in 14-mark answers — it carries **2 to 3 marks** typically.
- [x] In **discrete-time systems**, the difference $n - k > 0$ means **future**; $n - k < 0$ means **past**; $n - k = 0$ means **present**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — CLASSIFICATION OF SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Introduction to Classification

In **Signal Processing & Communication**, the very first conceptual step before analyzing, designing, or building any system is to **classify** the signals and the systems we deal with. Classification is the taxonomic framework that allows engineers to predict the behaviour of a signal or system, decide which mathematical tools (Fourier, Laplace, Z-transform) can be applied, and determine the feasibility of real-time implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Classification of Signals** is the systematic categorization of signals based on their mathematical properties such as continuity in time, periodicity, energy/power content, determinism, and symmetry. **Classification of Systems** is the systematic categorization of systems based on their response to inputs — linearity, time-invariance, causality, stability, memory, and invertibility.

## 1.2 Why Classification Matters — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> 🧠 **Analogy — The Medical Triage Room:**
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Before a doctor prescribes treatment, the patient is **classified** — cardiac, orthopaedic, neurological. Each class follows a different protocol. Similarly, in signal processing, we *first* classify a signal (say, as a *periodic energy signal*), and only then can we confidently apply the right tool (Fourier Series, in this case). Misclassification = wrong tool = wrong answer. The same logic applies to systems: classifying a system as **LTI (Linear Time-Invariant)** opens the door to convolution, transfer functions, and frequency-domain analysis. Misclassifying it as LTI when it is non-linear is a fatal engineering error.

## 1.3 The Big Picture — Two Master Taxonomies

**A. Signal Classification** is based purely on the **properties of the signal x(t) or x[n]** itself.

**B. System Classification** is based purely on the **behaviour of the operator T{·}** that maps input to output: y(t) = T{x(t)}.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to remember:**
> - Total signal energy: **E** (measured in Joules, J)
> - Average signal power: **P** (measured in Watts, W)
> - Fundamental period: **T₀** (for CT signals) or **N₀** (for DT signals)
> - Fundamental frequency: **f₀ = 1/T₀** (Hz) or **Ω₀ = 2π/N₀** (rad/sample)

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Signals — Step-by-Step Logic

### **A. Continuous-Time (CT) vs Discrete-Time (DT) Signals**

| Property | Continuous-Time x(t) | Discrete-Time x[n] |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable | Real, continuous $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Integer $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Examples | Speech, ECG, voltage waveform | Daily closing stock price, sampled audio |
| Tools | Laplace, Fourier, CTFT | Z-transform, DTFT, DFT |

---

### **B. Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals**

A signal is **periodic** if it repeats after a fixed interval $T_0$ (CT) or $N_0$ (DT). The fundamental condition is:

$$x(t) = x(t + T_0) \quad \forall \, t \in \mathbb{R}$$

$$x[n] = x[n + N_0] \quad \forall \, n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

> The smallest positive $T_0$ or $N_0$ is called the **fundamental period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** Periodic signals can be represented by **Fourier Series** (CT) or **Discrete-Time Fourier Series**. Aperiodic signals require the **Fourier Transform** (CTFT) or **DTFT**. Applying the wrong tool yields nonsensical results.

---

### **C. Deterministic vs Random Signals**

- **Deterministic:** Can be completely described by an explicit mathematical expression. Example: $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$.
- **Random (Stochastic):** Behaviour described only in probabilistic terms. Example: Thermal noise, $n(t)$.

---

### **D. Energy Signals vs Power Signals** ⚡ (Most High-Yield for KTU Exams)

This classification is based on whether the signal has **finite energy** or **finite (non-zero) power**.

**Energy of a Continuous-Time Signal:**

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt$$

**Power of a Continuous-Time Signal:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt$$

**Energy of a Discrete-Time Signal:**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$$

**Power of a Discrete-Time Signal:**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Valuation:** A signal **cannot be both an energy AND a power signal simultaneously** (except the trivial zero signal). It is *either* energy, *or* power, *or* neither. Examiners award full marks only when this rule is explicitly stated.

---

### **E. Even and Odd Signals — Symmetry Classification**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Even part)}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Odd part)}$$

$$\therefore x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

> **Real-world utility:** Even signals have a *real* Fourier Transform; odd signals have an *imaginary* Fourier Transform. This dramatically simplifies spectral analysis in communication receivers.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Systems — Step-by-Step Logic

### **A. Linear vs Non-Linear Systems**

A system $T$ is **linear** if it obeys superposition:

$$T\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = a\,T\{x_1(t)\} + b\,T\{x_2(t)\}$$

This single equation contains two laws: **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**.

---

### **B. Time-Invariant (TI) vs Time-Varying (TV) Systems**

A system is **time-invariant** if a time-shift in the input produces an identical time-shift in the output:

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; y(t - t_0) = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$$

---

### **C. Causal vs Non-Causal Systems**

A system is **causal** if the output at any time $t$ depends *only* on present and past input values, **never** on future values:

$$y(t) = f\{x(\tau) : \tau \leq t\}$$

---

### **D. Stable vs Unstable Systems (BIBO Stability)**

A system is **BIBO Stable (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stable)** if every bounded input produces a bounded output. The **necessary and sufficient condition** is:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty \quad \text{(CT)}$$

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty \quad \text{(DT)}$$

where $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ is the **impulse response** of the system.

---

### **E. Static (Memoryless) vs Dynamic (With Memory) Systems**

- **Static:** Output depends *only* on present input. Example: $y(t) = 2x(t)$.
- **Dynamic:** Output depends on past/future inputs. Example: $y(t) = x(t-1) + x(t)$.

---

### **F. Invertible vs Non-Invertible Systems**

A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs, i.e., the input can be uniquely recovered from the output via an inverse system $T^{-1}$.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CT Energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt$ | Joules (J); $0 < E < \infty$ for energy signal |
| 2 | CT Power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt$ | Watts (W); $0 < P < \infty$ for power signal |
| 3 | DT Energy | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | DT Power | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Watts (W) |
| 5 | Periodicity (CT) | $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ | Smallest $+ve \; T_0$ is fundamental period |
| 6 | Periodicity (DT) | $x[n] = x[n + N_0]$ | Smallest $+ve \; N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$ is fundamental period |
| 7 | Even-Odd Decomposition | $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \; x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ |
| 8 | Linearity | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | Must hold for *all* $x_1, x_2, a, b$ |
| 9 | Time-Invariance | $y(t-t_0) = T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | Output shift matches input shift |
| 10 | BIBO Stability (CT) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | Absolute integrability of impulse response |
| 11 | BIBO Stability (DT) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolute summability of impulse response |
| 12 | Causal System | $y(t)$ depends on $x(\tau), \; \tau \leq t$ | No future-input dependence |
| 13 | Power relation to Energy | $P = 0$ for energy signal; $E = \infty$ for power signal | Mutual exclusivity |

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy of a Decaying Exponential $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$, $a > 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Determine if $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ is an energy signal, a power signal, or neither. Compute the relevant quantity.

**Step 1 — Identify the Signal**

$x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ exists only for $t \geq 0$ because of the unit-step $u(t)$.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Energy $E$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt \\
  &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( e^{-at} \right)^{2} \, dt \quad \text{(since } x(t)=0 \text{ for } t<0 \text{)} \\
  &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \left[ \frac{e^{-2at}}{-2a} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
  &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{e^{-2aT}}{-2a} - \frac{e^{0}}{-2a} \right) \\
  &= 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{2a} \right) \\
  &= \frac{1}{2a} \quad \text{Joules}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion on Energy**

Since $0 < E = \frac{1}{2a} < \infty$ for $a > 0$, the signal is a **finite-energy signal** ✔.

**Step 5 — Compute Average Power $P$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \, dt \\
  &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{1}{2a} \\
  &= 0 \quad \text{(since } \frac{1}{2T} \to 0 \text{ as } T \to \infty \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Classification**

$$P = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad 0 < E < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{x(t) = e^{-at}u(t) \text{ is an ENERGY signal}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power of a Sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$

**Step 1 — Square the Signal**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert x(t) \vert^{2} &= A^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega_0 t + \phi) \\
&= A^{2} \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \quad \text{(using } \cos^{2}\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^{2} \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt \\
  &= \frac{A^{2}}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt + \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right] \\
  &= \frac{A^{2}}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ 2T + 0 \right] \\
  &= \frac{A^{2}}{2} \quad \text{Watts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The cosine term integrates to zero over a symmetric interval because it is a periodic, zero-mean signal.

**Step 3 — Compute Total Energy**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{A^{2}}{2} \left[ 1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \right] dt = \infty$$

**Step 4 — Final Classification**

$$0 < P = \frac{A^{2}}{2} < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad E = \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) \text{ is a POWER signal}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Testing System Linearity

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Test:** For system $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$, apply the linearity axiom $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\}$ and check whether it equals $a y_1 + b y_2$. If *yes for all* $a, b, x_1, x_2$ → Linear. If even *one counter-example* exists → Non-Linear.

**Test Case: $y(t) = x^{2}(t)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} &= (a x_1 + b x_2)^{2} \\
&= a^{2} x_1^{2} + b^{2} x_2^{2} + 2ab x_1 x_2 \\
&\neq a x_1^{2} + b x_2^{2} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-term $2ab x_1 x_2$ violates superposition. **Conclusion: Non-Linear System** ❌.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Testing Time-Invariance

**Test Case: $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$**

**Step 1 — Apply a delay $t_0$ to the input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the output for delayed input:**

$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the delayed output:**

$$y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 4 — Compare $y_1(t)$ vs $y(t - t_0)$:**

$$
y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t - t_0) \quad \neq \quad (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0) = y(t - t_0)
$$

**Conclusion: Time-Varying System** ❌.

---

## 3.5 The Even–Odd Decomposition Theorem — Full Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** Every real signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as the sum of its even part $x_e(t)$ and odd part $x_o(t)$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Define:} \quad x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
\\
\text{Add them:} \quad x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{2x(t)}{2} = x(t) \;\;\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify even symmetry of $x_e$:**

$$
x_e(-t) = \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t) \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Verify odd symmetry of $x_o$:**

$$
x_o(-t) = \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -x_o(t) \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Engineering Utility:** In communication transceivers, this decomposition is the mathematical foundation of **Hilbert Transforms** and **Single-Sideband (SSB) modulation**.

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between energy signal and power signal. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Total Energy $E$ | Finite, $0 < E < \infty$ | Infinite, $E = \infty$ |
| Average Power $P$ | Zero, $P = 0$ | Finite, $0 < P < \infty$ |
| Duration | Finite-duration or decaying | Persistent (extends to $\pm \infty$) |
| Example | $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ | $x(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t)$ |

For the energy signal example: $E = \int_0^\infty e^{-4t} dt = \frac{1}{4}$ J; $P = 0$. ✔
For the power signal example: $P = \frac{5^2}{2} = 12.5$ W; $E = \infty$. ✔

---

### **Q2. State the condition for BIBO stability of a continuous-time LTI system. Is the system with impulse response $h(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$ BIBO stable?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Condition:** A CT-LTI system is BIBO stable *if and only if* its impulse response is **absolutely integrable**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

**Test for the given system:**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-t} \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t} \, dt = \left[ -e^{-t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - (-1) = 1 < \infty \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO Stable** ✔.

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Classify the following signals based on (i) continuity, (ii) periodicity, (iii) energy/power nature. Justify each classification with necessary calculations.** **(7 marks)**

1. $x_1(t) = e^{-3t} \cos(2t) \, u(t)$
2. $x_2(t) = 10 \sin(50\pi t + \pi/4)$
3. $x_3[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$

**Model Solution:**

**Signal 1: $x_1(t) = e^{-3t} \cos(2t) \, u(t)$**

- **(i) Continuity:** Continuous in time $t$ → **Continuous-Time signal** ✔
- **(ii) Periodicity:** The exponential envelope $e^{-3t}$ breaks periodicity → **Aperiodic** ❌
- **(iii) Energy/Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( e^{-3t} \cos(2t) \right)^{2} dt \\
  &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \cos^{2}(2t) \, dt \\
  &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(4t)}{2} \, dt \\
  &= \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \, dt + \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t}\cos(4t) \, dt \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{36 + 16} = \frac{1}{12} + \frac{6}{104} < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Finite energy, $P = 0$ → **Energy Signal** ✔

**Signal 2: $x_2(t) = 10 \sin(50\pi t + \pi/4)$**

- **(i) Continuity:** Continuous → **Continuous-Time** ✔
- **(ii) Periodicity:** Sinusoid with $\omega_0 = 50\pi$ rad/s → $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{50\pi} = 0.04$ s → **Periodic** ✔
- **(iii) Energy/Power:** $P = \frac{10^2}{2} = 50$ W, $E = \infty$ → **Power Signal** ✔

**Signal 3: $x_3[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$**

- **(i) Continuity:** Discrete index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ → **Discrete-Time** ✔
- **(ii) Periodicity:** Geometric decay → **Aperiodic** ❌
- **(iii) Energy/Power:**

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - 1/4} = \frac{4}{3} \;\;\text{J} \;\;\checkmark$$

→ **Energy Signal** ✔

---

**(b) Determine the even and odd components of $x(t) = (1 + t + t^{2})\cos(t)$. Plot or describe the symmetry properties.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using the decomposition formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
       &= \frac{(1 + t + t^{2})\cos(t) + (1 - t + t^{2})\cos(-t)}{2} \\
       &= \frac{(1 + t + t^{2})\cos(t) + (1 - t + t^{2})\cos(t)}{2} \quad (\cos(-t) = \cos(t)) \\
       &= \frac{\cos(t)\left[(1 + t + t^{2}) + (1 - t + t^{2})\right]}{2} \\
       &= \frac{\cos(t) \cdot (2 + 2t^{2})}{2} = (1 + t^{2})\cos(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
       &= \frac{(1 + t + t^{2})\cos(t) - (1 - t + t^{2})\cos(t)}{2} \\
       &= \frac{\cos(t) \cdot 2t}{2} = t \cos(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = (1 + t^{2})\cos(t) + t\cos(t) = (1 + t + t^{2})\cos(t) = x(t)$ ✔

**Symmetry Properties:**
- $x_e(t) = (1 + t^{2})\cos(t)$: **Even** because $x_e(-t) = (1 + (-t)^2)\cos(-t) = (1+t^2)\cos(t) = x_e(t)$ ✔
- $x_o(t) = t\cos(t)$: **Odd** because $x_o(-t) = (-t)\cos(-t) = -t\cos(t) = -x_o(t)$ ✔

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Define linearity and time-invariance of a system. Test the system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ for both properties.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Linearity:** A system obeys superposition — $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$.
- **Time-Invariance:** $y(t - t_0) = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$ for any delay $t_0$.

**Test 1: Linearity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} &= t \cdot (a x_1 + b x_2) + \frac{d}{dt}(a x_1 + b x_2) \\
                  &= a \left( t x_1 + \frac{dx_1}{dt} \right) + b \left( t x_2 + \frac{dx_2}{dt} \right) \\
                  &= a \, T\{x_1\} + b \, T\{x_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ **Linear** ✔

**Test 2: Time-Invariance**

Apply delay to input first:

$$
y_1(t) = T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0) + \frac{d}{dt} x(t - t_0)
$$

Apply delay to output:

$$
y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) x(t - t_0) + \frac{d}{dt} x(t - t_0)
$$

Comparing:

$$y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t - t_0) + x'(t - t_0) \quad \neq \quad (t - t_0) x(t - t_0) + x'(t - t_0) = y(t - t_0)$$

The coefficient $t$ is the culprit. **Time-Varying** ❌

**Final Classification:** **Linear but Time-Varying (LTV)** system.

---

**(b) Explain causality and BIBO stability. Check whether the system $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1] - x[n-3]$ is causal and stable.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Causality Definition:** A system is causal if the output at time $t$ (or $n$) depends only on present and past inputs, not on future inputs.

**BIBO Stability Definition:** Every bounded input $x(\cdot)$ with $\vert x(\cdot) \vert \leq M$ produces a bounded output $\vert y(\cdot) \vert \leq M'$. For an LTI system, the necessary and sufficient condition is absolute summability of the impulse response: $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.

**Test 1: Causality of $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1] - x[n-3]$**

The output at index $n$ uses:
- $x[n]$ — present input ✔
- $x[n-1]$ — past input (delay of 1 sample) ✔
- $x[n-3]$ — past input (delay of 3 samples) ✔

No future inputs are referenced. **Therefore, the system is Causal** ✔

**Test 2: BIBO Stability**

The impulse response is obtained by applying $x[n] = \delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] - \delta[n-3]$$

This gives:
- $h[0] = 1$
- $h[1] = 2$
- $h[3] = -1$
- $h[n] = 0$ for all other $n$

Check absolute summability:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert + \vert -1 \vert = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 < \infty \;\;\checkmark$$

**Therefore, the system is BIBO Stable** ✔

**Final Classification:** **Causal and BIBO-Stable LTI System** ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Energy & Power Mutex Error:** Students often write "the signal is an energy signal with power $P > 0$". This is **logically impossible** and will cost 1–2 marks. Always state the mutual exclusivity explicitly.
> 2. **Linearity Counter-Example Mistake:** Saying "$y(t) = x^2(t)$ is linear because squaring is a smooth operation" — wrong. Always perform the algebraic superposition test. A single counter-example is sufficient to declare non-linearity.
> 3. **Causality Misjudgment:** Students confuse causality with "real-time implementation". Causality is a *mathematical* property — if the formula has $x[n+1]$, it is non-causal *even if* the system is later implemented in real time using a delay line.
> 4. **Stability Condition Omission:** The BIBO condition is *necessary AND sufficient* for LTI systems. For non-LTI systems, the impulse response test does *not* apply — KTU examiners deduct marks if you don't qualify the statement.
> 5. **Periodicity in DT:** A discrete signal $x[n]$ with fundamental period $N_0$ requires $N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$. If the computed $N_0$ is not an integer, the DT signal is **aperiodic**, even if its continuous counterpart is periodic.

---

## 🏁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember High-Yield Points for KTU 2024:**

- 📌 **Signal Classification = 5 Axes:** CT/DT, Periodic/Aperiodic, Deterministic/Random, Energy/Power, Even/Odd.
- 📌 **System Classification = 6 Axes:** Linear/Non-Linear, TI/TV, Causal/Non-Causal, Stable/Unstable, Static/Dynamic, Invertible/Non-Invertible.
- 📌 **Energy Signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$. **Power Signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$. Mutually exclusive.
- 📌 **BIBO Stability (CT):** $\int \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$. **BIBO Stability (DT):** $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.
- 📌 **Linearity Test:** Superposition with **both** additivity and homogeneity.
- 📌 **Time-Invariance Test:** Delay output $\stackrel{?}{=}$ Output of delayed input.
- 📌 **Causality Test:** No $x(t+\tau)$ for $\tau > 0$ (CT) or no $x[n+k]$ for $k > 0$ (DT).
- 📌 **Even-Odd Decomposition:** $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$, $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$.
- 📌 **Energy of $e^{-at}u(t)$, $a>0$:** $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ J.
- 📌 **Power of $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$:** $P = \frac{A^2}{2}$ W.
- 📌 **LTI = Linear + Time-Invariant.** This unlocks convolution, Fourier methods, and transfer functions.
- 📌 **In communication systems:** Energy signals = transient data bursts; Power signals = continuous carriers (e.g., radio sinusoids).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — Properties of Systems: LINEARITY

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LINEARITY — THE GOLDEN PROPERTY OF SYSTEMS**
> A system $\mathcal{T}$ is said to be **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Principle of Superposition**, which is the combined application of two fundamental properties:
> 1. **Additivity** (Superposition)
> 2. **Homogeneity** (Scaling)

Mathematically, for any two inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ with corresponding outputs $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$, the system is linear if:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a \, y_1(t) + b \, y_2(t)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary scalar (real or complex) constants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero-In-State Test (Necessary Condition):**
> A linear system **must** produce a **zero output for a zero input**.
> $$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$$
> If this fails, the system is **non-linear** — guaranteed. This is the **first test** every KTU examiner expects you to apply.

## 1.2 Two Sub-Properties

### A. Additivity (Superposition Property)
If the system produces $y_1(t)$ for $x_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ for $x_2(t)$, then it must produce $y_1(t) + y_2(t)$ for $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$.

$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = y_1(t) + y_2(t)$$

### B. Homogeneity (Scaling Property)
If the system produces $y(t)$ for $x(t)$, then scaling the input by a constant $a$ must scale the output by the **same** constant.

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \, x(t)\} = a \, y(t)$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **The Water Tap Analogy:**
> Imagine a **water tank with a tap**. The input is the water flow rate into the tank, and the output is the water flow rate out of the tap.
> - If you **double** the input water flow (homogeneity), the output water flow also **doubles** — proportional response.
> - If you turn on **two taps simultaneously** (additivity), the total output equals the **sum** of individual outputs.
> - If you **shut the input completely** (zero input), **no water flows out** (zero output).
> This proportional, additive behavior is the essence of **linearity**.

A **non-linear** system would be like a tap whose flow rate depends on the *square* of the input — double the input, you get **four times** the output (parabolic response).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Anatomy of Linearity

Let us deconstruct the linearity condition into its **logical building blocks**:

### Step 1: System Definition
A system $\mathcal{T}$ is an operator that maps an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$:
$$x(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y(t)$$

### Step 2: Homogeneity Test
- Apply scaled input $a \cdot x(t)$ to the system.
- Required response: $a \cdot y(t)$.
- **Violation indicator:** Output scaling factor differs from $a$, or depends on $x(t)$.

### Step 3: Additivity Test
- Apply sum of two inputs $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ to the system.
- Required response: $y_1(t) + y_2(t)$.
- **Violation indicator:** Cross-term appears (e.g., $2 x_1(t) x_2(t)$).

### Step 4: Combined Superposition Test
- Apply $a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)$.
- Required response: $a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)$.
- **Why this matters:** It is the **only** condition you must verify in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| **Property** | **Mathematical Condition** | **Test Method** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Homogeneity** | $\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$ | Scale input, check if output scales by same factor |
| **Additivity** | $\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$ | Apply sum, check if output is sum of individual outputs |
| **Linearity (Combined)** | $\mathcal{T}\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a y_1(t) + b y_2(t)$ | Apply weighted sum, verify no extra terms |
| **Zero-Input Test** | $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ | Apply zero input; if output is non-zero, system is non-linear |
| **Incremental Linearity** | $y(t) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} + c$ where $c$ is independent of $x(t)$ | Linear in **increments**, but has DC bias |

## 2.3 Critical "Trap" Systems for KTU Exams 🎯

> [!WARNING]
> **Constant Term Trap:**
> $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ is **NOT linear** — the constant $5$ violates the zero-input condition.
> $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5 \cdot x(0)$ — depends on context (if $x(0)$ is an initial condition, this can be an **LTI** system).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Linearity

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|
| Communication Systems | Superposition enables analysis of multiple carriers simultaneously |
| Filter Design | Linear filters (FIR, IIR) preserve signal integrity |
| Fourier/Laplace Analysis | All frequency-domain tools assume linearity |
| Control Systems | Linear control enables transfer function analysis |
| Audio Processing | Mixers, amplifiers are designed to be linear to avoid distortion |

## 2.5 Linear vs Non-Linear — Quick Reference

| **System** | **Equation** | **Linear?** | **Reason** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Amplifier | $y(t) = Kx(t)$ | ✅ Yes | Pure scaling |
| Differentiator | $y(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | ✅ Yes | Linear operator |
| Integrator | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | ✅ Yes | Linear operator |
| DC Offset | $y(t) = x(t) + C$ | ❌ No | Constant violates zero-input |
| Squarer | $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | ❌ No | Non-linear operation |
| Modulator | $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ | ✅ Yes | Multiplication by known signal is linear |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Linearity Test of $y(t) = 3x(t) + 2$

### Given:
$$y(t) = 3x(t) + 2$$

### Step 1: Zero-Input Test
Substitute $x(t) = 0$:
$$y(t) = 3(0) + 2 = 2$$

Since $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 2 \neq 0$, the **zero-input test fails**.

### Step 2: Homogeneity Test
Compute $\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\}$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = 3[a\,x(t)] + 2 = 3a\,x(t) + 2$$

Compare with $a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$:
$$a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a[3x(t) + 2] = 3a\,x(t) + 2a$$

### Step 3: Equate and Compare
$$3a\,x(t) + 2 \neq 3a\,x(t) + 2a \quad (\text{unless } a=1)$$

**Conclusion:** ❌ **The system is NON-LINEAR** due to the constant term **2**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Linearity Test of $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$

### Given:
$$y(t) = x(t) \cos(\omega_c t)$$

### Step 1: Zero-Input Test
$$y(t) = 0 \cdot \cos(\omega_c t) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2: Homogeneity Test
Apply $a \cdot x(t)$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,x(t) \cos(\omega_c t)$$

Required: $a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$:
$$a \cdot [x(t) \cos(\omega_c t)] = a\,x(t) \cos(\omega_c t) \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Additivity Test
Apply $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = [x_1(t) + x_2(t)] \cos(\omega_c t) = y_1(t) + y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4: Combined Linearity
Apply $a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = [a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$$
$$= a\,x_1(t)\cos(\omega_c t) + b\,x_2(t)\cos(\omega_c t) = a\,y_1(t) + b\,y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** ✅ **The system is LINEAR**. Multiplication by a **known deterministic signal** is a linear operation — this is the foundation of **modulation**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Linearity Test of $y(t) = x^2(t)$

### Given:
$$y(t) = x^2(t) = [x(t)]^2$$

### Step 1: Zero-Input Test
$$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0^2 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (passes first test)}$$

### Step 2: Homogeneity Test
Apply $a\,x(t)$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = [a\,x(t)]^2 = a^2 x^2(t)$$

Required: $a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$:
$$a \cdot x^2(t) = a\,x^2(t)$$

### Step 3: Equate and Compare
$$a^2\,x^2(t) \neq a\,x^2(t) \quad (\text{unless } a = 0 \text{ or } a = 1)$$

**Conclusion:** ❌ **The system is NON-LINEAR** due to the squaring operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The squaring operation creates **cross-terms** when applied to a sum:
> $$\mathcal{T}\{x_1 + x_2\} = (x_1+x_2)^2 = x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2 \neq x_1^2 + x_2^2$$
> The extra $2x_1 x_2$ term is the **smoking gun** of non-linearity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Linearity Test of $y[n] = 2x[n] + 3x[n-1]$ (Discrete)

### Given:
$$y[n] = 2x[n] + 3x[n-1]$$

### Step 1: Apply Zero Input
$$y[n] = 2(0) + 3(0) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2: Apply Scaled Input $a\,x[n]$
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x[n]\} = 2a\,x[n] + 3a\,x[n-1] = a(2x[n] + 3x[n-1]) = a\,y[n] \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Apply Sum of Inputs $x_1[n] + x_2[n]$
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = 2[x_1[n]+x_2[n]] + 3[x_1[n-1]+x_2[n-1]]$$
$$= [2x_1[n] + 3x_1[n-1]] + [2x_2[n] + 3x_2[n-1]] = y_1[n] + y_2[n] \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** ✅ **The system is LINEAR**. This is a classic **FIR filter** structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linearity Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: System y = T x t "] --> B["Step 1: Zero Input Test x t = 0"]
    B --> C{"Is y t = 0?"}
    C -->|"NO"| D["NON-LINEAR STOP"]
    C -->|"YES"| E["Step 2: Homogeneity Test T a x t = a T x t ?"]
    E -->|"NO"| D
    E -->|"YES"| F["Step 3: Additivity Test T x1 x2 = T x1 + T x2 ?"]
    F -->|"NO"| D
    F -->|"YES"| G["LINEAR CONFIRMED"]
    D --> H["Identify source: constant, square, log, exp, modulus, etc."]
    G --> I["Proceed to test Time Invariance for LTI classification"]
```

## 4.2 Superposition Principle — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Layer"]
        X1["x1 t scaled by a"]
        X2["x2 t scaled by b"]
    end

    subgraph SUM1["Summing Junction"]
        S1["a x1 t + b x2 t"]
    end

    subgraph SYS["System T"]
        T["T is Linear"]
    end

    subgraph SUM2["Output Layer"]
        S2["a y1 t + b y2 t"]
        Y1["y1 t = T x1 t"]
        Y2["y2 t = T x2 t"]
    end

    X1 --> S1
    X2 --> S1
    S1 --> T
    T --> S2

    X1 -.->|"separate path"| Y1
    X2 -.->|"separate path"| Y2
    Y1 --> S2
    Y2 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Linear vs Non-Linear System Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["System Definition"] --> Q1{"Contains constant term independent of x t ?"}
    Q1 -->|"YES"| NL1["NON-LINEAR Class: DC Offset System"]
    Q1 -->|"NO"| Q2{"Contains x squared, x cubed, or higher powers?"}
    Q2 -->|"YES"| NL2["NON-LINEAR Class: Polynomial Nonlinearity"]
    Q2 -->|"NO"| Q3{"Contains mod, abs, sin, cos of x t?"}
    Q3 -->|"YES"| NL3["NON-LINEAR Class: Trigonometric Nonlinearity"]
    Q3 -->|"NO"| Q4{"Contains multiplication of x t with itself?"}
    Q4 -->|"YES"| NL4["NON-LINEAR Class: Multiplicative Nonlinearity"]
    Q4 -->|"NO"| LIN["LINEAR: Passes Superposition Test"]
```

## 4.4 The Incremental Linearity Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LIN["Linear System T"]
        T1["y t = T x t"]
    end

    subgraph INCR["Incrementally Linear System"]
        T2["y t = T x t + C"]
        C1["C is constant independent of x t"]
    end

    subgraph DIFF["Difference yields linear"]
        D["y1 t - y2 t = T x1 t - T x2 t = T x1 - x2 t"]
    end

    LIN --> DIFF1["Zero input gives zero output"]
    INCR --> DIFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define linearity of a system. State the two conditions a system must satisfy to be classified as linear.**

**Model Answer:**

Linearity is a fundamental property of a system that allows the principle of superposition to hold. A system $\mathcal{T}$ is **linear** if it satisfies the following two conditions:

**1. Additivity (Superposition):**
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

**2. Homogeneity (Scaling):**
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

These can be combined into a single condition:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = a\,y_1(t) + b\,y_2(t)$$

A **necessary condition** is that zero input must produce zero output: $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Check whether the system $y(t) = \sin[x(t)]$ is linear or non-linear.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Homogeneity Test:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = \sin[a\,x(t)]$$
$$a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a\sin[x(t)]$$

Since $\sin[a\,x(t)] \neq a\sin[x(t)]$ in general (e.g., $\sin[2x] \neq 2\sin[x]$), homogeneity fails.

**Step 2 — Additivity Test:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \sin[x_1(t) + x_2(t)] \neq \sin[x_1(t)] + \sin[x_2(t)]$$

**Conclusion:** ❌ The system $y(t) = \sin[x(t)]$ is **NON-LINEAR**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** Define linearity and homogeneity of a system. Explain the procedure to test linearity of a system with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Check whether the following systems are linear or non-linear:
   - (i) $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$
   - (ii) $y(t) = x(t) + x^2(t)$
   - (iii) $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (a)

**Definition of Linearity:**
A system $\mathcal{T}$ is linear if it satisfies both homogeneity and additivity:
$$\mathcal{T}\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

**Definition of Homogeneity:**
If $x(t) \rightarrow y(t)$, then for any constant $a$: $a\,x(t) \rightarrow a\,y(t)$.
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

**Procedure to Test Linearity:**

1. **Step 1 — Zero Input Test:** Apply $x(t) = 0$. If $y(t) \neq 0$, conclude non-linear immediately.
2. **Step 2 — Homogeneity:** Apply $a\,x(t)$ and verify $\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$.
3. **Step 3 — Additivity:** Apply $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ and verify $\mathcal{T}\{x_1+x_2\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$.
4. **Step 4 — Combined Test:** Apply $a x_1 + b x_2$ and verify it equals $a y_1 + b y_2$.

**Example:** $y(t) = 5x(t)$ — Homogeneity: $5[a\,x(t)] = a[5x(t)]$ ✓ — Additivity: $5[x_1+x_2] = 5x_1 + 5x_2$ ✓ — Hence **linear**.

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (b)

**(i) $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$:**

**Zero input test:** $y(t) = t \cdot 0 = 0$ ✓

**Homogeneity:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = t \cdot [a\,x(t)] = a\,t\,x(t)$$
$$a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a \cdot [t\,x(t)] = a\,t\,x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Additivity:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1 + x_2\} = t[x_1(t) + x_2(t)] = t\,x_1(t) + t\,x_2(t) = y_1 + y_2 \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **System (i) is LINEAR** (multiplication by known time function $t$ is linear).

**(ii) $y(t) = x(t) + x^2(t)$:**

**Homogeneity:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,x(t) + a^2 x^2(t)$$
$$a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a x(t) + a x^2(t)$$

Since $a^2 x^2(t) \neq a x^2(t)$ in general, homogeneity fails.

❌ **System (ii) is NON-LINEAR** due to the squared term.

**(iii) $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$:**

**Zero input test:** $e^0 = 1 \neq 0$ — fails immediately.

❌ **System (iii) is NON-LINEAR** (exponential operation is non-linear).

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**a)** Explain the principle of superposition with a neat mathematical formulation. How is it related to linearity? **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Determine whether the following systems are linear:
   - (i) $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$
   - (ii) $y(t) = 2x(t-3) + 5$
   - (iii) $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (a)

**Principle of Superposition:**
The principle of superposition states that the response of a linear system to a weighted sum of inputs equals the weighted sum of the individual responses to each input acting alone.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
If $x_1(t) \rightarrow y_1(t)$ and $x_2(t) \rightarrow y_2(t)$, then for any constants $a, b$:
$$a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} a\,y_1(t) + b\,y_2(t)$$

**Two Component Properties:**
- **Additivity:** $x_1(t) + x_2(t) \rightarrow y_1(t) + y_2(t)$
- **Homogeneity:** $a\,x(t) \rightarrow a\,y(t)$

**Relation to Linearity:**
A system is **linear if and only if** the superposition principle holds. Linearity is essentially the defining axiom that makes superposition valid. Without linearity, signals cannot be decomposed into simpler components (e.g., sinusoids via Fourier analysis) for analysis. Superposition is the **operational expression** of linearity.

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (b)

**(i) $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$:**

$$\mathcal{T}\{a x[n]\} = n \cdot a x[n] = a \cdot n x[n] = a\,y[n] \quad \checkmark$$
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1 + x_2\} = n[x_1 + x_2] = n x_1 + n x_2 = y_1 + y_2 \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **System (i) is LINEAR** (multiplication by independent variable $n$).

**(ii) $y(t) = 2x(t-3) + 5$:**

**Zero input test:** $y(t) = 2 \cdot 0 + 5 = 5 \neq 0$ — **fails immediately**.

$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(t)\} = 2a\,x(t-3) + 5$$
$$a\,\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = 2a\,x(t-3) + 5a$$

Since $5 \neq 5a$ for general $a$, homogeneity fails.

❌ **System (ii) is NON-LINEAR** due to the constant $5$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students identify $y(t) = 2x(t-3) + 5$ as linear because it "looks like an LTI equation." The constant **5** disqualifies it. To make it linear, write it as $y(t) = 2x(t-3)$ or treat the constant as an **initial condition** of an integrator.

**(iii) $y(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$:**

**Zero input test:** $\int_{-\infty}^{t} 0 \, d\tau = 0$ ✓

**Homogeneity:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{a\,x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} a\,x(\tau)\,d\tau = a\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau = a\,y(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Additivity:**
$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1+x_2\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t}[x_1(\tau)+x_2(\tau)]\,d\tau = y_1(t) + y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **System (iii) is LINEAR** (integration is a linear operator).

---

## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Linearity Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Zero-Input Test:** Students jump directly to homogeneity/additivity tests and lose 1-2 marks if the system has a constant term. Always start with $x(t) = 0$.
> 2. **Confusing Linear with Incremental Linearity:** $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ is **non-linear** in absolute terms but **incrementally linear** — output changes are linear in input changes. State this clearly in your answer.
> 3. **Not Explicitly Showing Cross-Terms:** For $y = x^2$, you must show $(x_1+x_2)^2 = x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2$ and highlight the offending $2x_1 x_2$ term.
> 4. **Missing "Linear but Time-Variant":** A linear system need NOT be time-invariant. $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is linear but NOT LTI. KTU expects you to distinguish these.
> 5. **Skipping Final Conclusion:** Always end with a clear "Therefore, the system is LINEAR / NON-LINEAR" statement for full marks.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| **#** | **Key Takeaway** |
|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Linearity = Additivity + Homogeneity (combined = Superposition) |
| 2 | **First test always:** $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ — if fails, system is non-linear |
| 3 | Linear operators: scaling, summation, integration, differentiation, time-shift, multiplication by known signal |
| 4 | Non-linear operations: $x^2$, $x^3$, $\vert x \vert$, $\sin(x)$, $\cos(x)$, $\log(x)$, $e^x$, $x_1 \cdot x_2$ |
| 5 | Constant term $C$ (independent of $x$) makes system **non-linear** |
| 6 | Linear ≠ Time-Invariant (LTI requires both) |
| 7 | System $y = ax + b$ is **linear only if $b = 0$** |
| 8 | Incremental linearity: $y_1 - y_2 = \mathcal{T}\{x_1 - x_2\}$ still holds for $y = ax + b$ |
| 9 | FIR filters $y[n] = \sum a_k x[n-k]$ are **always linear** |
| 10 | Modulation $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ is **linear** (multiplication by known signal) |
| 11 | Squaring $y = x^2$ creates cross-term $2x_1 x_2$ — definitive proof of non-linearity |
| 12 | Integration $\int x(\tau)d\tau$ is linear — fundamental to LTI system theory |
| 13 | In KTU 14-mark questions: **Always show all three tests** for full marks |
| 14 | Distinguish "linear in mathematics" vs "linear in engineering" — engineering includes zero-input/output condition |
| 15 | Remember: Linearity is the **backbone** of Fourier, Laplace, and Z-transform analysis |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Invariance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Invariance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Invariance (TI)** is a fundamental property of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** systems. A system $\mathcal{T}$ is said to be **time-invariant** if and only if a time shift (delay or advance) applied to the input signal produces an **identical time shift** in the output signal, with **no change in shape, amplitude, or any other characteristic**.

Mathematically, if the system operation is denoted as:

$$y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

then for the system to be time-invariant, the following condition **must hold for every arbitrary shift $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$**:

$$\mathcal{T}\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$$

In discrete-time form, the condition translates to:

$$\mathcal{T}\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Time Invariance is a **mandatory classification property** for characterizing systems in Module 1. It is tested alongside **Linearity, Causality, Stability, and Memory** as part of system classification questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Think of time invariance like a **faithful photocopier machine for sound**:

- Imagine you **record a song** today and play it through a speaker. The speaker produces some output waveform.
- Now imagine you **record the same song tomorrow** (a time-shifted version of the input) and play it through the **same speaker**.
- If the speaker is **time-invariant**, it will reproduce the song **identically** — same waveform shape, same loudness, same quality — just shifted in time by exactly one day.

> **Real-world analogy:** A high-fidelity audio amplifier is essentially time-invariant. Whether you speak into the microphone at 9 AM or 9 PM, the amplifier applies the *same* gain and *same* processing at each instant — it doesn't "know" what time it is.

> **Counter-analogy (Time-Variant):** A musician manually turning a **volume knob** based on the time of day creates a *time-variant system*. The output of the speaker now depends on *when* the input arrives, not just *what* the input is.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Operator** | Mapping from input to output | $\mathcal{T}\{\cdot\}$ |
| **Time Shift** | Delay applied to input | $t_0$ (continuous), $n_0$ (discrete) |
| **Reference Output** | Original system response | $y(t)$ |
| **Shifted Output** | Output with shifted input | $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ |
| **TI Condition** | Both sides of equality must match | $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} \stackrel{?}{=} y(t-t_0)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for students:** "**Shift the input, then observe.**" If the system's behavior at time $t$ depends *only* on the input's behavior at the corresponding past time $(t - t_0)$, the system is time-invariant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Steps for Testing Time Invariance

To rigorously determine if a system $\mathcal{T}$ is time-invariant, the following **five-step procedure** is mandated by KTU valuation patterns:

1. **Step 1 — Establish the Reference:** Compute the original output $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$ for an arbitrary input $x(t)$.
2. **Step 2 — Apply Time Shift to Input:** Replace every occurrence of $t$ in the input with $(t - t_0)$, forming $x(t - t_0)$.
3. **Step 3 — Compute Shifted Output (LHS):** Pass the shifted input through the system: $y_1(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t - t_0)\}$.
4. **Step 4 — Shift the Original Output (RHS):** Replace every $t$ in the original output $y(t)$ with $(t - t_0)$, forming $y_2(t) = y(t - t_0)$.
5. **Step 5 — Compare:** If $y_1(t) = y_2(t)$ for **all** $t$ and **all** $t_0$, the system is **Time-Invariant (TI)**. Otherwise, it is **Time-Variant (TV)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Test:** A system is a *rule* (algorithm, equation, or device). If the rule itself contains an **explicit dependence on the absolute time variable** (e.g., a coefficient like $t$, $n$, or $\cos(t)$), then shifting the input causes the rule to *evaluate differently*, breaking the equality.

---

## 2.2 Common Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant Indicators

| System Equation | Indicator | Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | No explicit $t$ dependence | **Time-Invariant** |
| $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-2)$ | Only shifted input arguments | **Time-Invariant** |
| $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | Multiplier $t$ present | **Time-Variant** |
| $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$ | Time-dependent gain | **Time-Variant** |
| $y(t) = x(2t)$ | Time-scaled argument | **Time-Variant** |
| $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ | Multiplier $n$ present | **Time-Variant** |
| $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | Only shifted input arguments | **Time-Invariant** |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory formulas and conditions** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce without derivation credit loss.

| # | Formula / Condition | Description | Validity |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | Continuous-time TI condition | $\forall t, \forall t_0$ |
| 2 | $\mathcal{T}\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ | Discrete-time TI condition | $\forall n, \forall n_0$ |
| 3 | $y_1(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | LHS of TI test | Computed first |
| 4 | $y_2(t) = y(t-t_0)$ | RHS of TI test | Computed second |
| 5 | $y_1(t) \equiv y_2(t) \Rightarrow \text{TI}$ | TI conclusion | Strict equality required |
| 6 | $y_1(t) \neq y_2(t) \Rightarrow \text{TV}$ | TV conclusion | One counter-example suffices |

> **Engineering Utility:** Time invariance is the **cornerstone assumption** that enables **Convolution**, **Fourier Analysis**, and **z-Transform/Laplace Transform** techniques. Without TI, these powerful tools cannot be applied to analyze real-world systems like filters, communication channels, and control systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation Matrix — Testing Five Classic Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** KTU examiners frequently select systems that "look" linear but are time-variant (or vice-versa). The five examples below cover the most commonly tested cases. **Memorize the methodology**, not just the answers.

---

### Example A — System: $y(t) = x^2(t)$ (Expected: TI ✓)

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = x^2(t)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Input:** Replace $t$ with $(t-t_0)$ in the input:

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHS** by passing $x_1(t)$ through the system:

$$y_1(t) = [x(t - t_0)]^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute RHS** by shifting the original output:

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = [x(t - t_0)]^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$y_1(t) = [x(t - t_0)]^2 = y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Invariant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** Although the system is **non-linear**, it contains no explicit dependence on absolute time $t$ outside the input argument — so time-shifting is preserved.

---

### Example B — System: $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ (Expected: TV ✗)

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHS:**

$$y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

> *Note carefully:* The multiplicative factor remains $t$, **not** $(t-t_0)$, because it is a system parameter, not an input variable.

**Step 4 — Compute RHS:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t - t_0) \neq (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0) = y_2(t)$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Variant**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often write $y_1(t) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$ by incorrectly shifting the multiplicative factor $t$. This is the **most common marking error** in KTU answer sheets for TV systems.

---

### Example C — System: $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$ (Expected: TV ✗)

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHS:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute RHS:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t - t_0)$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t - t_0)\cos(t) \neq x(t - t_0)\cos(t - t_0) = y_2(t)$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Variant**.

> **Physical interpretation:** $\cos(t)$ acts as a time-dependent gain (a *time-varying amplifier*). If you speak into this system at different times, the gain applied is different — hence, time-variant.

---

### Example D — System: $y(t) = x(2t)$ (Time Scaling) (Expected: TV ✗)

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = x(2t)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHS:**

$$y_1(t) = x(2t - t_0)$$

**Step 4 — Compute RHS:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = x(2(t - t_0)) = x(2t - 2t_0)$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$y_1(t) = x(2t - t_0) \neq x(2t - 2t_0) = y_2(t)$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Variant**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Time scaling (e.g., $x(2t)$, $x(t/2)$) ALWAYS produces a time-variant system. Even if the equation "looks pure," the scaling factor combined with a shift creates unequal results. This is a KTU board favorite for trick questions.

---

### Example E — System: $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-2)$ (Expected: TI ✓)

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = x(t) + x(t - 2)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHS:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t - t_0) + x(t - t_0 - 2)$$

**Step 4 — Compute RHS:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0) + x((t - t_0) - 2) = x(t - t_0) + x(t - t_0 - 2)$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t - t_0) + x(t - t_0 - 2) = y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Invariant**.

> **Insight:** Only the input variable $x(\cdot)$ appears in the equation — no standalone $t$, $n$, $\sin$, $\cos$, etc. This is a **structural signature of TI systems**.

---

## 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| System | LHS: $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | RHS: $y(t-t_0)$ | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | $[x(t-t_0)]^2$ | $[x(t-t_0)]^2$ | **TI** |
| $y(t) = t\,x(t)$ | $t\,x(t-t_0)$ | $(t-t_0)\,x(t-t_0)$ | **TV** |
| $y(t) = x(t)\cos(t)$ | $x(t-t_0)\cos(t)$ | $x(t-t_0)\cos(t-t_0)$ | **TV** |
| $y(t) = x(2t)$ | $x(2t-t_0)$ | $x(2t-2t_0)$ | **TV** |
| $y(t) = x(t)+x(t-2)$ | $x(t-t_0)+x(t-t_0-2)$ | $x(t-t_0)+x(t-t_0-2)$ | **TI** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Time Invariance Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: System T and Input x given] --> B[Step 1: Compute y of t = T applied to x of t]
    B --> C[Step 2: Form shifted input x of t minus t0]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute y1 of t = T applied to x of t minus t0 - LHS]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute y2 of t = y of t minus t0 - RHS]
    E --> F{Step 5: Is y1 of t strictly equal to y2 of t for ALL t and ALL t0?}
    F -->|YES| G[CONCLUSION: System is TIME INVARIANT - TI]
    F -->|NO| H[CONCLUSION: System is TIME VARIANT - TV]
    G --> I[END: Document the equality proof]
    H --> J[END: Provide one counter-example shift value]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px
    style J fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TI Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        direction TB
        IN1[Original Input x of t]
        IN2[Shifted Input x of t minus t0]
    end

    subgraph SYSTEM["SYSTEM T"]
        direction TB
        SYS[Mathematical Operator T]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        direction TB
        OUT1[Reference Output y of t]
        OUT2[LHS y1 of t = T applied to x of t minus t0]
        OUT3[RHS y2 of t = y of t minus t0]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["VERIFICATION STAGE"]
        direction TB
        CMP{Equality Check}
        RES_TI[TI Confirmed]
        RES_TV[TV Detected]
    end

    IN1 --> SYS
    SYS --> OUT1
    IN2 --> SYS
    SYS --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> OUT3
    OUT2 --> CMP
    OUT3 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Equal for all t and t0| RES_TI
    CMP -->|Mismatch for some t or t0| RES_TV

    style IN1 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003399
    style IN2 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003399
    style SYS fill:#fff0cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style OUT2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style OUT3 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style CMP fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px
    style RES_TI fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style RES_TV fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Visual Schema — Comparing TI vs TV Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TI_CASE["TIME-INVARIANT CASE: y of t = x squared of t"]
        direction LR
        TI_A[Input shifted x of t minus t0] --> TI_B[System T squares the input] --> TI_C[Output x of t minus t0 squared]
        TI_D[Reference y of t minus t0] -.->|Identical Match| TI_C
    end

    subgraph TV_CASE["TIME-VARIANT CASE: y of t = t times x of t"]
        direction LR
        TV_A[Input shifted x of t minus t0] --> TV_B[System multiplies by t not t minus t0] --> TV_C[Output t times x of t minus t0]
        TV_D[Reference y of t minus t0 equals t minus t0 times x of t minus t0] -.->|Mismatch Detected| TV_C
    end

    style TI_CASE fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style TV_CASE fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style TI_A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#006600
    style TI_B fill:#ffffff,stroke:#006600
    style TI_C fill:#ffffff,stroke:#006600
    style TI_D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#006600
    style TV_A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#990000
    style TV_B fill:#ffffff,stroke:#990000
    style TV_C fill:#ffffff,stroke:#990000
    style TV_D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#990000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define Time Invariance. State the mathematical condition for a continuous-time system to be classified as time-invariant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Invariance:** A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in the input signal results in an identical time shift in the output signal, with no change in the output's shape or characteristics.

**Mathematical Condition:**

If $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$ is the original input-output relationship, then the system is time-invariant if and only if:

$$\mathcal{T}\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0) \quad \forall\, t \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \forall\, t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$$

If this equality holds for all $t$ and all shifts $t_0$, the system exhibits time invariance; otherwise, it is time-variant.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Differentiate between Time-Invariant (TI) and Time-Variant (TV) systems. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Time-Invariant (TI) | Time-Variant (TV) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Time shift in input = identical shift in output | Output changes character with time shift |
| **Mathematical Condition** | $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} \neq y(t-t_0)$ |
| **Example** | $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-2)$ | $y(t) = t\,x(t)$ |

**Example of TI:** $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-2)$ — Shifting input by $t_0$ gives $x(t-t_0) + x(t-t_0-2)$, which equals $y(t-t_0)$. **Time-Invariant.**

**Example of TV:** $y(t) = t\,x(t)$ — Here the multiplicative gain $t$ changes with absolute time, so a shift in input produces a different gain factor. **Time-Variant.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions feature an **internal choice**. Both Question A and Question B carry equal marks. Students answer **ONE complete choice**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Test whether the system described by $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$ is time-invariant. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reference Output:**

$$y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$$

**Step 2 — Shifted Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — LHS Computation:**

$$y_1(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} = x(t-t_0) \cdot \cos(t)$$

**Step 4 — RHS Computation:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = x(t-t_0) \cdot \cos(t-t_0)$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0)\cos(t) \quad \neq \quad x(t-t_0)\cos(t-t_0) = y_2(t)$$

Since $y_1(t) \neq y_2(t)$, the system is **Time-Variant**. **(7 marks — full allocation)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write $\cos(t-t_0)$ in the LHS step by incorrectly assuming the time factor shifts along with the input. The system parameter $\cos(t)$ is **fixed at absolute time $t$** and does not shift when the input is shifted. This error leads to a wrong "TI" conclusion.

---

**Q. (b)** Determine whether the discrete-time system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-3]$ is time-invariant. Justify your answer with a complete proof. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reference Output:**

$$y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-3]$$

**Step 2 — Shifted Input:**

$$x_1[n] = x[n - n_0]$$

**Step 3 — LHS Computation:**

$$y_1[n] = \mathcal{T}\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-3]$$

**Step 4 — RHS Computation:**

$$y_2[n] = y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[(n-n_0)-3]$$

$$y_2[n] = (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-3]$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$y_1[n] = n \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-3]$$
$$y_2[n] = (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-3]$$

$$y_1[n] \neq y_2[n]$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Variant** due to the multiplier $n$ in the first term. **(7 marks — full allocation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Test the system $y(t) = x(t) + 2x(t-1) - x(t-2)$ for time invariance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reference Output:**

$$y(t) = x(t) + 2x(t-1) - x(t-2)$$

**Step 2 — Shifted Input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — LHS Computation:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0) + 2x(t-t_0-1) - x(t-t_0-2)$$

**Step 4 — RHS Computation:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t - t_0) = x(t-t_0) + 2x(t-t_0-1) - x(t-t_0-2)$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0) + 2x(t-t_0-1) - x(t-t_0-2) = y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Invariant** because the equation contains only the input variable and its shifted versions, with **no explicit dependence on absolute time $t$**. **(7 marks — full allocation)**

---

**Q. (b)** Examine the system $y(t) = x(2t+3)$ for the time invariance property. Provide a counter-example shift to justify your conclusion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reference Output:**

$$y(t) = x(2t+3)$$

**Step 2 — Shifted Input:** Let $t_0 = 1$ (choose any non-zero shift):

$$x_1(t) = x(t - 1)$$

**Step 3 — LHS Computation:**

$$y_1(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-1)\} = x(2t+3-1) = x(2t+2)$$

**Step 4 — RHS Computation:**

$$y_2(t) = y(t-1) = x(2(t-1)+3) = x(2t+1)$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$y_1(t) = x(2t+2) \quad \neq \quad x(2t+1) = y_2(t)$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **Time-Variant** because the time scaling factor $2$ inside $x(2t+3)$ interacts with the shift $t_0$ non-commutatively. The counter-example $t_0 = 1$ clearly demonstrates this mismatch. **(7 marks — full allocation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Linearity with Time Invariance (–2 marks):** A system can be linear but time-variant (e.g., $y(t) = t\,x(t)$), or non-linear but time-invariant (e.g., $y(t) = x^2(t)$). Always test each property independently.
>
> 2. **Incorrectly shifting system parameters (–2 to –3 marks):** In $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$, the multiplier $t$ does **NOT** shift to $(t-t_0)$ when computing LHS. It is a system parameter evaluated at the absolute time of observation.
>
> 3. **Incomplete comparison (–1 mark):** Writing only one side of $y_1(t)$ vs $y_2(t)$ without explicit comparison. KTU examiners require the comparison statement for full credit.
>
> 4. **Missing the "for all $t$ and all $t_0$" qualifier (–1 mark):** TI must hold universally, not just for one chosen shift. State this explicitly.
>
> 5. **Time-scaling oversight (–2 marks):** Systems with $x(at)$ or $x(t/b)$ are ALWAYS time-variant. Students frequently mark them TI — this is a board-favorite trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Definition:** TI means a time shift in input produces an identical time shift in output.
- ☐ **Master Equation:** $\mathcal{T}\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ for all $t$, all $t_0$.
- ☐ **Discrete Form:** $\mathcal{T}\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$ for all $n$, all $n_0$.
- ☐ **Five-Step Test:** Reference → Shift Input → LHS → RHS → Compare.
- ☐ **TV Indicators:** Look for explicit $t$, $n$, $\sin(t)$, $\cos(t)$, $\exp(t)$, or time-scaling $x(at)$ inside the system equation.
- ☐ **TI Indicators:** Only the input $x(\cdot)$ and its shifted versions appear, with no standalone time variable.
- ☐ **Common TI Examples:** $y = x^2$, $y = x(t) + x(t-2)$, $y = x[n] - x[n-1]$.
- ☐ **Common TV Examples:** $y = t\,x(t)$, $y = x(t)\cos(t)$, $y = x(2t)$, $y = n\,x[n]$.
- ☐ **Critical Insight:** Linearity and Time Invariance are **independent** properties. One does not imply the other.
- ☐ **Engineering Importance:** TI + Linearity = **LTI system**, enabling Convolution, Fourier, Laplace, and z-Transform analysis.
- ☐ **Counter-Example Method:** To prove a system is TV, **one shift value $t_0 \neq 0$** producing inequality is sufficient.
- ☐ **Universal Test:** To prove TI, the equality must hold for **arbitrary** $t_0$, not just a chosen value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Causality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of continuous-time and discrete-time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems analyzed under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Causality** is formally defined as a system property wherein the output response at any arbitrary time instant $t$ (or sample index $n$) depends **exclusively** on the present and past values of the input signal, and is **completely independent** of any future values of the input.

A system is **causal** if and only if for any two input signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ that are identical for all $t \leq t_0$, the corresponding outputs $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ are also identical for all $t \leq t_0$.

Mathematically, the impulse response of a causal LTI system must satisfy:

$$h(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad t < 0 \quad \text{(Continuous-Time)}$$

$$h[n] = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad n < 0 \quad \text{(Discrete-Time)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** In an examination, the impulse response $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ is the **single most important tool** for determining causality. If the impulse response has any non-zero values for $t < 0$ or $n < 0$, the system is non-causal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **weather forecaster** who can only observe what is currently happening and what has already happened in the atmosphere. You cannot see next week's weather before it arrives. This is exactly how a causal system behaves — it has **no knowledge of the future**.

- A **causal system** is like a person watching a cricket match live on TV. The person reacts to each ball as it is bowled, using only what has happened up to that point.
- A **non-causal system** is like a person who has already watched the entire match highlights package. They "know" future events and can react to things that haven't happened yet from the perspective of any given instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Realizability:** All real-world physical systems are inherently causal because physical laws (Newton's laws, Maxwell's equations, thermodynamics) do not permit a response before a cause. Causality is therefore a **necessary condition for real-time physical implementation** of any system.

### 1.3 Standard Terminology and Metrics

| Property | Causal System | Anti-Causal System | Non-Causal System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Support of $h(t)$** | $t \geq 0$ | $t \leq 0$ | Both positive and negative |
| **Future Dependency** | **No** (only past & present) | Yes (only future) | Yes (both past and future) |
| **Physically Realizable** | **Yes** | No | No (in real-time) |
| **ROC of Laplace Transform** | Right-half plane ($\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_0$) | Left-half plane | Annular region (excludes both extremes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict vs. Non-Strict Causality:** A *strictly causal* system is one where the output at time $t$ depends only on input values strictly in the past (i.e., $x(\tau)$ for $\tau < t$), excluding the present instant. However, in most KTU textbook treatments, "causal" is used inclusively to mean dependence on past **and** present inputs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why and How Causality Works

To rigorously understand causality, we decompose the principle into structured logical steps:

- **Step 1 — The Fundamental Physical Constraint:** In any real physical apparatus (resistor, capacitor, antenna, filter), the cause must precede the effect. Energy cannot be released before it is stored. Hence, real systems obey a time-arrow.

- **Step 2 — Mathematical Encoding via Convolution:** For an LTI system, the output is given by the convolution integral:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$
If $h(\tau) = 0$ for $\tau < 0$, the integration reduces from $0$ to $+\infty$, and $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t-\tau)$ where $\tau \geq 0$, which corresponds to past and present inputs.

- **Step 3 — Discrete-Time Formulation:** The convolution sum in discrete-time is:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k]\, x[n-k]$$
Causality requires $h[k] = 0$ for $k < 0$, so the sum starts at $k = 0$:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty} h[k]\, x[n-k]$$
This shows that $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$ (present and past samples).

- **Step 4 — Region of Convergence (ROC) Link:** For causal LTI systems described by rational Laplace transforms, the ROC is always a **right-half plane** in the $s$-domain, i.e., $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_{\text{max}}$, where $\sigma_{\text{max}}$ is the location of the rightmost pole.
