# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** is a linear amplitude modulation scheme in which the message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied directly by the high-frequency carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, producing a transmitted signal whose spectrum contains **only the two sidebands** (Upper Sideband and Lower Sideband) with the carrier component completely suppressed.

Mathematically, the DSB-SC waveform is expressed as:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DSB-SC belongs to the family of *linear modulation* systems. Unlike DSB-FC (Conventional AM), the carrier is **NOT transmitted**, which makes DSB-SC the most power-efficient form of full-carrier double-sideband transmission, achieving a theoretical transmission efficiency of **100%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Suppression Criterion:** A DSB-SC signal has *zero DC component* in its envelope when $m(t)$ has zero mean, i.e., $\overline{m(t)} = 0$. This is the mathematical fingerprint used in KTU problems to identify DSB-SC waveforms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silent Megaphone"

Imagine you are addressing a large crowd through a **megaphone**. The battery (the carrier) supplies the energy needed to project your voice (the message) across the distance. 

- In **Conventional AM (DSB-FC)**, the megaphone continuously hisses a loud "SHHH" (the carrier), wasting battery power even when you are silent.
- In **DSB-SC**, the "SHHH" is intelligently removed — the megaphone only amplifies your voice itself. The battery (transmitted power) is conserved because no energy is wasted on a constant hissing tone.

However, there is a catch — at the receiver, you need a **second identical megaphone** (a synchronized local oscillator) to recover the voice. This is the fundamental cost of carrier suppression: **coherent (synchronous) demodulation** is mandatory.

---

## 1.3 Classification of DSB-SC

DSB-SC is a member of the broader **Amplitude Modulation family**:

| Modulation Type | Carrier Transmitted? | Sidebands Transmitted? |
|---|---|---|
| DSB-FC (Conventional AM) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Both (USB + LSB) |
| **DSB-SC (Double Sideband SC)** | ❌ **No** | ✅ Both (USB + LSB) |
| SSB (Single Sideband) | ❌ No | ✅ Only ONE (USB or LSB) |
| VSB (Vestigial Sideband) | ❌ No | ✅ One full + vestige of other |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Students often confuse DSB-SC with SSB. The memory hook: **DSB-SC transmits BOTH sidebands but NO carrier; SSB transmits ONE sideband and NO carrier.** Both have 100% efficiency, but DSB-SC occupies **twice the bandwidth** of SSB.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Parameters

- **Carrier Frequency $f_c$:** typically in the **RF range** (530 kHz – 1.6 MHz for AM broadcast, or MHz/GHz for higher-frequency applications).
- **Message Bandwidth $W$:** audio signals typically occupy **300 Hz to 3.4 kHz** (telephony) or **15 kHz** (high-fidelity audio).
- **Modulation Index $\mu$:** *Not defined* for DSB-SC in the conventional sense, since there is no unmodulated carrier to serve as a reference amplitude.
- **Transmission Efficiency $\eta$:** $\eta_{DSB-SC} = 100\%$ (theoretically).

---

## 1.5 Why DSB-SC Matters in Engineering

| Application Domain | Role of DSB-SC |
|---|---|
| **Color Television** | Chrominance subcarrier modulation (I/Q channels) |
| **Quadrature Multiplexing** | Two DSB-SC signals share the same bandwidth via $90°$ phase orthogonality |
| **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** | Baseband I/Q processing inherently uses DSB-SC |
| **Radar Pulse Compression** | Coherent detection of phase-coded pulses |
| **Analog Telemetry** | Biomedical signal transmission where power conservation is critical |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Questions on DSB-SC frequently appear alongside **Quadrature Carrier Multiplexing (QCM)** because the two are mathematically inseparable. Master the DSB-SC derivation, and QCM becomes a one-step extension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Time-Domain Generation Principle

The DSB-SC signal is generated by **direct multiplication** of the message $m(t)$ with the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. This can be achieved by:

1. **Balanced Modulator** (most common — uses two matched non-linear devices).
2. **Ring Modulator** (uses four diodes in a bridge configuration — preferred at high frequencies).
3. **Multiplier IC** (e.g., AD633, MC1496) — the modern integrated approach.
4. **Direct digital multiplication** in DSP/SDR systems.

The mathematical generation is:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = m(t) \times A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

---

## 2.2 Frequency-Domain Representation (Spectral Analysis)

The Fourier Transform of the product $m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is obtained via the **modulation theorem**:

$$\boxed{m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\bigr]}$$

Therefore, the spectrum of the DSB-SC signal is:

$$S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\bigr]$$

**Key spectral features:**
- The baseband spectrum $M(f)$ is **shifted** to $\pm f_c$.
- Each sideband carries **half the original amplitude** ($\frac{A_c}{2}$ factor).
- **No delta function** at $\pm f_c$ (this is what distinguishes it from DSB-FC).
- Spectrum exhibits **conjugate symmetry** for real-valued signals.

---

## 2.3 Phasor Interpretation (KTU Favorite Topic)

The DSB-SC signal can be visualized as a **carrier phasor whose amplitude varies** with the instantaneous value of $m(t)$, with **phase reversals** every time $m(t)$ crosses zero.

- When $m(t) > 0$: phasor lies in-phase with the carrier.
- When $m(t) < 0$: phasor flips by **$180°$** (phase reversal).
- When $m(t) = 0$: phasor collapses to origin.

This **$180°$ phase discontinuity** at every zero-crossing of $m(t)$ is the **visual signature** of DSB-SC on a CRO display (the characteristic "bow-tie" or "pinched" envelope pattern).

---

## 2.4 Power Analysis

The total average transmitted power of a DSB-SC signal is:

$$P_{DSB-SC} = \overline{s^2(t)} = \overline{A_c^2 m^2(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)}$$

Expanding using $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P_{DSB-SC} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Carrier Power Term!** In DSB-FC the total power splits as $P_t = P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB}$. In DSB-SC, the carrier term $P_c$ is **completely absent**, so **all transmitted power resides in the sidebands**, yielding $\eta = 100\%$.

---

## 2.5 Coherent (Synchronous) Demodulation

Since DSB-SC has no carrier to "envelope-detect," the receiver must **regenerate a local carrier** synchronized in **frequency and phase** with the transmitter's carrier. The steps are:

1. **Multiply** the received DSB-SC signal by a locally generated carrier $A_c'\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$.
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** the product to remove the high-frequency $2f_c$ component.
3. **Amplify** to recover $\tfrac{A_c A_c'}{2}\,m(t)\cos(\phi)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase Error Penalty:** Any phase mismatch $\phi$ between local and incoming carrier causes the demodulated output to be scaled by $\cos(\phi)$. If $\phi = 90°$, the output is **zero** (complete signal loss — this is the *quadrature null* principle exploited in QCM). This makes **phase-locked loops (PLLs)** or **Costas loops** essential in practical DSB-SC receivers.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DSB-SC time-domain signal | $s(t) = A_c\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | DSB-SC spectrum | $S(f) = \tfrac{A_c}{2}[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)]$ | V/Hz |
| 3 | Transmission Bandwidth | $BW = 2W$ | Hz (W = message bandwidth) |
| 4 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = \tfrac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$ | Watts |
| 5 | Sideband Power (each) | $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \tfrac{P_t}{2}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = 100\%$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Demodulated Output | $y(t) = \tfrac{A_c^2}{2}\,m(t)\cos(\phi)$ | V (after LPF) |
| 8 | Phase Error Sensitivity | Output $\propto \cos(\phi)$ | $\phi$ in radians |
| 9 | Envelope | $\vert s(t)\vert = A_c\,\vert m(t)\vert$ | V (always non-negative) |
| 10 | Modulation Index | Not applicable (no unmodulated carrier) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** For DSB-SC, **Bandwidth = 2W** and **Efficiency = 100%** are the two numbers you must write first in any KTU 14-mark question. Examiners specifically check these.

---

## 2.7 Comparison: DSB-SC vs DSB-FC (Conventional AM)

| Feature | DSB-FC (Conventional AM) | DSB-SC |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier transmitted? | Yes | No |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ |
| Total Power | $P_c\left(1 + \tfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | $\tfrac{A_c^2}{2}\overline{m^2(t)}$ |
| Efficiency | $\tfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ (max 33.3%) | **100%** |
| Demodulation | Envelope detector (simple) | Coherent detector (complex) |
| Synchronization needed? | No | **Yes** (frequency + phase) |
| Noise performance | Same as DSB-SC | Same as DSB-FC |
| Applications | AM broadcast, CB radio | TV chrominance, QCM, telemetry |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "DSB-SC and DSB-FC have the same bandwidth and the same noise performance — then why prefer DSB-SC?" **Answer:** Because DSB-SC saves the wasted carrier power (the carrier carries no information), enabling **higher efficiency** and **lower battery consumption** in portable transmitters.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: DSB-SC Spectrum from Time-Domain Product

### Step 1: State the Time-Domain Expression

The DSB-SC signal is defined as the product of the message $m(t)$ and the carrier:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 2: Express the Carrier Using Euler's Formula

The cosine carrier is decomposed into its complex exponentials:

$$\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{e^{j2\pi f_c t} + e^{-j2\pi f_c t}}{2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Rearrange

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \left[\frac{e^{j2\pi f_c t} + e^{-j2\pi f_c t}}{2}\right]$$

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{j2\pi f_c t} + \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{-j2\pi f_c t}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Modulation (Frequency-Shift) Theorem

The **frequency shift property** of the Fourier Transform states:

$$m(t)\,e^{j2\pi f_c t} \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}}\; M(f - f_c)$$

$$m(t)\,e^{-j2\pi f_c t} \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}}\; M(f + f_c)$$

### Step 5: Take the Fourier Transform of Both Sides

Applying $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ to the expression in Step 3:

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,M(f - f_c) + \frac{A_c}{2}\,M(f + f_c)$$

$$\boxed{S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\bigr]}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The baseband spectrum $M(f)$ centered at $f=0$ is replicated at $+f_c$ (Upper Sideband) and at $-f_c$ (Lower Sideband), each scaled to half amplitude. **No impulse appears at $\pm f_c$** — confirming carrier suppression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Average Transmitted Power

### Step 1: Definition of Average Power

For a real, periodic (or stationary) signal, average power over one period $T_0$ is:

$$P_t = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s^2(t)\,dt = \overline{s^2(t)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the DSB-SC Expression

$$P_t = \overline{\left[A_c\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\right]^2}$$

$$P_t = A_c^2\,\overline{m^2(t)\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Trigonometric Identity

Using $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P_t = A_c^2\,\overline{m^2(t) \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2}}$$

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)} + \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t)}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Cross Term

Since $m(t)$ is a low-frequency message (bandlimited to $W \ll f_c$) and $\cos(4\pi f_c t)$ is a high-frequency oscillation at $2f_c$, the product $m^2(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t)$ has **zero mean** over a long observation window:

$$\overline{m^2(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t)} = 0$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{P_{DSB-SC} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,P_m}$$

where $P_m = \overline{m^2(t)}$ is the normalized message power.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Coherent Demodulation Output

### Step 1: Multiply by Local Carrier

Let the received signal be $r(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)$ (neglecting noise initially). The local oscillator generates $\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ with a phase error $\phi$:

$$v(t) = r(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

$$v(t) = A_c\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Product-to-Sum Identity

Using $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$v(t) = \frac{A_c\,m(t)}{2}\bigl[\cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)\bigr]$$

### Step 3: Apply the Low-Pass Filter

The LPF rejects the high-frequency term $\cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)$ (centered at $2f_c$) and passes only the baseband term:

$$y(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\cos(\phi)$$

### Step 4: Special Cases Analysis

| Phase Error $\phi$ | Output $y(t)$ | Observation |
|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | $\tfrac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)$ | Ideal — full recovery |
| $30°$ | $0.433\,m(t)$ | Attenuated by 13 dB |
| $60°$ | $0.25\,m(t)$ | Attenuated by 6 dB |
| $90°$ | $0$ | **Quadrature null** — total signal loss |
| $180°$ | $-\tfrac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)$ | Sign inversion (still recoverable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** This derivation is the **most frequently asked** question in Module 3. The examiner expects every algebraic step, the explicit use of the trigonometric identity, and a clear statement of the LPF cutoff requirement: **$f_{cutoff} \geq W$** and **$f_{cutoff} \ll 2f_c$**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 2\cos(100\pi t)$ modulates a carrier $c(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$ in a DSB-SC modulator. Find:
- (i) The DSB-SC time-domain expression
- (ii) The transmission bandwidth
- (iii) The total transmitted power
- (iv) The frequency-domain sketch (key frequencies only)

**Solution:**

**(i) Time-domain expression:**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = 2\cos(100\pi t) \cdot 10\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$$

Using the product-to-sum identity $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$s(t) = 10\bigl[\cos(2\pi(10000 - 50)t) + \cos(2\pi(10000 + 50)t)\bigr]$$

$$\boxed{s(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \times 9950\,t) + 10\cos(2\pi \times 10050\,t)\;\text{V}}$$

**(ii) Bandwidth:**

The message frequency is $f_m = 50$ Hz. Hence $W = 50$ Hz, and:

$$BW = 2W = 2 \times 50 = 100\;\text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Total Power:**

The message has $A_m = 2$ V, so $P_m = \overline{m^2(t)} = \frac{A_m^2}{2} = 2\;\text{W}$ (assuming 1 Ω load).

$$P_{DSB-SC} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,P_m = \frac{10^2}{2}\times 2 = 100\;\text{W}$$

**(iv) Frequency sketch:**

The spectrum contains impulses at $f = \pm 9950$ Hz and $f = \pm 10050$ Hz, each of weight $\frac{A_c A_m}{4} = 5$ V/Hz, with **no impulse at $\pm 10$ kHz** (carrier suppressed).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** For single-tone modulation $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the DSB-SC output reduces to **two pure tones** at $f_c \pm f_m$. Memorize the formula:
> $$A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t) \cdot A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \tfrac{A_m A_c}{2}\bigl[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\bigr]$$

---

## 3.5 Generation Using Balanced Modulator (Analytical Walkthrough)

A balanced modulator uses **two square-law modulators** with outputs subtracted. Each square-law device produces:

$$v_i(t) = a\,v_{in}(t) + b\,v_{in}^2(t)$$

When the inputs are $v_1 = m(t) + c(t)$ and $v_2 = m(t) - c(t)$:

$$v_1 - v_2 = 2c(t) + 2b\,m(t)\,[2m(t)c(t)] \quad \text{(after algebra)}$$

After filtering the unwanted $m^2(t)$ term with a bandpass filter centered at $f_c$, only the desired $4b\,m(t)c(t)$ remains — **the carrier $\pm 2c(t)$ cancels out**, producing DSB-SC.

$$\boxed{v_{out}(t) = 4b\,m(t)\,A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DSB-SC Transmitter (Balanced Modulator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Message Signal m(t)"] --> C["Balanced Modulator"]
    B["Carrier Oscillator A_c cos 2 pi f_c t"] --> C
    C --> D["Bandpass Filter Centered at f_c"]
    D --> E["DSB-SC Output s(t)"]
    F["Local Oscillator with PLL Lock"] -.-> B
    G["Antenna Matching Network"] --> E
    E --> H["Channel Propagation Medium"]
```

**Description of Flow:**

- `A` (Message) and `B` (Carrier) are the two inputs to the `C` (Balanced Modulator).
- The output of `C` contains $m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ plus a small residual carrier and DC offset.
- `D` (Bandpass Filter) cleans the spectrum, removing out-of-band harmonics and any DC component.
- `E` represents the final `s(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$` signal.
- `F` is a **PLL-locked** local oscillator (often derived from a pilot tone or pilot carrier in practice) ensuring frequency stability.
- `G` is the antenna matching and `H` is the channel.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Coherent (Synchronous) DSB-SC Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Received r(t) plus n(t)"] --> C["Multiplier Stage"]
    B["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi f_c t plus phase error phi"] --> C
    C --> D["Low Pass Filter Cutoff W"]
    D --> E["DC Blocker Capacitor"]
    E --> F["Automatic Gain Control AGC"]
    F --> G["Demodulated Output m(t) hat"]
    H["Phase Locked Loop for Carrier Recovery"] --> B
    A -.Pilot tone.-> H
```

**Description of Flow:**

- `A` is the noisy received DSB-SC signal.
- `B` is a local oscillator whose frequency is locked to $f_c$ via a `H` (PLL).
- The `C` (Multiplier) produces sum and difference frequencies.
- `D` (LPF) removes the $2f_c$ term, leaving the baseband.
- `E` removes any DC offset, `F` (AGC) normalizes amplitude, and `G` is the recovered message $\hat{m}(t)$.
- The dashed line from `A` to `H` indicates that a **low-level pilot carrier** may be transmitted alongside the DSB-SC signal to assist the receiver's PLL (a common technique in practice to avoid the need for a separate pilot channel).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Ring Modulator (Alternative DSB-SC Generator)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Message Input m(t) via Center Tapped Transformer T1"] --> B["Diode Bridge D1 D2 D3 D4"]
    C["Carrier Input A_c cos 2 pi f_c t via Center Tapped Transformer T2"] --> B
    B --> D["Output Transformer T3 Summing Node"]
    D --> E["Bandpass Filter f_c plus or minus W"]
    E --> F["DSB-SC Output s(t)"]
    G["Bias Control for Diode Switching"] -.-> B
```

**Description of Flow:**

- The `A` (message) enters via the center-tapped primary of `T1`.
- The `C` (carrier) enters via the center-tapped primary of `T2` and **switches the diode bridge polarity** at the carrier rate.
- When the carrier is positive, `D1` and `D2` conduct, routing $m(t)$ to the output with one polarity; when negative, `D3` and `D4` conduct, routing with the opposite polarity.
- The net effect is **multiplication of $m(t)$ by $\text{sgn}[\cos(2\pi f_c t)]$** — a square-wave carrier.
- `E` (BPF) reconstructs the sinusoidal carrier multiplication by removing the odd harmonics of the square wave (which fall at $3f_c, 5f_c, \ldots$).
- The output `F` is the DSB-SC signal.

---

## 4.4 Phasor Diagram — DSB-SC Envelope Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["m(t) positive region"] --> B["In-phase phasor length A_c m(t)"]
    C["m(t) zero crossing"] --> D["Phasor collapses to origin"]
    E["m(t) negative region"] --> F["180 degree phase reversal phasor length A_c m(t)"]
    B --> G["Envelope traces m(t) magnitude"]
    F --> G
    D --> G
```

**Description of Flow:**

- Region `A`: When `m(t)` is positive, the phasor `B` aligns with the carrier reference.
- Region `C`: At every zero crossing of `m(t)`, the phasor `D` momentarily shrinks to zero.
- Region `E`: When `m(t)` becomes negative, the phasor `F` flips by $180°$.
- `G` shows that the **envelope** (the curve tracing the phasor tips) is $|m(t)|$, which has sharp notches at the zero crossings — this is the **bow-tie** or **pinched envelope** CRO pattern unique to DSB-SC.

---

## 4.5 Spectral Diagram — DSB-SC Spectrum (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Baseband M(f) centered at 0 Hz bandwidth W"] --> B["Shifted copy at plus f_c Upper Sideband"]
    A --> C["Shifted copy at minus f_c Lower Sideband"]
    B --> D["No impulse at plus f_c carrier suppressed"]
    C --> E["No impulse at minus f_c carrier suppressed"]
    D --> F["Total occupied bandwidth equals 2W"]
    E --> F
```

**Description of Flow:**

- `A` is the original baseband message spectrum.
- `B` and `C` are the two shifted replicas, with **no delta function at $\pm f_c$**.
- `D` and `E` emphasize the **absence of carrier impulses** (this is the visual signature distinguishing DSB-SC from DSB-FC).
- `F` confirms the total occupied bandwidth is exactly $2W$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) modulation. State any two advantages over conventional AM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
DSB-SC is an amplitude modulation technique in which the message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, producing a transmitted signal $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ whose spectrum contains only the two sidebands (USB and LSB) with the carrier component completely suppressed.

**Advantages over Conventional AM (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Higher power efficiency** — 100% of transmitted power resides in the information-bearing sidebands (vs. max 33.3% in DSB-FC).
2. **No carrier power wastage** — all battery/transmitter power is utilized for information transfer.
3. **Reduced interference to adjacent channels** in some deployment scenarios due to lack of strong carrier component.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Why can't a simple envelope detector be used to demodulate a DSB-SC signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An envelope detector works by rectifying the incoming signal and low-pass filtering it, exploiting the fact that the envelope of a DSB-FC signal faithfully follows $|1 + m(t)|$ when $\mu \leq 1$.

In DSB-SC, the signal $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ has an envelope of $|A_c m(t)|$, which exhibits **$180°$ phase reversals** at every zero-crossing of $m(t)$ — the envelope **collapses to zero** at these points, producing a **"pinched" or "bow-tie" pattern** rather than a smooth waveform.

A simple envelope detector would recover $|m(t)|$ (a full-wave rectified version), losing the **sign information** of $m(t)$ and introducing severe distortion near zero crossings. Therefore, **coherent (synchronous) demodulation** — requiring a phase-locked local oscillator — is mandatory for DSB-SC.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer exactly **one full question** per module section (with internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the DSB-SC signal in both time and frequency domain. Sketch the spectrum for a single-tone modulating signal and show that the carrier is absent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 4\cos(2000\pi t)$ modulates a carrier $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^5 t)$ in a DSB-SC system. Calculate (i) the transmission bandwidth, (ii) the total transmitted power across a $50\,\Omega$ load, and (iii) the frequencies present in the modulated output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Time-domain expression (2 marks):**
The DSB-SC signal is generated by direct product multiplication:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Frequency-domain expression (3 marks):**
Using the modulation theorem $m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \tfrac{1}{2}[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)]$:

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)\bigr]$$

**Single-tone spectrum sketch (2 marks):**
For $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the spectrum $M(f) = \frac{A_m}{2}[\delta(f-f_m) + \delta(f+f_m)]$.

Substituting:
$$S(f) = \frac{A_c A_m}{4}\bigl[\delta(f-f_c-f_m) + \delta(f-f_c+f_m) + \delta(f+f_c-f_m) + \delta(f+f_c+f_m)\bigr]$$

The spectrum shows four impulses at $f = \pm(f_c \pm f_m)$ with **no impulse at $f = \pm f_c$** — confirming the carrier is completely suppressed. The four impulses form two pairs symmetric about $\pm f_c$, representing the **Upper Sideband (USB)** at $f_c + f_m$ and **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at $f_c - f_m$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $A_m = 4$ V, $f_m = 1000$ Hz, $A_c = 20$ V, $f_c = 10^5$ Hz, $R_L = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$W = f_m = 1000\;\text{Hz}$$

$$BW = 2W = 2 \times 1000 = 2000\;\text{Hz} = 2\;\text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Total Transmitted Power (3 marks):**

Message power: $P_m = \frac{A_m^2}{2} = \frac{4^2}{2} = 8$ W (normalized to 1 Ω).

$$P_{DSB-SC} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\,P_m = \frac{20^2}{2}\times 8 = 100 \times 8 = 1600\;\text{W (into 1 Ω)}$$

Across a 50 Ω load:

$$P_{50\Omega} = \frac{P_{1\Omega}}{R_L} = \frac{1600}{50} = 32\;\text{W}$$

(Alternatively, $P = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} = \frac{(A_c A_m/2)^2}{2 R} = \frac{(40)^2/2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{800}{100} = 8$ W per sideband; total = 16 W. ⚠️ **Note:** This depends on whether $A_c$ and $A_m$ are peak or peak-to-peak — students must show their assumed convention.)

**(iii) Frequencies in the output (2 marks):**

$$f_c \pm f_m = 10^5 \pm 1000\;\text{Hz} = 99{,}000\;\text{Hz and }101{,}000\;\text{Hz}$$

The output contains **two frequencies**: **99 kHz (LSB)** and **101 kHz (USB)**. The carrier frequency 100 kHz is **absent**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation of DSB-SC using a **balanced modulator**. Show mathematically that the carrier component cancels at the output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **coherent detection** of a DSB-SC signal with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the demodulated output and discuss the effect of a **phase error $\phi$** in the local oscillator. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Balanced Modulator (7 marks)

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
A balanced modulator consists of two identical square-law (non-linear) devices whose outputs are subtracted.

```
m(t) + c(t) → [Square-Law Device 1] → v1(t) ─┐
                                               ├──[Subtractor]──→ BPF → s(t)
m(t) - c(t) → [Square-Law Device 2] → v2(t) ─┘
```

The inputs are $v_1(t) = m(t) + c(t)$ and $v_2(t) = m(t) - c(t)$, where $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

**Mathematical derivation (3 marks):**
Each square-law device has the characteristic:

$$v_i(t) = a\,v_{in}(t) + b\,v_{in}^2(t)$$

Therefore:

$$v_1(t) = a[m(t) + c(t)] + b[m(t) + c(t)]^2$$
$$v_2(t) = a[m(t) - c(t)] + b[m(t) - c(t)]^2$$

Subtracting:

$$v_1 - v_2 = 2a\,c(t) + 2b\left[2m(t)c(t)\right]$$

$$v_1 - v_2 = 2a\,c(t) + 4b\,m(t)c(t)$$

The first term is the **carrier** (linear in $c(t)$), and the second term is the **desired DSB-SC product**.

**After bandpass filtering (1 mark):**
The BPF centered at $f_c$ rejects any DC component and any low-frequency $m^2(t)$ term, leaving:

$$s(t) = 4b\,A_c\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\;\text{(DSB-SC output)}$$

The linear carrier term $2a\,c(t)$ can be eliminated by careful balance of the two square-law devices — this is the **"balanced"** in the name. In practice, perfect balance is achieved by trimming a potentiometer until the output shows carrier null on a spectrum analyzer.

---

#### Part (b) — Coherent Detection (7 marks)

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
r(t) → [Multiplier] → [LPF cutoff W] → [DC Blocker] → m̂(t)
         ↑
         │
   [Local Oscillator cos(2πf_c t + φ)]
         ↑
   [PLL / Costas Loop for carrier recovery]
```

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $r(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Multiplying by the local carrier:

$$v(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

Using $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$v(t) = \frac{A_c m(t)}{2}\bigl[\cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)\bigr]$$

After the LPF (which removes the $2f_c$ component):

$$\boxed{\hat{m}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\cos(\phi)}$$

**Effect of phase error $\phi$ (2 marks):**
- For $\phi = 0$: $\hat{m}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}m(t)$ — **perfect recovery**.
- For small $\phi$: the output is **attenuated by $\cos(\phi)$** — e.g., $\phi = 30°$ causes 6 dB loss.
- For $\phi = 90°$: $\hat{m}(t) = 0$ — **complete signal loss** (quadrature null).
- For $\phi = 180°$: $\hat{m}(t) = -\frac{A_c}{2}m(t)$ — **sign inversion** (recoverable via a comparator).

This extreme phase sensitivity is why practical DSB-SC receivers employ a **Costas loop** or **PLL with pilot-tone-assisted carrier recovery**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DSB-SC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{A_c}{2}$ in the spectrum.** The Fourier Transform of $m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is $\frac{1}{2}[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)]$, NOT $M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)$. Marks lost: 1–2 per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Confusing DSB-SC with SSB.** A student who writes "$BW = W$" for DSB-SC loses full marks. Always state **$BW = 2W$** for DSB-SC.
>
> 3. **Saying "modulation index $\mu$" for DSB-SC.** The modulation index is defined only for DSB-FC (Conventional AM). For DSB-SC, write "**the carrier is suppressed**" or "**no unmodulated carrier exists**."
>
> 4. **Using an envelope detector in the demodulation block diagram.** This is a *fatal* conceptual error for DSB-SC. The examiner will deduct 3–4 marks immediately.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the LPF cutoff range.** A correct coherent demodulator must have **$W \leq f_{cutoff} \ll 2f_c$**. Simply writing "LPF" without justifying the cutoff loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Power calculation error: forgetting to divide by load resistance.** If the question specifies a load $R_L$, the final power answer must be **$P = V_{rms}^2 / R_L$**, not just $V_{rms}^2$. Many students lose the final mark here.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DSB-SC in 60 Seconds:**

- ✅ **Definition:** $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — product of message and carrier, no DC bias added.
- ✅ **Spectrum:** $S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)]$ — baseband shifted to $\pm f_c$, no impulses at $\pm f_c$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2W$ (same as DSB-FC and DSB-SC).
- ✅ **Power:** $P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\overline{m^2(t)}$ — entire power in sidebands, no carrier term.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = 100\%$ (theoretical maximum).
- ✅ **Generation:** Balanced modulator, ring modulator, or analog multiplier IC (AD633, MC1496).
- ✅ **Demodulation:** **Coherent / synchronous detection** is mandatory — envelope detector will fail.
- ✅ **Phase sensitivity:** Output $\propto \cos(\phi)$ — $\phi = 90°$ causes total signal loss (quadrature null).
- ✅ **Envelope:** $|s(t)| = A_c |m(t)|$ — exhibits sharp notches at zero crossings of $m(t)$ (the "bow-tie" CRO pattern).
- ✅ **Single-tone result:** $A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t) \cdot A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{A_m A_c}{2}[\cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$.
- ✅ **Comparison with DSB-FC:** Same bandwidth, same noise performance, **better efficiency** for DSB-SC; **simpler receiver** for DSB-FC.
- ✅ **Applications:** TV chrominance (I/Q modulation), QCM, coherent radar, software-defined radio, analog telemetry.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Power conservation — no wasted carrier transmission.
- ✅ **Key disadvantage:** Complex receiver requiring PLL/Costas loop for carrier synchronization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SSB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** SSB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SSB (Single Sideband Modulation)?

**Single Sideband (SSB) modulation** is an amplitude modulation technique in which only **one** of the two sidebands (either the Upper Sideband or the Lower Sideband) generated during the AM process is transmitted, while the other sideband and (usually) the carrier are suppressed. It is the most bandwidth- and power-efficient form of analog AM communication.

The mathematical representation of an SSB signal transmitting only the **Upper Sideband (USB)** is:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A_c \cdot \hat{m}(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The **Lower Sideband (LSB)** SSB signal is:

$$
s_{LSB}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \cdot \hat{m}(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $\hat{m}(t)$ is the **Hilbert transform** of the message signal $m(t)$ and $f_c$ is the carrier frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SSB modulation is treated as a power- and bandwidth-efficient refinement of DSB-SC. The Board regularly tests its **time-domain representation, bandwidth, power, and generation using the Phase-Shift (Hartley) method.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sending a heavy parcel through a courier service. Standard AM is like sending **the box, the packaging, and a duplicate box** — only one is needed. DSB-SC removes the carrier (the box) but still sends both sidebands (two copies of the content). 

**SSB** is the ultimate optimization: the courier removes *both* the carrier and the duplicate sideband, sending **only one pristine copy** of the information. The receiver must intelligently reconstruct the missing half using a locally generated reference (a process called **coherent/synchronous detection**) — much like piecing together a torn document when you already know the language and grammar.

| Feature | AM (DSB-FC) | DSB-SC | **SSB** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Transmitted | Yes | No | No |
| Sidebands Transmitted | Both | Both | **Only One** |
| Bandwidth | $2W_m$ | $2W_m$ | **$W_m$** |
| Power Efficiency | Low (~33%) | Medium | **Highest** |

> [!NOTE]
> The suppression of the carrier and one sideband makes SSB the standard choice for **HF radio communications, amateur radio, maritime, and military long-distance communication**, where bandwidth conservation and power efficiency are mission-critical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spectral Structure of SSB

When a message $m(t)$ with spectrum $M(f)$ (band-limited to $\vert f \vert \le W_m$) modulates a carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the DSB-SC spectrum is:

$$
S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \right]
$$

For **USB transmission**, we retain only the portion above $+f_c$ and below $-f_c$:

$$
S_{USB}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) U(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) U(-f - f_c) \right]
$$

For **LSB transmission**, we retain the portion below $+f_c$ and above $-f_c$:

$$
S_{LSB}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) U(f_c - f) + M(f + f_c) U(f + f_c) \right]
$$

where $U(f)$ is the unit step function.

## 2.2 Time Domain Representation (using Hilbert Transform)

The Hilbert transform $\hat{m}(t)$ creates a **90° phase-shifted version** of $m(t)$ across all frequencies. The SSB signal can be compactly written as:

$$
s_{SSB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \mp \frac{A_c}{2} \hat{m}(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

> The **minus sign** corresponds to **USB**, and the **plus sign** corresponds to **LSB**.

## 2.3 Why the Hilbert Transform?

The Hilbert transform is essentially a **broadband quadrature phase shifter**. In DSB-SC, the upper and lower sidebands carry **mirror-image** information. By adding the message to a 90°-shifted version multiplied by the quadrature carrier, **one sideband constructively adds and the other destructively cancels**, leaving only the desired sideband.

## 2.4 Generation Methods

### (a) Filter Method
- Generate DSB-SC using a balanced modulator
- Pass through a highly selective **bandpass filter** to remove the unwanted sideband
- Drawback: Requires extremely sharp filters, especially for low-frequency voice messages (e.g., 300 Hz–3 kHz)

### (b) Phase-Shift Method (Hartley Modulator)
- Uses two balanced modulators, a 90° phase-shift network for the message, and a 90° phase-shift network for the carrier
- One output path gives USB, the other gives LSB
- Drawback: Requires a precise wideband 90° phase shifter for the message

### (c) Weaver's Method (Third Method)
- Uses two stages of balanced modulation and low-pass filtering
- Avoids the need for wideband 90° phase shifters
- Suited for very low-frequency or DC-containing messages

## 2.5 Detection of SSB Signals

SSB **cannot** be detected using an envelope detector. It requires **coherent (synchronous) detection**:

- The receiver multiplies the incoming SSB signal by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ that is **frequency-synchronized and phase-locked** to the transmitter
- Low-pass filtering recovers $m(t)$

$$
s_{SSB}(t) \cdot 2\cos(2\pi f_c t) \xrightarrow{LPF} m(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase error sensitivity:** A phase error $\phi$ in the local carrier produces a demodulated output $m(t)\cos\phi + \hat{m}(t)\sin\phi$, distorting the signal. This is the **price of carrier suppression**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | $BW_{SSB} = W_m$ | Half of DSB bandwidth (in Hz) |
| **SSB-SC Power** | $P_{SSB-SC} = \dfrac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{4R}$ | Power in the single sideband (Watts) |
| **USB Time-Domain** | $s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t]$ | Upper Sideband representation |
| **LSB Time-Domain** | $s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[m(t)\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t]$ | Lower Sideband representation |
| **Hilbert Transform** | $\hat{M}(f) = -j \text{sgn}(f) \cdot M(f)$ | Frequency-domain definition |
| **Demodulated Output** | $y(t) = m(t)\cos\phi + \hat{m}(t)\sin\phi$ | With phase error $\phi$ in local oscillator |
| **Power Savings vs DSB** | $50\%$ | SSB transmits half the DSB power |
| **Bandwidth Savings vs AM** | $50\%$ | SSB occupies half the AM bandwidth |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HF Radio Communications (3–30 MHz):** SSB is the dominant mode for long-distance voice communication (maritime, aviation, military).
- **Amateur (Ham) Radio:** SSB (USB above 10 MHz, LSB below 10 MHz) is standard.
- **Point-to-Point Voice Links:** Used in telephone carrier systems and land-mobile radio.
- **Spectrum Efficiency:** Allows **twice as many channels** in a given frequency band compared to AM/DSB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SSB Time-Domain Expression from DSB-SC

**Starting Point:** DSB-SC signal:

$$
s_{DSB}(t) = m(t) \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

**Step 1:** Express $m(t)$ using the inverse Fourier integral.

$$
m(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} M(f) e^{j2\pi f t} \, df
$$

**Step 2:** Decompose $m(t)$ into positive and negative frequency components.

$$
m(t) = \int_{0}^{W_m} M(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df + \int_{-W_m}^{0} M(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df
$$

**Step 3:** Define the **positive-frequency component** $m_+(t)$ and **negative-frequency component** $m_-(t)$:

$$
m_+(t) = \int_{0}^{W_m} M(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df
$$

$$
m_-(t) = \int_{-W_m}^{0} M(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df
$$

**Step 4:** Note that for a **real** message signal, $M(-f) = M^*(f)$, so $m_-(t) = m_+^*(t)$. Also, $m(t) = m_+(t) + m_-(t)$.

**Step 5:** Now form the DSB-SC signal and separate its positive and negative frequencies around $\pm f_c$.

$$
s_{DSB}(t) = m_+(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_+(t) e^{-j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{-j\omega_c t}
$$

Regroup the terms as USB (positive frequencies near $+f_c$ and negative frequencies near $-f_c$):

$$
s_{USB}(t) = m_+(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{-j\omega_c t}
$$

**Step 6:** Use the identities: $2\cos(\omega_c t) = e^{j\omega_c t} + e^{-j\omega_c t}$ and $2j\sin(\omega_c t) = e^{j\omega_c t} - e^{-j\omega_c t}$.

Substitute and rearrange:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = \frac{1}{2}[m_+(t) + m_-(t)](e^{j\omega_c t} + e^{-j\omega_c t}) - \frac{1}{2j}[m_+(t) - m_-(t)](e^{j\omega_c t} - e^{-j\omega_c t})
$$

**Step 7:** Recognize the components:

- $m_+(t) + m_-(t) = m(t)$
- $m_+(t) - m_-(t) = j\hat{m}(t)$, where $\hat{m}(t)$ is the Hilbert transform of $m(t)$

**Step 8:** Substitute back and simplify.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{USB}(t) &= \frac{1}{2} m(t) \cdot 2\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{2j} \cdot j\hat{m}(t) \cdot 2j\sin(\omega_c t) \\
&= m(t) \cos(\omega_c t) - j^2 \hat{m}(t) \sin(\omega_c t) \\
&= m(t) \cos(\omega_c t) + \hat{m}(t) \sin(\omega_c t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — careful with signs. The correct derivation yields:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = m(t) \cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t) \sin(\omega_c t)
$$

$$
s_{LSB}(t) = m(t) \cos(\omega_c t) + \hat{m}(t) \sin(\omega_c t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:** Most KTU textbooks adopt:
> - **USB:** $s_{USB}(t) = m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$
> - **LSB:** $s_{LSB}(t) = m(t)\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$
>
> Always verify with the reference answer key provided by your module instructor.

## 3.2 Derivation of SSB Power

For an SSB-SC signal transmitting only the USB:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t)\right]
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the mean-square value (proportional to power) over a resistor $R$:

$$
P_{SSB} = \frac{1}{R} \overline{s_{USB}^2(t)}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{USB}^2(t) = \frac{A_c^2}{4}\Big[ & m^2(t)\cos^2(\omega_c t) + \hat{m}^2(t)\sin^2(\omega_c t) \\
& - 2 m(t)\hat{m}(t)\cos(\omega_c t)\sin(\omega_c t) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply time-averaging. Since $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$ vary slowly compared to the carrier:

- $\overline{\cos^2(\omega_c t)} = \frac{1}{2}$
- $\overline{\sin^2(\omega_c t)} = \frac{1}{2}$
- $\overline{\cos(\omega_c t)\sin(\omega_c t)} = 0$

**Step 4:** Use the energy-preserving property of the Hilbert transform: $\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} = \overline{m^2(t)}$.

**Step 5:** Combine all results.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{SSB} &= \frac{A_c^2}{4R} \left[ \frac{1}{2}\overline{m^2(t)} + \frac{1}{2}\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} \right] \\
&= \frac{A_c^2}{4R} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\overline{m^2(t)} \\
&= \frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{4R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The total SSB power equals the power in **one sideband only**, which is exactly **half** the total DSB-SC power (which is $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{2R}$).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | AM (DSB-FC) | DSB-SC | **SSB-SC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulation index | $\mu = A_m / A_c$ | N/A | N/A |
| Carrier power | $\frac{A_c^2}{2R}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Sideband power (each) | $\frac{A_m^2}{4R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R}$ | $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2}}{4R}$ | $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2}}{4R}$ |
| **Total power** | $\frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right]$ | $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2}}{2R}$ | $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2}}{4R}$ |
| **Bandwidth** | $2W_m$ | $2W_m$ | $W_m$ |
| **Detection** | Envelope | Coherent | Coherent |
| **Channel capacity (theoretical)** | $W_m$ channels | $W_m$ channels | $2W_m$ channels |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spectral Representation of SSB

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the frequency-domain structure of an SSB signal compared to AM and DSB-SC.

```mermaid
graph LR
    MSG["Message Spectrum M(f) Band: -Wm to +Wm"]
    AM_SPEC["AM Spectrum: Carrier at fc + Two Sidebands Width: 2Wm"]
    DSB_SPEC["DSB-SC Spectrum: Two Sidebands No Carrier Width: 2Wm"]
    USB_SPEC["SSB USB Spectrum: Only Upper Sideband Band: fc to fc plus Wm"]
    LSB_SPEC["SSB LSB Spectrum: Only Lower Sideband Band: fc minus Wm to fc"]
    MSG --> AM_SPEC
    MSG --> DSB_SPEC
    MSG --> USB_SPEC
    MSG --> LSB_SPEC
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hartley (Phase-Shift) SSB Modulator

```mermaid
graph LR
    MT["Message m(t)"]
    MS["Message 90 deg Phase Shifter"]
    CAR["Carrier cos wct"]
    CS["Carrier 90 deg Phase Shifter sin wct"]
    BM1["Balanced Modulator 1"]
    BM2["Balanced Modulator 2"]
    SUM["Summing Junction"]
    OUT["SSB Output sSSB t"]
    MT --> BM1
    MS --> BM2
    CAR --> BM1
    CS --> BM2
    BM1 --> SUM
    BM2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT
```

**Operation Logic:**
- **Balanced Modulator 1** multiplies $m(t)$ with $\cos(\omega_c t)$ → produces DSB-SC containing both sidebands
- **Balanced Modulator 2** multiplies $\hat{m}(t)$ with $\sin(\omega_c t)$ → produces another DSB-SC
- The summer adds them with appropriate signs: one sideband **adds constructively**, the other **cancels destructively**
- Output is a single sideband (USB if $-$ sign at summer; LSB if $+$ sign)

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Filter Method of SSB Generation

```mermaid
graph LR
    MSG2["Message m(t)"]
    CAR2["Carrier cos wct"]
    BM["Balanced Modulator"]
    DSB["DSB-SC Output Both Sidebands"]
    BPF["Bandpass Filter Selects One Sideband"]
    SSB2["SSB Output Single Sideband"]
    MSG2 --> BM
    CAR2 --> BM
    BM --> DSB
    DSB --> BPF
    BPF --> SSB2
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Coherent SSB Demodulator

```mermaid
graph LR
    RXIN["Received SSB Signal sSSB t"]
    LOC["Local Oscillator cos wct Synchronized"]
    MIX["Multiplier Product Detector"]
    LPF["Lowpass Filter cutoff Wm"]
    OUT2["Recovered Message mhat t"]
    RXIN --> MIX
    LOC --> MIX
    MIX --> LPF
    LPF --> OUT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Requirement:** The local oscillator must be **frequency-synchronized AND phase-locked** to the transmitter carrier. A Costas loop or PLL is typically used in practical SSB receivers to maintain synchronization.

## 4.5 Functional Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Modulation Technique Selection"]
    Q1{"Carrier Required?"}
    AM_PATH["Conventional AM DSB-FC Envelope Detection"]
    DSB_PATH["DSB-SC Coherent Detection"]
    Q2{"Both Sidebands Required?"}
    SSB_PATH["SSB-SC Coherent Detection Power and Bandwidth Efficient"]
    VSB_PATH["VSB Partial Sideband Used in TV"]
    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes| AM_PATH
    Q1 -->|No| DSB_PATH
    DSB_PATH --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| DSB_PATH
    Q2 -->|No| SSB_PATH
    AM_PATH -.-> VSB_PATH
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier (SSB-SC) modulation. State any two advantages of SSB-SC over conventional AM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** SSB-SC is an amplitude modulation technique in which the carrier and one of the two sidebands (either upper or lower) are suppressed, transmitting only the remaining sideband. It is generated by passing a DSB-SC signal through a highly selective bandpass filter, or by using the phase-shift (Hartley) method.

**Advantages over AM (1.5 marks):**
1. **Bandwidth reduction:** SSB-SC requires only $W_m$ Hz of bandwidth, compared to $2W_m$ Hz for AM, doubling the number of channels that can be accommodated.
2. **Power efficiency:** Since the carrier (which carries no information) and one redundant sideband are suppressed, all transmitted power is utilized for conveying the message, giving 100% power efficiency.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain why envelope detection cannot be used for demodulating SSB signals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An envelope detector (diode detector with RC filter) recovers the message only when the modulated envelope faithfully follows the message signal, which is true for AM (DSB-FC) provided $\mu \le 1$.

In SSB-SC, the carrier is absent and only one sideband is transmitted. The waveform does **not** have an envelope proportional to the original message — the envelope is severely distorted and contains artifacts from the missing carrier and sideband. Therefore, the envelope detector outputs a distorted version of $m(t)$, not $m(t)$ itself.

**SSB requires coherent (synchronous) detection**, where the incoming SSB signal is multiplied by a locally generated carrier that is phase- and frequency-synchronized with the transmitter, followed by low-pass filtering to recover $m(t)$. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the time-domain expression for the SSB-SC signal transmitting the **Upper Sideband (USB)** of a message $m(t)$ modulated on a carrier $A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$. Clearly state the role of the Hilbert transform in this derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **phase-shift (Hartley) method** of generating an SSB-SC signal. Show mathematically that one of the sidebands cancels at the output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Start with DSB-SC signal (1 mark):**

$$
s_{DSB}(t) = m(t) A_c \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

**Step 2 — Spectral decomposition (2 marks):**
Express $m(t)$ in terms of its positive and negative frequency parts:

$$
m(t) = m_+(t) + m_-(t)
$$

where $m_+(t)$ contains frequencies in $[0, W_m]$ and $m_-(t)$ contains frequencies in $[-W_m, 0]$.

**Step 3 — Form the DSB-SC spectrum (1 mark):**

$$
s_{DSB}(t) = m_+(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_+(t) e^{-j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{-j\omega_c t}
$$

**Step 4 — Select only the USB components (1 mark):**

$$
s_{USB}(t) = m_+(t) e^{j\omega_c t} + m_-(t) e^{-j\omega_c t}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler identities and recognize Hilbert transform (1 mark):**
Using $m_+(t) - m_-(t) = j\hat{m}(t)$ and algebraic manipulation, we obtain:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[ m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Role of Hilbert transform (1 mark):**
The Hilbert transform $\hat{m}(t)$ acts as a **broadband 90° phase shifter** for the message. By adding $m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ and $-\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$ (the in-phase and quadrature DSB signals), the upper and lower sidebands experience **constructive and destructive interference**, respectively. This selective cancellation is what allows only one sideband to survive — the essence of SSB generation.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The Hartley (Phase-Shift) SSB Modulator consists of:
- **Path 1:** Message $m(t)$ → Balanced Modulator 1 → output
- **Path 2:** Message $m(t)$ → 90° Phase Shifter → $\hat{m}(t)$ → Balanced Modulator 2 → output
- **Carrier split:** Carrier $\cos(\omega_c t)$ → directly to BM1; Carrier → 90° Phase Shifter → $\sin(\omega_c t)$ → to BM2
- **Summing Junction:** Adds both outputs with appropriate sign

**Mathematical Proof of Sideband Cancellation (4 marks):**

**Output of Balanced Modulator 1 (1 mark):**

$$
y_1(t) = m(t) \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

(DSB-SC with both USB and LSB)

**Output of Balanced Modulator 2 (1 mark):**

$$
y_2(t) = \hat{m}(t) \sin(\omega_c t)
$$

(Another DSB-SC, but with the message Hilbert-transformed and carrier in quadrature)

**Summing with appropriate sign for USB (1 mark):**

$$
s_{USB}(t) = y_1(t) - y_2(t) = m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

**Sideband Analysis (1 mark):** Taking the Fourier transform and using the Hilbert property $\hat{M}(f) = -j \text{sgn}(f) M(f)$:

- At positive frequencies: $M(f-f_c)$ adds with itself (constructive) and the Hilbert term contributes **negatively** with the same magnitude → USB dominates
- At negative frequencies (mirror USB): The two terms **cancel** → LSB is suppressed

Hence, only the **USB** appears at the output, while the LSB is fully cancelled.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** An SSB-SC signal is generated by a message $m(t) = 5\cos(1000\pi t)$ modulating a carrier $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^5 t)$. 
   - (i) Determine whether the resulting SSB is USB or LSB. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) Calculate the bandwidth and total transmitted power of the SSB-SC signal across a 50 Ω load. **(3 marks)**
   - (iii) Write the mathematical expression of the SSB-SC signal. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **coherent detection** method for SSB-SC signals with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the demodulated output when there is a phase error $\phi$ in the local oscillator. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $m(t) = 5\cos(1000\pi t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 500 \cdot t)$, so message frequency $W_m = 500$ Hz
- $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^5 t)$, so carrier frequency $f_c = 100$ kHz = 100,000 Hz

**(i) Identifying USB or LSB (2 marks):**

Using the phase-shift (Hartley) method with the convention:
- USB: $s_{USB}(t) = m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$
- LSB: $s_{LSB}(t) = m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) + \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$

For a single-tone message, the Hilbert transform of $\cos(\omega_m t)$ is $\sin(\omega_m t)$. Therefore:

$$
s_{USB}(t) = 5\cos(500\cdot 2\pi t)\cos(100000 \cdot 2\pi t) - 5\sin(500 \cdot 2\pi t)\sin(100000 \cdot 2\pi t)
$$

Applying the product-to-sum identity:
$$
\cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B = \cos(A+B)
$$

$$
s_{USB}(t) = 5\cos(2\pi(100000 + 500)t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 100500 \cdot t)
$$

The frequency $100{,}500$ Hz $> f_c = 100{,}000$ Hz → **Upper Sideband (USB)**.

**(ii) Bandwidth and Power (5 marks):**

**Bandwidth (2 marks):**
$$
BW_{SSB} = W_m = 500 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Power Calculation (3 marks):**
Mean square of the message: $\overline{m^2(t)} = \frac{5^2}{2} = 12.5 \text{ V}^2$

SSB-SC power across $R = 50\,\Omega$:

$$
P_{SSB} = \frac{A_c^2 \cdot \overline{m^2(t)}}{4R} = \frac{20^2 \cdot 12.5}{4 \cdot 50} = \frac{400 \cdot 12.5}{200} = \frac{5000}{200} = 25 \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) Mathematical Expression (2 marks):**

$$
s_{USB}(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 100500 \cdot t) \text{ volts}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Received SSB signal → Multiplier (Product Detector) → Lowpass Filter (cutoff $W_m$) → Output $\hat{m}(t)$

Local Oscillator $\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)$ feeds the multiplier.

**Mathematical Derivation (5 marks):**

Let the received SSB signal be (USB case):

$$
s_{USB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[ m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t) \right]
$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Multiply by the local carrier with phase error $\phi$:

$$
2\cos(\omega_c t + \phi) \cdot s_{USB}(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t + \phi) \left[ m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) - \hat{m}(t)\sin(\omega_c t) \right]
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Use the product-to-sum identities:

$$
2\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)\cos(\omega_c t) = \cos(\phi) + \cos(2\omega_c t + \phi)
$$

$$
-2\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)\sin(\omega_c t) = \sin(\phi) - \sin(2\omega_c t + \phi)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply lowpass filter to remove the $2\omega_c$ terms:

$$
y(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[ m(t)\cos(\phi) + \hat{m}(t)\sin(\phi) \right]
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The demodulated output is:

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[ m(t)\cos\phi + \hat{m}(t)\sin\phi \right]}
$$

**Observation:** 
- If $\phi = 0$: $y(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t)$ → **perfect demodulation**
- If $\phi \neq 0$: The output contains a **distorted** combination of $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$, leading to waveform distortion. This shows the **phase sensitivity** of coherent detection in SSB systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Mixing up the sign between USB and LSB. Always remember: **USB = minus, LSB = plus** (in the most commonly adopted KTU textbook convention).
> 2. **Bandwidth Confusion:** Many students write $BW = 2W_m$ for SSB — this is wrong! SSB bandwidth is **$W_m$**, exactly half of DSB.
> 3. **Power Formula Mistakes:** SSB power is $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{4R}$, NOT $\frac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{2R}$ (which is DSB-SC). Forgetting the factor of 1/4 loses full marks.
> 4. **Detection Method:** Writing "envelope detector" for SSB demodulation is a guaranteed **zero** for that sub-part. Always specify **coherent/synchronous detection**.
> 5. **Hilbert Transform Definition:** Do not confuse Hilbert transform with Fourier transform. The Hilbert transform shifts the **phase** by ±90°, it does not change the **magnitude** of the spectrum.
> 6. **Phase Error in Coherent Detection:** Students often forget the $\hat{m}(t)\sin\phi$ term in the demodulated output. The full expression must be written to earn full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SSB Modulation**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **SSB** transmits only **one** sideband (USB or LSB); both carrier and the other sideband are suppressed.
- ✅ **SSB bandwidth** = $W_m$ (half of DSB).
- ✅ **SSB-SC power** = $\dfrac{A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{4R}$ (half of DSB-SC).
- ✅ **Detection** must be **coherent (synchronous)** — envelope detector will not work.
- ✅ Phase error in local carrier causes **distortion** of the recovered message.

### Time-Domain Expressions
- ✅ $s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t]$
- ✅ $s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[m(t)\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t]$
- ✅ $\hat{m}(t)$ = **Hilbert transform** of $m(t)$ (broadband 90° phase shifter)

### Generation Methods
- ✅ **Filter Method** — DSB-SC + sharp BPF (needs very selective filter)
- ✅ **Phase-Shift (Hartley) Method** — Two balanced modulators + 90° phase shifters
- ✅ **Weaver's Method** — Two-stage modulation, avoids wideband phase shifters

### Detection Method
- ✅ **Coherent Detector:** Multiply by $2\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)$, then LPF
- ✅ Output: $y(t) = m(t)\cos\phi + \hat{m}(t)\sin\phi$

### Advantages
- ✅ **50% bandwidth saving** (twice the number of channels)
- ✅ **High power efficiency** (no carrier or redundant sideband power)
- ✅ **Reduced noise** (narrower bandwidth admits less noise)

### Disadvantages
- ✅ **Complex and costly receivers** (need carrier synchronization)
- ✅ **Difficult to generate** at low frequencies (sharp filter requirement)
- ✅ **Phase-sensitive demodulation**

### Key Properties of Hilbert Transform
- ✅ $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f) \cdot M(f)$
- ✅ Energy preserved: $\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} = \overline{m^2(t)}$
- ✅ $m(t) = -\hat{\hat{m}}(t)$ (double Hilbert transform = negative of original)

### Applications
- ✅ **HF radio communications** (maritime, aviation, military)
- ✅ **Amateur (Ham) radio** bands
- ✅ **Point-to-point voice and data links**
- ✅ **Land mobile radio systems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle Modulation – Frequency and Phase Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Angle Modulation – Frequency and Phase Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Angle Modulation

In continuous-wave (CW) modulation theory, **Angle Modulation** is the class of modulation techniques in which the **angle (argument)** of a sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the baseband (message) signal $m(t)$, while the amplitude of the carrier is held constant. Mathematically, if the carrier is expressed in its generalized complex-exponential form, the transmitted signal is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[\theta_i(t)\bigr]$$

where $A_c$ is the **constant carrier amplitude** and $\theta_i(t)$ is the **instantaneous phase** of the modulated wave, which is a function of the message signal $m(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Angle modulation is a non-linear modulation scheme, meaning superposition of message signals does **not** result in superposition of modulated signals. This distinguishes it from amplitude modulation (linear modulation).

Angle modulation comprises two principal sub-classes:

1. **Phase Modulation (PM):** The instantaneous phase deviation is proportional to the message signal $m(t)$.
2. **Frequency Modulation (FM):** The instantaneous frequency deviation is proportional to the message signal $m(t)$.

The general modulated waveform for angle modulation is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\bigr]$$

where $f_c$ is the **carrier frequency** and $\phi(t)$ is the **phase deviation** (or excess phase) produced by the modulation process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Steering Wheel vs. The Accelerator

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Intuitive Analogy):** Imagine you are driving a car on a circular track at a constant speed (constant amplitude, fixed average speed = fixed carrier frequency).

- **Phase Modulation (PM)** is like the **steering wheel**: The *position* of the steering wheel is directly controlled by the message. Turn it gently, and the car points slightly to a new direction; turn it sharply, and the car swerves aggressively. The angle of the wheel (phase) is *directly* proportional to the input.
- **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is like the **accelerator (or brake) pedal**: The *rate of turning* the steering wheel (i.e., the *change* in direction per unit time, which is the frequency of the car's heading rotation) is proportional to the message. Press the pedal, the car spins faster.

Thus, **PM and FM are intimately related**: integrating the message gives FM from PM, and differentiating the message gives PM from FM.

## 1.3 Phase Modulation (PM) — Formal Definition

In Phase Modulation, the **instantaneous phase deviation** $\phi_{\text{PM}}(t)$ is directly proportional to the modulating (message) signal $m(t)$:

$$\phi_{\text{PM}}(t) = k_p \, m(t)$$

where $k_p$ is the **phase sensitivity (or phase deviation constant)** of the PM modulator, measured in **radians per volt (rad/V)**. The PM modulated signal is:

$$s_{\text{PM}}(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + k_p \, m(t)\bigr]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum phase deviation is denoted by $\Delta\phi$ (in **radians**), and the **phase modulation index** $\beta_p$ is defined as $\beta_p = \Delta\phi$.

## 1.4 Frequency Modulation (FM) — Formal Definition

In Frequency Modulation, the **instantaneous frequency deviation** is directly proportional to the message signal $m(t)$. The instantaneous frequency is:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \, m(t)$$

where $k_f$ is the **frequency sensitivity (or frequency deviation constant)** of the FM modulator, measured in **Hz per volt (Hz/V)**. The instantaneous phase is the integral of instantaneous frequency:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{t} f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

So the FM modulated signal is:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The maximum frequency deviation is denoted by $\Delta f$ (in **Hz**), and the **frequency modulation index** $\beta_f$ is defined as $\beta_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$, where $W$ is the highest message frequency (bandwidth of the message).

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Carrier amplitude** $A_c$: held constant in angle modulation (in Volts).
- **Carrier frequency** $f_c$: in **Hz** (usually RF range, e.g., 88–108 MHz for commercial FM broadcast).
- **Phase sensitivity** $k_p$: in **rad/V**.
- **Frequency sensitivity** $k_f$: in **Hz/V**.
- **Frequency deviation** $\Delta f$: in **Hz**.
- **Phase deviation** $\Delta\phi$: in **radians**.
- **Modulation index** $\beta$: dimensionless quantity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — General Angle-Modulated Signal Framework

Start with the generic carrier:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Introduce the excess phase $\phi(t)$ to yield:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\bigr]$$

### Step 2 — Define Instantaneous Frequency

The instantaneous frequency $f_i(t)$ is the time-derivative of the *total instantaneous phase angle* $\theta_i(t)$ (divided by $2\pi$):

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = f_c + \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** Excess phase $\phi(t)$ and instantaneous frequency deviation are coupled by the calculus pair: $\phi(t) = 2\pi \int f_d(\tau)\, d\tau$ and $f_d(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\dfrac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$.

### Step 3 — Distinguish PM and FM

- **For PM:** $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$, so $f_d(t) = \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi}\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.
- **For FM:** $f_d(t) = k_f m(t)$, so $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$.

### Step 4 — The PM-FM Duality Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PM and FM are interchangeable** by passing the message signal through an integrator (to convert PM to FM) or a differentiator (to convert FM to PM), followed by appropriate scaling of the deviation constants.

Specifically:
- If $s(t)$ is an FM signal produced by $m(t)$, then $s(t)$ is **also** a PM signal produced by $\int m(t)\, dt$.
- If $s(t)$ is a PM signal produced by $m(t)$, then $s(t)$ is **also** an FM signal produced by $\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.

This is why a **PM modulator followed by an integrator** is the classic Armstrong indirect FM generation technique.

## 2.2 Modulation Index — Unifying Concept

For a **single-tone message** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

| Modulation Type | Maximum Deviation | Modulation Index $\beta$ |
|---|---|---|
| PM | $\Delta\phi = k_p A_m$ | $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ |
| FM | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | $\beta_f = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$ |

## 2.3 Bandwidth Classification

- **Narrowband FM (NBFM):** $\beta_f \ll 1$ (typically $\beta_f < 0.5$ rad). Bandwidth $\approx 2W$.
- **Wideband FM (WBFM):** $\beta_f \gg 1$ (typically $\beta_f \ge 5$ rad). Bandwidth computed via Carson's rule or Bessel-function analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the modulation index of PM and FM. For a single-tone message of frequency $f_m$, PM's $\beta_p$ is *independent* of $f_m$ (it depends on $A_m$), while FM's $\beta_f$ is *inversely* proportional to $f_m$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General angle-modulated signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)]$ | $A_c$ in V, $f_c$ in Hz |
| 2 | Instantaneous frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\dfrac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$ | Hz |
| 3 | PM phase deviation | $\phi_{\text{PM}}(t) = k_p m(t)$ | $k_p$ in rad/V |
| 4 | FM excess phase | $\phi_{\text{FM}}(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $k_f$ in Hz/V |
| 5 | PM max phase deviation | $\Delta\phi = k_p A_m$ | radians |
| 6 | FM max frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Hz |
| 7 | PM modulation index | $\beta_p = \Delta\phi = k_p A_m$ | dimensionless (rad) |
| 8 | FM modulation index | $\beta_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Carson's Rule (bandwidth) | $B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2W(\beta + 1)$ | Hz |
| 10 | Bessel-function spectral amplitude | $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert$ for $n$th-order sideband | dimensionless |
| 11 | NBFM bandwidth | $B_{\text{NBFM}} \approx 2W$ | Hz |
| 12 | WBFM (high-$\beta$) rule of thumb | $B_T \approx 2\Delta f = 2\beta W$ | Hz |
| 13 | FM signal power | $P_s = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Watts (constant) |
| 14 | Single-tone FM spectrum coefficient | $s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos[2\pi(f_c + nf_m)t]$ | Fourier series |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FM Broadcast Radio (88–108 MHz):** Uses WBFM with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $W = 15$ kHz, giving $\beta_f = 5$.
- **TV Audio (Analog):** Uses FM with $\Delta f = 25$ kHz.
- **Two-Way Radio / Walkie-Talkies:** NBFM with $\beta_f \approx 1$.
- **PM Applications:** Older analog mobile communication systems, phase-shift keying (PSK) precursor concepts.
- **Key advantage of FM over AM:** Superior noise immunity due to constant envelope (information carried in zero-crossings, not amplitude).
- **Key disadvantage:** Requires significantly larger bandwidth than AM (Carson's rule trade-off).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Narrowband FM (NBFM) Approximation from the General FM Signal

We start with the FM signal:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

Use the trigonometric identity $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(\beta\sin\text{-phase term}) - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(\beta\sin\text{-phase term})$$

Define $\theta(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$ as the excess phase. So:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos[\theta(t)] - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin[\theta(t)]$$

**NBFM condition:** When $\vert \theta(t) \vert \ll 1$ radian, we apply the small-angle approximation:

$$\cos[\theta(t)] \approx 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sin[\theta(t)] \approx \theta(t)$$

Substituting:

$$s_{\text{NBFM}}(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A_c \theta(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

Insert $\theta(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$:

$$s_{\text{NBFM}}(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - 2\pi A_c k_f \left[\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The NBFM signal structurally resembles a DSB-SC AM signal where the message is replaced by the integral of the message. This is the foundation of Armstrong's indirect FM generator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Tone FM Spectrum via Bessel Functions

Consider the single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. Then:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

The excess phase becomes:

$$\theta(t) = 2\pi k_f \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Hence the FM signal is:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)\bigr]$$

We use the **Jacobi-Anger expansion** identity:

$$e^{j\beta\sin x} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\, e^{jnx}$$

where $J_n(\beta)$ is the **Bessel function of the first kind, order $n$**. Applying this:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = \text{Re}\left\{A_c\, e^{j2\pi f_c t}\, e^{j\beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)}\right\}$$

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = \text{Re}\left\{A_c\, e^{j2\pi f_c t} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta_f)\, e^{jn 2\pi f_m t}\right\}$$

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta_f)\cos\bigl[2\pi(f_c + nf_m)t\bigr]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The FM spectrum is **theoretically infinite** in extent, consisting of a carrier at $f_c$ and an infinite set of sidebands at $f_c \pm f_m$, $f_c \pm 2f_m$, $f_c \pm 3f_m$, … with amplitudes $A_c \vert J_n(\beta_f) \vert$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth

For a bandlimited message with highest frequency $W$, the FM spectrum theoretically extends to infinity. However, **practical transmission** truncates sidebands whose amplitudes fall below a threshold (typically 1% of carrier, i.e., $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert \ge 0.01$).

For **large $\beta$**, an empirical engineering approximation is **Carson's Rule**:

$$B_T = 2(\Delta f + W)$$

**Derivation Sketch:**

The number of significant sidebands (on either side of the carrier) is approximately $\beta + 1$. Each sideband pair occupies $f_m$ spacing. For a single-tone message, $f_m = W$ and the sideband pair count is $\beta+1$, so:

$$B_T = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{W} + 1\right)W = 2(\Delta f + W)$$

For general (non-single-tone) bandlimited message:

$$\boxed{B_T = 2(\Delta f + W)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Carson's rule captures approximately 98% of the total FM signal power. It is the most widely used bandwidth formula in KTU board exams.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Average Power of FM Signal

The instantaneous power of $s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos[\theta(t)]$ is:

$$p(t) = s_{\text{FM}}^2(t) = A_c^2 \cos^2[\theta(t)]$$

The average power is:

$$P_s = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2} A_c^2 \cos^2[\theta(t)]\, dt = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Result:** The average power of an FM (or PM) signal is **independent** of the modulation index $\beta$ and the message signal. All modulation-induced power is redistributed among the sidebands; the carrier amplitude remains $A_c$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Relationship Between PM and FM Modulation Indices

For the *same* single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

- **PM index:** $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ (depends only on $A_m$).
- **FM index:** $\beta_f = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ (depends on both $A_m$ and $f_m$).

If we want the *same* signal to be either PM or FM:

- Treating $s(t)$ as PM of $m(t)$: $\beta_p = k_p A_m = \Delta\phi$.
- Treating $s(t)$ as FM of $m(t)$: $\beta_f = k_f A_m / f_m = \Delta f / f_m$.

The relationship: $\beta_p = \beta_f$ when the message amplitude is scaled appropriately.

## 3.6 Numerical Example — Worked Bessel Function Problem

**Problem:** An FM signal has $\beta_f = 5$, $f_c = 100$ MHz, $f_m = 1$ kHz, $A_c = 10$ V. Compute:
1. Frequency deviation $\Delta f$.
2. Carson's-rule bandwidth $B_T$.
3. Highest-order significant sideband (using rule: $n_{\max} \approx \beta + 1$).

**Solution:**

Step 1: $\Delta f = \beta_f \cdot f_m = 5 \times 1\,\text{kHz} = 5\,\text{kHz}$.

Step 2: $B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(5\,\text{kHz} + 1\,\text{kHz}) = 12\,\text{kHz}$.

Step 3: $n_{\max} = \beta + 1 = 6$, so significant sidebands exist from $n = -6$ to $n = +6$, spanning $f_c \pm 6f_m = 100\,\text{MHz} \pm 6\,\text{kHz}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Conceptual Map: PM vs. FM Relationship

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the duality and calculus-pair relationship between Phase Modulation and Frequency Modulation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMsg[Message Signal m of t] --> nodePMdirect[PM Modulator with sensitivity kp]
    nodeMsg --> nodeInt[Integrator Block]
    nodeInt --> nodeFMviaInt[FM Modulator with sensitivity kf]
    nodeMsg --> nodeDiff[Differentiator Block]
    nodeDiff --> nodePMviaDiff[PM Modulator with sensitivity kp]
    nodePMdirect --> nodeOut1[PM Output s PM of t]
    nodeFMviaInt --> nodeOut2[FM Output s FM of t]
    nodePMviaDiff --> nodeOut2
    nodeOut2 --> nodeCarrier[Constant Envelope Carrier of Frequency fc]
    nodeOut1 --> nodeCarrier
    nodeCarrier --> nodeBW[Bandwidth by Carson Rule 2 Delta f plus W]
    nodeBW --> nodeApps[Applications FM Radio TV Audio PM in PSK Precursors]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Armstrong Indirect FM Generator

The Armstrong indirect method uses PM followed by integration to generate WBFM from NBFM. This is the most-tested generation method in KTU board questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSrc[Message Source m of t] --> nodeBalMod[Balanced Modulator Produces DSB SC]
    nodeOsc[Carrier Oscillator fc] --> nodeBalMod
    nodeBalMod --> nodeMixer1[Frequency Mixer Stage 1]
    nodeLO1[Local Oscillator flo1] --> nodeMixer1
    nodeMixer1 --> nodeBPF1[Bandpass Filter selects lower sideband]
    nodeBPF1 --> nodePM[Phase Shifter Network yields NBPM]
    nodePM --> nodeInt[Integrator Block converts NBPM to NBFM]
    nodeInt --> nodeFreqMult[Frequency Multiplier chain n times]
    nodeFreqMult --> nodeMixer2[Frequency Mixer Stage 2]
    nodeLO2[Local Oscillator flo2] --> nodeMixer2
    nodeMixer2 --> nodeBPF2[Bandpass Filter selects desired FM carrier]
    nodeBPF2 --> nodeOut[WBFM Output s of t at carrier fc]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Spectrum Comparison: AM, NBFM, and WBFM

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the relative spectral footprints of the three modulation schemes for an equivalent message bandwidth $W$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAM[AM Spectrum Carrier plus Two Sidebands Total BW 2W]
    nodeNBFM[NBFM Spectrum Carrier plus Two Sidebands Total BW approx 2W]
    nodeWBFM[WBFM Spectrum Carrier plus many Sidebands Total BW by Carson 2 Delta f plus W]
    nodeAM --> nodeTrade[Bandwidth Power Trade off]
    nodeNBFM --> nodeTrade
    nodeWBFM --> nodeTrade
    nodeTrade --> nodeKey[Key Result FM trades bandwidth for noise immunity]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Instantaneous Frequency vs. Time for FM

The Mermaid diagram below models the time-domain variation of $f_i(t)$ for an FM signal with a sinusoidal message, illustrating the deviation $\Delta f$ around $f_c$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFc[Carrier Frequency fc on y axis] --> nodeMid[Midline Reference]
    nodeDeltaUp[Upper Bound fc plus Delta f] --> nodeMid
    nodeDeltaLow[Lower Bound fc minus Delta f] --> nodeMid
    nodeMsgShape[Message Shape A cos two pi fm t] --> nodeFreqSweep[Frequency Sweep follows message envelope]
    nodeFreqSweep --> nodeDev[Maximum deviation occurs at message peaks]
    nodeDev --> nodePeriod[Period of sweep equals one over fm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Short Answer [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define angle modulation. Differentiate between Phase Modulation (PM) and Frequency Modulation (FM) with suitable equations.

**Model Answer:**

Angle modulation is a class of continuous-wave modulation in which the angle (or argument) of the carrier sinusoid is varied in accordance with the baseband message signal $m(t)$, while the carrier amplitude $A_c$ is held constant.

The general form is $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)]$, where $\phi(t)$ is the excess phase.

**PM vs FM:**
- **PM:** $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ — phase deviation is proportional to the message.
- **FM:** $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$ — phase deviation is proportional to the integral of the message; equivalently, instantaneous frequency deviation is proportional to $m(t)$.

In PM, the *phase* tracks the message directly; in FM, the *frequency* tracks the message. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2 — Short Answer [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** Define the modulation index of an FM signal. For a single-tone modulating signal $A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, derive the expression for the FM modulation index.

**Model Answer:**

The FM modulation index $\beta_f$ is defined as the ratio of the maximum frequency deviation $\Delta f$ to the highest message frequency $W$ (or modulating frequency $f_m$ for a single tone):

$$\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}$$

For $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the instantaneous frequency is $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, so the maximum deviation is $\Delta f = k_f A_m$. Hence:

$$\beta_f = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity indicating the depth of modulation. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Starting from the general expression of an angle-modulated signal, derive the mathematical representation of a Frequency Modulated (FM) signal and clearly define the frequency sensitivity constant $k_f$. Derive the expression for the instantaneous frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-tone message $m(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \times 500\, t)$ is used to frequency modulate a $100$ MHz carrier. The frequency sensitivity is $k_f = 10$ kHz/V. Determine:
  1. The modulation index $\beta_f$.
  2. The Carson's-rule bandwidth $B_T$.
