  3. The maximum phase deviation $\Delta\phi$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — General Angle Modulation:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos[\theta_i(t)] = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)]$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous Frequency Definition:**

The instantaneous frequency is defined as the time-derivative of the total instantaneous phase (in radians per second) divided by $2\pi$:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = f_c + \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — FM Constraint:**

In FM, the instantaneous frequency deviation $f_i(t) - f_c$ is proportional to $m(t)$:

$$f_i(t) - f_c = k_f m(t) \implies f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

The constant $k_f$ has units of **Hz per volt** and is called the *frequency sensitivity* of the FM modulator.

**Step 4 — Integrate to Obtain Phase:**

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi \int_{-\infty}^{t} f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Final FM Signal:**

$$\boxed{s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $A_m = 5$ V, $f_m = 500$ Hz, $f_c = 100$ MHz, $k_f = 10$ kHz/V.

**Part (i) — Modulation Index:**

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 10\,\text{kHz/V} \times 5\,\text{V} = 50\,\text{kHz}$$

$$\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{50\,\text{kHz}}{500\,\text{Hz}} = 100$$

**Part (ii) — Carson's Rule Bandwidth:**

$$B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(50\,\text{kHz} + 500\,\text{Hz}) = 2(50.5\,\text{kHz}) = 101\,\text{kHz}$$

**Part (iii) — Maximum Phase Deviation:**

For a single-tone message, the maximum phase deviation equals the modulation index:

$$\Delta\phi_{\max} = \beta_f = 100\,\text{radians}$$

(Equivalently, $\Delta\phi_{\max} = 2\pi k_f \int m(t)\, dt_{\max} = 2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot \dfrac{5}{2\pi \cdot 500} = 100$ rad.) **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, show that the FM signal can be expressed as a Fourier series using Bessel functions, and hence determine the FM signal's bandwidth using Carson's rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FM broadcast station transmits a maximum frequency deviation of $\Delta f = 75$ kHz with a highest message frequency of $W = 15$ kHz.
  1. Calculate the modulation index $\beta_f$.
  2. Determine the Carson's-rule transmission bandwidth.
  3. If a sinusoidal message of $f_m = 15$ kHz is applied, find the number of significant sideband pairs (use $n \approx \beta + 1$ rule).
  4. Comment on the noise-immunity vs. bandwidth trade-off in this scheme. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Single-Tone FM Expression:**

With $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the integral is:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

The excess phase is:

$$\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

So the FM signal becomes:

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$$

**Step 2 — Complex Exponential Form:**

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = \text{Re}\left\{A_c\, e^{j2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)}\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Jacobi–Anger Identity:**

$$e^{j\beta_f \sin(2\pi f_m t)} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta_f)\, e^{jn 2\pi f_m t}$$

where $J_n(\beta_f)$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order $n$.

**Step 4 — Fourier Series Expansion:**

$$s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta_f)\cos[2\pi(f_c + nf_m)t]$$

The spectrum consists of a carrier ($n = 0$) and sidebands at $f_c \pm f_m$, $f_c \pm 2f_m$, … with amplitudes $A_c \vert J_n(\beta_f) \vert$.

**Step 5 — Carson's Rule:**

For large $\beta_f$, significant sidebands extend up to order $n \approx \beta_f + 1$, giving bandwidth:

$$B_T = 2(\beta_f + 1)f_m = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{W} + 1\right)W = 2(\Delta f + W)$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Part (i):**

$$\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f}{W} = \frac{75\,\text{kHz}}{15\,\text{kHz}} = 5$$

**Part (ii):**

$$B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(75 + 15)\,\text{kHz} = 180\,\text{kHz}$$

**Part (iii):**

For $f_m = 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5$, so the number of significant sideband pairs is approximately $n \approx \beta + 1 = 6$. Total significant sideband count = $2 \times 6 = 12$ sidebands (6 on each side of the carrier).

**Part (iv):**

FM trade-off: Compared to AM (which needs only $2W = 30$ kHz), FM requires 180 kHz — six times more bandwidth. However, this expanded bandwidth buys **superior noise immunity** (theoretically infinite SNR improvement for high modulation index via the **Carson's rule / bandwidth exchange**), constant-envelope transmission (allowing efficient class-C amplifiers), and the capture effect (rejection of weak co-channel interference). This is precisely why commercial FM broadcast is preferred for high-fidelity audio. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mark-loss area (Part A):** Forgetting to include units when stating $k_f$ (Hz/V) and $k_p$ (rad/V).
> - **Most common mark-loss area (Part B):** Failing to apply Carson's rule as $B_T = 2(\Delta f + W)$ and not $B_T = 2(\Delta f) + 2W$ written ambiguously; examiners expect the factored form. Also, students often confuse $W$ (highest message frequency) with $f_m$ (single-tone modulating frequency) — they are *equal* for single-tone, but *different* for general bandlimited messages.
> - **Bessel function trap:** Students may write $J_n$ without absolute-value bars when computing sideband *amplitudes*; the amplitude is $\vert J_n \vert$ since $J_n$ can be negative for certain $n, \beta$ combinations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready)**

- **Angle modulation** = constant envelope, non-linear modulation; $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)]$.
- **PM** deviates the *phase* proportional to $m(t)$; **FM** deviates the *frequency* proportional to $m(t)$.
- **PM and FM are dual** — differ by an integrator/differentiator.
- **Frequency sensitivity $k_f$** in **Hz/V**; **phase sensitivity $k_p$** in **rad/V**.
- **FM modulation index:** $\beta_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$ — *depends on $f_m$*.
- **PM modulation index:** $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ — *independent of $f_m$*.
- **Carson's rule:** $B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2W(\beta + 1)$ — captures ~98% of FM power.
- **NBFM** when $\beta \ll 1$: bandwidth $\approx 2W$, similar to AM.
- **WBFM** when $\beta \gg 1$: bandwidth $\approx 2\Delta f$.
- **Average FM power** = $A_c^2 / 2$ — *constant, independent of $\beta$*.
- **Single-tone FM spectrum:** $s(t) = A_c \sum_n J_n(\beta)\cos[2\pi(f_c + nf_m)t]$ — infinite in theory.
- **Bessel function zeros:** For $J_0(\beta) = 0$, the carrier component vanishes; this is the basis of Bessel-null FM measurement.
- **Armstrong indirect FM generator:** NBPM $\rightarrow$ Integrator $\rightarrow$ Frequency Multipliers $\rightarrow$ Mixer to translate carrier to final $f_c$.
- **Direct FM generation:** Uses a VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator) directly modulated by $m(t)$.
- **Key advantage of FM:** Superior noise immunity (constant envelope, trade bandwidth for SNR).
- **Key disadvantage:** Much larger bandwidth than AM (Carson's rule).
- **Real-world FM broadcast:** $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $W = 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5$, $B_T = 180$ kHz, $f_c$ in 88–108 MHz VHF band.
- **Exam formula to memorize cold:** $B_T = 2(\Delta f + W)$, $\beta_f = \Delta f / f_m$, $P_{\text{FM}} = A_c^2/2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory and Generation of Frequency Modulation (FM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory and Generation of Frequency Modulation (FM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is an angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier wave is varied linearly in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the baseband (modulating) signal, while the amplitude of the carrier is held constant.

The single-tone FM signal is mathematically expressed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_c t \,+\, 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \right]$$

where each symbol carries a precise physical meaning:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $A_c$ | Carrier amplitude | Volts |
| $f_c$ | Carrier (rest) frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $k_f$ | Frequency sensitivity of the modulator | Hz / Volt |
| $m(t)$ | Baseband modulating signal | Volts |
| $A_m$ | Peak amplitude of $m(t)$ | Volts |
| $f_m$ | Frequency of the modulating tone | Hertz (Hz) |

The term inside the brackets is called the **total instantaneous phase** $\theta_i(t)$, and the time derivative of this quantity (divided by $2\pi$) yields the **instantaneous frequency**:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\, m(t)$$

The maximum deviation of the instantaneous frequency from $f_c$ is the **frequency deviation**:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max } = k_f A_m\;}$$

The dimensionless ratio of frequency deviation to modulating frequency is the **modulation index**:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: The modulation index $\beta$ is **not** the same as the percentage modulation used in AM. In FM, $\beta$ can exceed 1 freely — this is what makes FM a *wideband* system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing (the swing = carrier wave) at a steady rhythmic pace. Now watch what happens when the child's excitement level (the modulating signal) changes:

- **When the child is calm** (small $m(t)$) → you push at a normal, steady swing rate (carrier frequency $f_c$).
- **When the child gets excited and screams loudly** (large positive $m(t)$) → you instinctively speed up the pushes to match the energy (frequency swings *higher*).
- **When the child gets bored and yawns** (large negative $m(t)$) → you slow the swing (frequency drops *lower*).
- **The amplitude of the swing (carrier amplitude) remains unchanged** — only the *pacing* (frequency) of your push varies.

> This is **exactly** what an FM modulator does. The *speed* (frequency) of the carrier follows the *loudness* (amplitude) of the voice, but the carrier's *size* (amplitude) never changes. This is why FM is naturally immune to amplitude noise (lightning, electrical interference) — those noises change the swing's *size*, which FM simply ignores.

The constant $k_f$ is analogous to the *sensitivity* of the pusher: a high $k_f$ means a tiny whisper produces a huge change in swing speed, while a low $k_f$ means even a shout barely changes the rhythm.

---

## 1.3 Narrowband vs Wideband FM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. Memorize the threshold:

| Class | Condition | Approximate Bandwidth | Spectral Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Narrowband FM (NBFM)** | $\beta \ll 1$ (typically $\beta < 0.5$) | $\text{BW} \approx 2 f_m$ | Carrier + one pair of sidebands (similar shape to AM) |
| **Wideband FM (WBFM)** | $\beta \gg 1$ (e.g., $\beta = 5$ for commercial FM) | $\text{BW} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ (Carson's rule) | Carrier + many sideband pairs; bandwidth grows with $\beta$ |

> The **Armstrong indirect method** exploits this distinction: NBFM is *easy* to generate, WBFM is *hard* to generate directly — so we generate NBFM first, then *multiply* the deviation and frequency up to WBFM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematics of Angle Modulation

FM is a *special case* of **angle modulation**, where the general form of the modulated signal is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\theta_i(t)\right] = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\right]$$

The quantity $\phi(t)$ is the **phase deviation**, and the **instantaneous angular frequency** is:

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_c + \frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

For FM, the phase deviation is the **integral** of the message:

$$\phi_{\text{FM}}(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This integration property is the *defining mathematical fingerprint* of FM — and the source of its noise-immunity advantage.

---

## 2.2 Spectral Representation — Bessel Function Expansion

When the modulating signal is a **single sinusoid** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the FM signal admits a beautiful decomposition into an infinite sum of harmonics using **Bessel functions of the first kind** $J_n(\beta)$:

$$\boxed{\;s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} J_n(\beta)\, \cos\!\left[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\right]\;}$$

This means the FM spectrum consists of a **carrier** (at $f_c$) plus an **infinite set of sideband pairs** spaced at integer multiples of $f_m$ away from the carrier. The amplitude of the $n$-th pair is governed by $J_n(\beta)$.

### Key Bessel Function Properties (KEE 2024 Hot List)

| Property | Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$ | Symmetry for even/odd $n$ | Lower and upper sidebands are *mirror images* in magnitude |
| $J_0(2.405) = 0$ | First null of carrier | At $\beta = 2.405$, the carrier component vanishes completely |
| $J_n(\beta) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ | Tail decay | Sidebands far from carrier are negligible; this *truncates* the spectrum |
| $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ | Power conservation | Total sideband power always equals $A_c^2/2$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize every row. KTU 2024 ESE questions are crafted by combining 2–3 of these in a single 14-mark problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ | Instantaneous frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Peak frequency deviation | Hz |
| 3 | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = k_f A_m / f_m$ | Modulation index (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Phase deviation (FM) | rad |
| 5 | $\text{BW}_{\text{NBFM}} \approx 2 f_m$ | NBFM bandwidth | Hz |
| 6 | $\text{BW}_{\text{WBFM}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$ | Carson's rule bandwidth | Hz |
| 7 | $P_s = A_c^2 / 2$ | Total average FM power (constant!) | W |
| 8 | $P_{\text{carrier}} = (A_c^2/2)\, J_0^2(\beta)$ | Power in carrier component | W |
| 9 | $P_{\text{sidebands}} = (A_c^2/2)\!\left[1 - J_0^2(\beta)\right]$ | Power in all sidebands | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{power}} = 1 - J_0^2(\beta)$ | Power efficiency of FM | — |
| 11 | $f_{\text{multiplier output}} = n \cdot f_{\text{input}}$ | Frequency multiplier scaling | Hz |
| 12 | $\Delta f_{\text{multiplier output}} = n \cdot \Delta f_{\text{input}}$ | Deviation scaling | Hz |

> Note: Notation $\vert \cdot \vert_{\max}$ in row 2 means *peak value* (absolute-value maximum).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial FM Broadcasting (88–108 MHz):** $\beta = 5$ is mandated; $\Delta f = 75$ kHz; Carson's rule gives BW = 200 kHz per channel. This *wide bandwidth* is exactly why FM is so robust against noise and multipath fading.
- **Two-Way Radio & Marine Communication:** NBFM ($\beta < 1$) is used to conserve spectrum; channel spacing of 12.5 kHz is common.
- **Space Telemetry & Spacecraft Tracking:** Deep-space probes use WBFM subcarriers (typically $\beta = 1$ to $5$).
- **Radar & SONAR:** The FM "chirp" is a linearly swept FM waveform used for matched-filter pulse compression — improving range resolution dramatically.
- **FM Synthesis (Music Electronics):** Musical synthesizers (Yamaha DX7, etc.) generate complex timbres using FM operators — a direct commercial descendant of Chowning's discovery.

> The **constant envelope** of FM ($A_c$ never changes) means the **transmitter power amplifier can operate at saturation** — yielding 100% DC-to-RF efficiency, unlike AM transmitters which must "back off" to preserve linearity. This is the second major engineering advantage of FM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — From the FM Definition to the Time-Domain Expression

We begin from the formal definition: the instantaneous frequency equals the carrier plus a term proportional to the message.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of instantaneous angular frequency.**

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_c + 2\pi k_f\, m(t)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides with respect to $t$ to recover the total phase $\theta_i(t)$.**

$$\theta_i(t) = \int_0^t \omega_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

> *Conversion logic:* Because $\omega_i(t)$ is the *time derivative* of phase, the inverse operation (integration) recovers the phase. The constant of integration is set to zero assuming zero initial phase.

**Step 3 — Substitute the total phase into the generic sinusoidal carrier template $s(t) = A_c \cos[\theta_i(t)]$.**

$$\boxed{\;s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\, 2\pi f_c t \,+\, 2\pi k_f \!\int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau \,\right]\;}$$

This is the canonical time-domain representation. The **integration of $m(t)$** inside the cosine is the unmistakable signature that this is FM (and not PM, where the message itself would appear inside the cosine).

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — FM Spectrum via Bessel Function Expansion (Single-Tone Modulation)

Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. We wish to express $s(t)$ in terms of harmonics of $f_m$.

**Step 1 — Compute the integral inside the cosine.**

$$\int_0^t A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and define $\beta$.**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi k_f A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right] = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Bessel function identity.**

The Jacobi–Anger expansion states that for any real $x$ and $\beta$:

$$e^{j\beta \sin(\theta)} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} J_n(\beta)\, e^{jn\theta}$$

Taking the real part and using $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) = A_c\, \text{Re}\!\left\{ e^{j2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)} \right\}$:

$$s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} J_n(\beta)\, \cos\!\left[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the spectral structure.**

The $n=0$ term is $A_c J_0(\beta) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — the carrier.
The $n=\pm 1$ terms are $A_c J_1(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c \pm f_m) t]$ — the first pair of sidebands.
Each $n$-th sideband is at frequency $f_c + n f_m$ with amplitude $A_c J_n(\beta)$.

> *Conclusion:* Unlike AM (which has only 3 spectral components), an FM signal theoretically has an *infinite* number of sidebands. Practically, we keep only those with $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert > 0.01$, and the number of "significant" sidebands is approximately $n \approx \beta + 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Carson's Rule for Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Count the number of significant sidebands.**

Empirically and theoretically, $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert < 0.04$ for $n > \beta + 1$ for most practical $\beta$. So the number of significant upper sidebands is $N \approx \beta + 1$.

**Step 2 — Compute the total spectral spread.**

The highest significant sideband is at $f_c + (\beta + 1) f_m$. The lowest is at $f_c - (\beta + 1) f_m$.

$$\text{Total spread} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$.**

$$\text{BW} = 2\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right)\! f_m = 2 \Delta f + 2 f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{BW}_{\text{Carson}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using Bessel table.** For $\beta = 5$, $f_m = 15$ kHz → BW = 200 kHz. This matches the FCC channel allocation for commercial FM broadcasting. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — NBFM Approximation (showing its similarity to AM)

Assume $\beta \ll 1$. Then $\cos(\beta \sin x) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\beta \sin x) \approx \beta \sin x$.

$$s_{\text{NBFM}}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$$

$$s_{\text{NBFM}}(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A_c \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\, \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

Apply the product-to-sum identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s_{\text{NBFM}}(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{A_c \beta}{2}\cos\!\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m) t\right] + \frac{A_c \beta}{2}\cos\!\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m) t\right]$$

> *Crucial observation:* The lower sideband is **inverted** (negative sign) compared to AM. This phase inversion of the lower sideband is the *spectral signature* distinguishing NBFM from AM. It is also why a **phase-shift network** is needed in the Armstrong indirect FM transmitter (to flip the lower sideband's sign).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Armstrong Indirect FM Generation

The Armstrong method is the *canonical* indirect FM generator. It is "indirect" because it generates **NBFM first** (using a phase modulator, which is easy) and then converts it to WBFM by a chain of **frequency multipliers** and a **frequency mixer**.

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below traces the signal at every stage. $f_{c1}$, $f_{c2}$ are intermediate carriers; $f_{LO}$ is the local oscillator frequency.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Integrator 1 over s]
    B --> C[Phase Modulator NBFM]
    D[Crystal Oscillator fc1] --> C
    C --> E[NBFM Signal centered at fc1]
    E --> F1[Frequency Multiplier x n1]
    F1 --> G1[Signal at n1 times fc1 with deviation n1 times delta f1]
    G1 --> H1[Mixer]
    LO1[Local Oscillator fLO] --> H1
    H1 --> I1[IF signal at lower frequency]
    I1 --> F2[Frequency Multiplier x n2]
    F2 --> G2[Final WBFM at carrier fcf and deviation delta fc]
    G2 --> J[Power Amplifier and Antenna]
```

### Stage-by-Stage Mathematical Trace

| Stage | Carrier Frequency | Frequency Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| After Phase Modulator | $f_{c1}$ | $\Delta f_1$ (very small, NBFM) |
| After Multiplier $\times n_1$ | $n_1 f_{c1}$ | $n_1 \Delta f_1$ |
| After Mixer (down-conversion by $f_{LO}$) | $n_1 f_{c1} - f_{LO}$ | $n_1 \Delta f_1$ *(deviation unchanged)* |
| After Multiplier $\times n_2$ | $f_{cf} = n_2(n_1 f_{c1} - f_{LO})$ | $\Delta f = n_1 n_2 \Delta f_1$ |

> The **mixer's crucial role**: multipliers *both* shift the carrier and amplify the deviation. Without mixing, the carrier would end up at an impractically high frequency. The mixer subtracts $f_{LO}$ from the carrier while leaving the deviation untouched, allowing *independent* control of carrier and deviation.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Direct FM Generation (Varactor Modulator)

The **direct method** modulates the frequency of an LC oscillator in real time by varying the capacitance using a **varactor diode** whose junction capacitance depends on the reverse-bias voltage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Message m of t plus DC bias] --> B[Varactor Diode Cv varies with voltage]
    B --> C[LC Tank Oscillator Frequency fc plus kf m of t]
    C --> D[Buffer Amplifier]
    D --> E[Class C Power Amplifier]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter for Harmonic Suppression]
    F --> G[Transmit Antenna]
```

### Varactor Capacitance Equation

The reverse-biased varactor acts as a voltage-dependent capacitor:

$$C_v(V) = \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_j}\right)^{\gamma}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $C_0$ | Zero-bias capacitance |
| $V_R$ | Reverse-bias voltage |
| $V_j$ | Junction potential (~ 0.7 V for Si) |
| $\gamma$ | Grading coefficient (0.5 for abrupt, 1.0 for linearly graded) |

The instantaneous oscillator frequency is $f_i(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L\, C_v(t)}}$, which — when linearized for small $m(t)$ — yields the FM expression $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.

### Comparison: Direct vs Indirect FM

| Property | Direct (Varactor / VCO) | Indirect (Armstrong) |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier stability | **Poor** (depends on LC tolerance, drifts with temperature) | **Excellent** (crystal-controlled) |
| Maximum $\beta$ achievable | High (limited by varactor linearity) | Limited by multiplier chain complexity |
| Complexity of hardware | Simple | Complex (mixers, multipliers, filters) |
| Spectral purity | Susceptible to incidental AM | Very clean |
| Typical use | Two-way radios, low-cost transmitters | Broadcast FM transmitters, high-fidelity links |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Frequency Modulation. Derive the relationship between modulation index $\beta$, frequency deviation $\Delta f$ and modulating frequency $f_m$.

**Model Answer:**

Frequency Modulation is an angle modulation technique in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier varies in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal, while the carrier amplitude is held constant.

The instantaneous frequency of an FM signal is:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

The maximum departure of $f_i$ from the carrier $f_c$ is the **frequency deviation**:

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max } = k_f A_m$$

For a single-tone modulating signal of frequency $f_m$, the **modulation index** is defined as the ratio:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

This index $\beta$ is the key parameter governing the bandwidth, number of significant sidebands, and the noise performance of the FM system.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State Carson's rule for FM bandwidth. A 100 MHz carrier is frequency modulated by a 5 kHz tone. The frequency deviation is 25 kHz. Compute the bandwidth using Carson's rule.

**Model Answer:**

Carson's rule states that the approximate transmission bandwidth of an FM signal containing 98% of the total signal power is:

$$\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$$

**Given:** $f_m = 5$ kHz, $\Delta f = 25$ kHz.

**Computation:**

$$\text{BW} = 2(25 + 5) = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ kHz}$$

The modulation index is $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = 25/5 = 5$, and the bandwidth check gives $\text{BW} = 2 f_m (\beta + 1) = 2 \times 5 \times 6 = 60$ kHz. ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A 14-mark FM question almost always tests *two* of the following in combination: (a) spectral/Bessel analysis (7 marks) + (b) bandwidth/power/Armstrong design (7 marks). Students who attempt *only* the spectral part lose the 7 marks allocated to bandwidth derivation. Always attempt **both sub-parts**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a single-tone FM signal $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ with $A_c = 10$ V, $\beta = 3$, $f_c = 100$ MHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz:

  (i) List the frequencies of all spectral components that have amplitude ≥ 1% of the carrier amplitude. Use the Bessel table.
  (ii) Compute the total power and the power in the carrier component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Carson's rule, compute the bandwidth of the FM signal in part (a). Compare it with the bandwidth obtained by the strict "1% amplitude threshold" criterion. Discuss the engineering significance of any difference.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify significant sidebands using the Bessel table for $\beta = 3$.**

| $n$ | $J_n(3)$ | $\vert J_n(3) \vert$ | Sideband frequency $f_c + n f_m$ (MHz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-0.260$ | 0.260 | 100.000 |
| 1 | $+0.339$ | 0.339 | 100.005, 99.995 |
| 2 | $+0.486$ | 0.486 | 100.010, 99.990 |
| 3 | $+0.309$ | 0.309 | 100.015, 99.985 |
| 4 | $+0.132$ | 0.132 | 100.020, 99.980 |
| 5 | $+0.043$ | 0.043 | 100.025, 99.975 |
| 6 | $+0.011$ | 0.011 | 100.030, 99.970 |
| 7 | $+0.0025$ | 0.0025 | below threshold |

**Step 2 — Apply the 1% threshold.**

The carrier amplitude is $A_c J_0(3) = 10 \times 0.260 = 2.60$ V. 1% of this is $0.026$ V, which corresponds to $J_n/A_c$ ratio of $0.0026$. We require $\vert J_n(3) \vert \geq 0.01$.

Therefore, sidebands up to $n = \pm 6$ qualify. There are **13 spectral components** (1 carrier + 6 upper + 6 lower).

Sideband frequencies: $f_c \pm n f_m$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ kHz away from 100 MHz.

**Step 3 — Total power.**

The total average power of any angle-modulated signal is independent of $\beta$:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{10^2}{2} = 50 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Carrier power.**

$$P_{\text{carrier}} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} J_0^2(\beta) = 50 \times (0.260)^2 = 50 \times 0.0676 = 3.38 \text{ W}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply Carson's rule.**

$$\text{BW}_{\text{Carson}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1) = 2 \times 5 \times (3 + 1) = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1% threshold criterion.**

The highest qualifying sideband is at $n = 6$, so the bandwidth is:

$$\text{BW}_{1\%} = 2 \times 6 \times f_m = 12 \times 5 = 60 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and engineering significance.**

Carson's rule gives a *smaller* bandwidth (40 kHz) than the strict 1% rule (60 kHz). This is because:

- Carson's rule preserves **98% of total power**, not 100% — it is an engineering *approximation* suited for filter design.
- The 1% rule is a *rigorous spectral* criterion; it includes all sidebands above an arbitrary amplitude threshold.
- In practice, receivers use Carson's rule to set IF filter bandwidth, trading off some power loss (the discarded $\approx 2\%$) for spectrum efficiency.

For commercial FM broadcasting ($\beta = 5$, $f_m = 15$ kHz), Carson's rule gives 200 kHz — the exact value chosen by the FCC. This demonstrates Carson's rule's reliability in real engineering.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **Armstrong indirect method** of FM generation. Show how the carrier frequency and frequency deviation can be independently controlled using a mixer and frequency multipliers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An Armstrong FM transmitter uses a crystal oscillator at $f_{c1} = 200$ kHz to generate NBFM with deviation $\Delta f_1 = 25$ Hz. The signal is passed through a frequency multiplier of ratio $n_1 = 64$, mixed with a local oscillator at $f_{LO} = 10.0$ MHz, and then passed through a second multiplier of ratio $n_2 = 24$. Find the final carrier frequency $f_{cf}$ and the final frequency deviation $\Delta f_c$. Comment on whether this is suitable for commercial FM broadcast ($\Delta f = 75$ kHz).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Armstrong indirect FM transmitter block diagram is given in **Section 4.1** above. The explanation is:

**Why indirect?** Generating WBFM directly is hard because a wideband frequency modulator must be highly linear. Instead, the Armstrong system generates *NBFM* (where linearity requirements are relaxed) and then *transforms* it into WBFM using frequency multipliers.

**Independent control of carrier and deviation:**

- **Frequency multipliers** scale *both* the carrier and the deviation by the same factor $n$. So $f_{\text{out}} = n f_{\text{in}}$ and $\Delta f_{\text{out}} = n \Delta f_{\text{in}}$.
- **Mixers** scale the carrier by the local-oscillator frequency but leave the deviation *unchanged*. So after mixing: $f_{\text{out}} = f_{\text{in}} - f_{LO}$ and $\Delta f_{\text{out}} = \Delta f_{\text{in}}$.
- By arranging the cascade [multiplier → mixer → multiplier], the designer can independently raise the deviation to the desired value (using the multiplier ratios) while simultaneously positioning the carrier in the desired broadcast band (using the mixer subtraction).

This is the key insight the examiner wants. **Diagram must show** the order: Integrator → Phase Modulator → Multiplier 1 → Mixer → Multiplier 2.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Trace the deviation.**

- After multiplier $n_1 = 64$: $\Delta f = 64 \times 25 = 1600$ Hz.
- The mixer does *not* alter deviation: $\Delta f = 1600$ Hz.
- After multiplier $n_2 = 24$: $\Delta f_c = 24 \times 1600 = 38{,}400$ Hz $= 38.4$ kHz.

**Step 2 — Trace the carrier frequency.**

- After multiplier $n_1$: $f = 64 \times 200 \text{ kHz} = 12{,}800 \text{ kHz} = 12.8$ MHz.
- After mixing with $f_{LO} = 10.0$ MHz: $f = 12.8 - 10.0 = 2.8$ MHz.
- After multiplier $n_2 = 24$: $f_{cf} = 24 \times 2.8 \text{ MHz} = 67.2$ MHz.

**Step 3 — Result and discussion.**

$$\boxed{\;f_{cf} = 67.2 \text{ MHz}, \qquad \Delta f_c = 38.4 \text{ kHz}\;}$$

The final modulation index is $\beta = 38.4 / 15 = 2.56$ (assuming $f_m = 15$ kHz, the standard broadcast audio bandwidth).

**Discussion:** The carrier is *outside* the commercial FM band (88–108 MHz) — to bring it into band, a third up-conversion mixer stage would be required. The deviation of 38.4 kHz is *less* than the 75 kHz standard — so a *further* deviation multiplier would be needed. To match commercial broadcast specs, the designer must cascade additional mixer and multiplier stages. This is precisely the engineering trade-off that Armstrong's system handles with a carefully chosen set of intermediate frequencies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *last thing* you should read before entering the exam hall. Read it twice.

- **FM Definition:** Instantaneous frequency varies linearly with the message. $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.
- **Modulation Index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = k_f A_m / f_m$ — *dimensionless, can exceed 1*.
- **Phase Deviation:** $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$ — note the **integration** (distinguishes FM from PM).
- **Bessel Expansion:** $s(t) = A_c \sum J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$ — infinite sidebands.
- **Carrier Null:** At $\beta = 2.405$, $J_0(\beta) = 0$ — no carrier power. Often used as a calibration test.
- **Carson's Rule:** $\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ — captures 98% of total power.
- **NBFM vs WBFM:** NBFM has $\beta \ll 1$, BW $\approx 2 f_m$, spectrum resembles AM but with **inverted lower sideband**. WBFM has $\beta \gg 1$, BW is much wider.
- **Total FM Power:** $P = A_c^2 / 2$ — *constant*, independent of $\beta$. Power is *redistributed* between carrier and sidebands as $\beta$ changes.
- **Generation — Direct:** Varactor or reactance modulator — simple but unstable carrier.
- **Generation — Indirect (Armstrong):** NBFM → frequency multiplier → mixer → frequency multiplier. Carrier stable because of crystal oscillator. Carrier and deviation controlled independently.
- **Frequency Multiplier Effect:** Scales *both* carrier and deviation by factor $n$.
- **Mixer Effect:** Shifts carrier by $f_{LO}$, but deviation is *unchanged*.
- **Commercial FM Specs:** $f_c \in [88, 108]$ MHz, $\beta = 5$, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, BW = 200 kHz, $f_m(\max) = 15$ kHz.
- **Noise Advantage of FM:** Output SNR improves as $3\beta^2(\beta + 1)$ over baseband SNR — the figure of merit grows quadratically with modulation index.
- **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis:** Standard 75 µs time-constant filters boost treble at TX and cut it at RX, improving noise performance by ~13 dB.
- **KTU Exam Triggers:** "Derive the FM signal expression" → use the integration step. "Find the bandwidth" → Carson's rule. "Sketch the spectrum" → Bessel table for first 4–6 pairs. "Design an Armstrong transmitter" → trace $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ through multiplier/mixer stages.

> **Final Mantra:** *FM trades bandwidth for noise immunity, has a constant envelope for power efficiency, and is best generated indirectly via Armstrong's elegant multiplier-mixer cascade.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superheterodyne Receivers (AM and FM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Superheterodyne Receivers (AM and FM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Superheterodyne Receiver** is a radio receiver architecture that employs **frequency mixing (heterodyning)** to convert the incoming Radio Frequency (RF) signal to a fixed, lower **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** signal before final demodulation. This conversion is achieved by mixing the RF signal with a locally generated signal from a **Local Oscillator (LO)** inside a non-linear device called a **Mixer**. The principle of **frequency translation** preserves the original modulation envelope while shifting the carrier to a convenient, stable frequency for high-gain amplification and selective filtering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Superheterodyne receiver uses the principle of heterodyning — combining two frequencies to produce sum and difference frequencies — to convert any incoming RF signal to a standard Intermediate Frequency (IF), thereby simplifying the design of high-selectivity, high-sensitivity receivers for both AM and FM broadcast systems.

## 1.2 Standard IF Frequencies (Industry & KTU Standard)

| Modulation | Standard IF | Region/Band |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AM Broadcast** | **455 kHz** | MW / SW (530 kHz – 30 MHz) |
| **FM Broadcast** | **10.7 MHz** | VHF Band II (88 MHz – 108 MHz) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Universal Translator" Analogy:** Imagine you are in a room where 100 people are simultaneously speaking 100 different languages, but you only understand **English**. You hire one translator (the **Mixer**) who listens to each speaker and instantly converts *whatever* language they speak into English at a fixed voice pitch (the **IF**). Your ears and brain (the **IF amplifier + demodulator**) only ever need to process English — never the original 100 languages. That is exactly what a superheterodyne receiver does: instead of trying to build 100 different amplifiers tuned to 100 different RF stations, it converts **every station** to one fixed, easy-to-handle frequency.

## 1.4 Why Superheterodyne? — The Motivation

- **Tuned Radio Frequency (TRF)** receivers of the early 1900s required multiple LC stages to be tuned simultaneously across the entire band — practically impossible with mechanical ganged capacitors.
- **Heterodyning** solves this: only the **antenna stage + local oscillator** tune to the RF, while the high-gain IF stages remain **permanently fixed** at one frequency.
- Result: **Superior selectivity, sensitivity, and stability** at a low cost.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"Why superheterodyne?"*, the answer must contain three keywords — **(1) Fixed IF for easy filtering, (2) High gain at IF without instability, (3) Better selectivity using sharp IF filters.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Superheterodyne Receiver

The complete signal flow consists of **five functional blocks**:

1. **RF Stage** — Antenna + RF Amplifier + RF Tuned Circuit (selects desired station)
2. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** — Performs frequency translation
3. **IF Stage** — Fixed-tuned high-gain amplifier chain (most of receiver gain occurs here)
4. **Detector / Demodulator** — Recovers original audio/baseband
5. **Audio Amplifier + Loudspeaker** — Reproduction

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

Let the desired RF carrier be $f_{RF}$ and the local oscillator frequency be $f_{LO}$. The mixer output contains sum and difference frequencies:

$$f_{out} = f_{RF} \pm f_{LO}$$

To produce a fixed IF, the LO is designed so that:

$$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} \quad \text{(Superheterodyne, high-side injection)}$$

OR

$$f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF} \quad \text{(Low-side injection)}$$

The **difference frequency** is selected by the IF filter:

$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-side injection** is preferred in commercial broadcast receivers because it gives better **image frequency rejection** due to the larger frequency separation between the desired RF and the image.

## 2.3 The Image Frequency Problem (CRITICAL KTU CONCEPT)

An unwanted signal located at the **image frequency** $f_{IM}$ can also mix with the LO to produce the same IF:

$$f_{IM} = f_{RF} + 2 \cdot f_{IF} \quad \text{(for high-side injection)}$$

This image signal must be rejected **before** the mixer by the **RF tuned circuit (Preselector)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials**. Memorize every one of these.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | LO frequency (high-side) | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 2 | LO frequency (low-side) | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Intermediate frequency | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Hz |
| 4 | Image frequency (high-side) | $f_{IM} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Image frequency (low-side) | $f_{IM} = f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Image rejection ratio | $\text{IRR} = \alpha_{RF}^2$ | Numeric (ratio) |
| 7 | Tracking error | $\Delta f = f_{desired} - f_{actual}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Selectivity (Q-factor) | $Q = \dfrac{f_c}{BW}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Mixer conversion gain | $G_c = \dfrac{v_{IF}}{v_{RF} \cdot v_{LO}}$ | S (siemens) approx |
| 10 | AM sensitivity (typical) | $\approx \mathbf{1 \, \mu V}$ | Field standard |
| 11 | FM sensitivity (typical) | $\approx \mathbf{0.5 \, \mu V}$ | Field standard |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often write $f_{IM} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$. **This is wrong!** The image is **twice the IF away** from the desired RF, NOT one IF away. The IF itself sits *between* $f_{RF}$ and $f_{IM}$ in the spectrum.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used in **every commercial AM/FM radio**, television receivers, mobile phones, satellite transponders, radar sets, and spectrum analyzers.
- Enables **multi-octave reception** using only narrow-band IF filters.
- Foundational to modern **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** front-ends.
- Critical for **avionics, marine communications, and military electronic warfare** systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mixer Output Frequencies (Heterodyning)

Let the RF input signal be $v_{RF}(t) = A_{RF} \cos(2\pi f_{RF} t)$ and the LO signal be $v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$.

The mixer is a non-linear device whose output contains the product of inputs. Applying the trigonometric identity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{mixer}(t) &= A_{RF} \cdot A_{LO} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{RF} t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_{RF} A_{LO}}{2} \Big[ \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_{RF})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_{RF})t\big) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **IF filter** selects only the difference term:

$$v_{IF}(t) = \frac{A_{RF} A_{LO}}{2} \cos\big(2\pi f_{IF} t\big)$$

This proves that **amplitude modulation envelope** (sidebands) riding on $f_{RF}$ is preserved on $f_{IF}$ — only the carrier shifts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Image Frequency Expression

By definition, an image frequency $f_{IM}$ also satisfies:

$$f_{IM} - f_{LO} = f_{IF}$$

Substituting $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{IM} - (f_{RF} + f_{IF}) &= f_{IF} \\[4pt]
f_{IM} - f_{RF} - f_{IF} &= f_{IF} \\[4pt]
f_{IM} &= f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that the image lies at a separation of **$2 f_{IF}$** above the desired RF, symmetrically placed on the opposite side of the LO.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)

Consider a single-tuned RF stage with voltage gain factor $\alpha_{RF}$ at the desired frequency and the same gain (approx) at the image frequency. The IF stage provides identical response to both.

Define the image rejection ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR (dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{RF,\,out}}{V_{IM,\,out}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\alpha_{desired}}{\alpha_{image}}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 40 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\alpha_{desired}}{\alpha_{image}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a single-tuned circuit with quality factor $Q$ tuned to $f_{RF}$, the attenuation at offset $\Delta f = 2 f_{IF}$ is:

$$\alpha_{image} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + Q^2\left(\dfrac{2 f_{IF}}{f_{RF}} + \dfrac{2 f_{IF}}{f_{RF}}\right)^2}} \approx \frac{1}{Q \cdot \dfrac{4 f_{IF}}{f_{RF}}}$$

Hence:

$$\text{IRR (dB)} \approx 40 \log_{10}\!\left(Q \cdot \frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_{RF}}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> This proves a critical design rule: **Higher IF and higher Q-factor of RF stage $\Rightarrow$ Better image rejection.**

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (AM Receiver)

**Problem:** An AM broadcast receiver uses $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz and is tuned to a station at $f_{RF} = 1000$ kHz. Find the LO frequency, image frequency, and image separation.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{LO} &= f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1000 + 455 = 1455 \text{ kHz} \\[4pt]
f_{IM} &= f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1910 \text{ kHz} \\[4pt]
\text{Image separation} &= 2 f_{IF} = 910 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Mixing image with LO: $1910 - 1455 = 455$ kHz = $f_{IF}$ ✓ (produces spurious IF response).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (FM Receiver)

**Problem:** An FM receiver is tuned to $f_{RF} = 98$ MHz with $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz. Determine the LO frequency and image frequency.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{LO} &= f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 98 + 10.7 = 108.7 \text{ MHz} \\[4pt]
f_{IM} &= f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 98 + 2(10.7) = 119.4 \text{ MHz} \\[4pt]
\text{Image separation} &= 2 f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In FM, the large 21.4 MHz separation makes image rejection easier compared to AM (only 910 kHz separation). This is one engineering reason FM uses a **higher IF**.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix: AM vs FM Superheterodyne

| Parameter | AM Superheterodyne | FM Superheterodyne |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard IF | **455 kHz** | **10.7 MHz** |
| RF band | 530 kHz – 30 MHz | 88 – 108 MHz |
| Image separation | 910 kHz | 21.4 MHz |
| Image rejection difficulty | Harder (small separation) | Easier (large separation) |
| IF bandwidth | ~10 kHz | ~200 kHz |
| Detector used | Envelope detector / AM detector | Foster-Seeley / Ratio detector / PLL |
| De-emphasis required | No | Yes (75 µs standard) |
| AGC usage | Essential | Limited / not essential |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — AM Superheterodyne Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ANTENNA] --> B[RF TUNED CIRCUIT AND RF AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[MIXER]
    D[LOCAL OSCILLATOR] --> C
    C --> E[IF AMPLIFIER 1 AT 455 KHZ]
    E --> F[IF AMPLIFIER 2 AT 455 KHZ]
    F --> G[AM DETECTOR ENVELOPE]
    G --> H[AGC FEEDBACK TO RF AND IF STAGES]
    G --> I[AUDIO AMPLIFIER]
    I --> J[LOUDSPEAKER]
    H -.-> B
    H -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FM Superheterodyne Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ANTENNA] --> B[RF TUNED CIRCUIT AND RF AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[MIXER]
    D[LOCAL OSCILLATOR AFC] --> C
    C --> E[IF AMPLIFIER CHAIN AT 10.7 MHZ]
    E --> F[LIMITER]
    F --> G[FM DETECTOR FOSTER SEELEY OR RATIO]
    G --> H[DE EMPHASIS NETWORK 75 US]
    H --> I[AUDIO AMPLIFIER]
    I --> J[LOUDSPEAKER]
    G --> K[AFC FEEDBACK]
    K -.-> D
```

## 4.3 Frequency Spectrum Translation Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[STAGE A ANTENNA RF SPECTRUM 530 KHZ TO 30 MHZ FOR AM]
    P2[STAGE B AFTER MIXER IF SPECTRUM 455 KHZ PLUS SIDEBANDS FOR AM]
    P3[STAGE C AFTER DETECTOR AUDIO BASEBAND 0 TO 5 KHZ]

    P1 -->|HETERODYNING BY MIXER AND LO| P2
    P2 -->|DEMODULATION| P3

    style P1 fill:#fff4cc
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff
    style P3 fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Showing Image Frequency Location

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F_DES[DESIRED RF SIGNAL FRF]
    F_LO[LOCAL OSCILLATOR FLO EQUALS FRF PLUS FIF]
    F_IM[IMAGE SIGNAL FIM EQUALS FRF PLUS 2 FIF]
    F_IFMIX[MIXER OUTPUT DIFFERENCE TERM FIF]

    F_DES --> F_IFMIX
    F_LO --> F_IFMIX
    F_IM --> F_IFMIX

    style F_DES fill:#90ee90
    style F_IM fill:#ff7f7f
    style F_IFMIX fill:#ffcc66
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Both the *desired* $f_{RF}$ and the *image* $f_{IM}$ produce the **same IF** at the mixer output. Only the **RF preselector** tuned circuit (before the mixer) is capable of rejecting the image. The IF stage cannot distinguish between the two — it sees only one IF signal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define superheterodyne receiver. Why is the Intermediate Frequency (IF) chosen as a fixed value rather than variable?**

**Model Answer:**

A superheterodyne receiver is a radio receiver that uses **frequency mixing** (heterodyning) between the incoming RF signal and a locally generated oscillator signal to translate the carrier to a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**, where most of the amplification and selectivity are achieved.

**The IF is fixed because:**

1. **Stable high gain** can be designed at a single fixed frequency without risk of oscillation.
2. **Sharp, narrow-band IF filters** (ceramic or mechanical) are commercially available only at fixed IFs — e.g., **455 kHz for AM** and **10.7 MHz for FM**.
3. **Easier alignment** — only the front-end RF stage and local oscillator must track the incoming station; the IF chain is permanently factory-aligned.
4. **Better selectivity** because a fixed IF permits multi-stage cascaded bandpass filtering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark question):** A common mistake is writing *only one* reason. KTU board expects **at least three** valid justifications to secure full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is image frequency? Why is it considered the most serious spurious response in a superheterodyne receiver?**

**Model Answer:**

The **image frequency** $f_{IM}$ is an undesired RF signal that lies at a separation of $2 f_{IF}$ from the desired RF signal, on the opposite side of the Local Oscillator. Mathematically:

$$f_{IM} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} \quad \text{(high-side injection)}$$

**Why it is the most serious spurious response:**

1. **Same IF is produced** — both the desired $f_{RF}$ and the image $f_{IM}$ mix with $f_{LO}$ to produce the **identical** $f_{IF}$. The IF stage cannot differentiate.
2. **Cannot be filtered at IF** — the IF filter accepts both signals equally.
3. **Must be rejected by RF preselector alone** — placing the entire burden of rejection on a single RF tuned circuit.
4. **Strong local image stations** can completely overpower the desired signal if RF selectivity is poor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the formula and skip the "why serious" reasoning. KTU board awards marks only for the **reasoning**, not just the equation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an AM superheterodyne receiver. Mention the function of each block. State the standard IF for AM broadcast.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An AM receiver has $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz and is tuned to a station at 1200 kHz. Calculate (i) Local Oscillator frequency, (ii) Image frequency, and (iii) Image rejection ratio if the RF stage Q-factor is 60.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1) — refer figure.**

| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Antenna | Captures electromagnetic waves; converts to RF voltage |
| RF tuned circuit | Selects desired $f_{RF}$, rejects image and out-of-band signals |
| RF amplifier | Provides initial low-noise gain |
| Mixer (with LO) | Translates $f_{RF}$ to $f_{IF}$ via heterodyning |
| Local Oscillator | Generates $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ |
| IF amplifier chain | Provides bulk of gain at fixed 455 kHz |
| AM detector | Recovers audio envelope (diode + RC filter) |
| AGC | Feeds back DC to control RF/IF gain |
| Audio amplifier | Drives loudspeaker |

**Standard AM IF = 455 kHz.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz, $f_{RF} = 1200$ kHz, $Q = 60$.

**(i) Local Oscillator Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{LO} &= f_{RF} + f_{IF} \\[2pt]
&= 1200 + 455 = 1655 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Image Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{IM} &= f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} \\[2pt]
&= 1200 + 2(455) = 1200 + 910 \\[2pt]
&= 2110 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Image Rejection Ratio (approximate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR (dB)} &\approx 40 \log_{10}\!\left(Q \cdot \frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_{RF}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 40 \log_{10}\!\left(60 \cdot \frac{4 \times 455}{1200}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 40 \log_{10}\!\left(60 \cdot 1.5167\right) \\[4pt]
&= 40 \log_{10}(91.0) \\[4pt]
&= 40 \times 1.959 = 78.36 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $f_{LO} = 1655$ kHz; $f_{IM} = 2110$ kHz; $\text{IRR} \approx 78.4$ dB.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an FM superheterodyne receiver with a neat block diagram. State why a **limiter** is used before the FM detector. Give the standard FM IF.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An FM superheterodyne receiver tuned to 100 MHz has $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz. Determine (i) LO frequency, (ii) Image frequency, (iii) Comment on image rejection difficulty compared to AM.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram** (refer Section 4.2 figure).

**Working:** RF signal at 100 MHz is selected by the antenna and RF tuned circuit, amplified, then mixed with $f_{LO}$ from the local oscillator. The mixer output is at $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz. The IF amplifier chain provides high gain and sharp bandpass filtering (200 kHz BW). A **limiter** removes any residual amplitude variations, ensuring only frequency variations reach the FM detector (Foster-Seeley or ratio detector). The recovered audio is de-emphasized (75 µs), amplified, and fed to the loudspeaker. AFC feedback corrects any LO drift.

**Why a Limiter?**
- Removes **AM noise and amplitude variations** picked up during propagation.
- Ensures the FM detector sees a **constant-amplitude** signal — necessary because Foster-Seeley and ratio detectors are sensitive to amplitude changes.
- Provides **additional gain** in the saturated (limiting) mode.

**Standard FM IF = 10.7 MHz.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $f_{RF} = 100$ MHz, $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz.

**(i) LO Frequency:**

$$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 100 + 10.7 = 110.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Image Frequency:**

$$f_{IM} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 100 + 2(10.7) = 121.4 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Image Rejection Comparison:**

The image separation in FM is:

$$2 f_{IF,\,FM} = 2 \times 10.7 = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$$

Compared to AM (only 910 kHz), FM enjoys a **~23.5× larger separation** between the desired RF and the image. This makes the RF preselector task significantly easier — even a modest Q-factor provides strong attenuation at 21.4 MHz offset. Therefore, **FM receivers exhibit much better image rejection** than AM receivers using similar RF stage design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark Part B):**
> - **Block diagram must be drawn** — students lose 2–3 marks by describing blocks in text only.
> - **Limiter justification** must include both *amplitude-noise removal* AND *constant input to detector*.
> - For the numerical part, **show every algebraic step**. Marks are distributed across each sub-calculation.
> - **Do not forget units** (kHz / MHz) — KTU board explicitly deducts for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Final-Day Cram Sheet:**

- [x] **Superheterodyne = Frequency Translator** — RF $\rightarrow$ fixed IF via Mixer + LO.
- [x] **Standard IFs:** **AM = 455 kHz**, **FM = 10.7 MHz** (memorize both).
- [x] **High-side injection preferred:** $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$.
- [x] **Image Frequency Formula:** $f_{IM} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ — *twice* the IF away, NOT one IF.
- [x] **Image must be rejected by RF preselector**, NOT by IF filters.
- [x] **Higher IF $\Rightarrow$ Easier image rejection**, but lower selectivity.
- [x] **Higher Q $\Rightarrow$ Better image rejection** (IRR $\propto 40\log_{10} Q$).
- [x] **AM Detector:** Envelope detector (diode + RC).
- [x] **FM Detector:** Foster-Seeley discriminator / Ratio detector / PLL.
- [x] **Limiter** is mandatory in FM before the detector to remove AM noise.
- [x] **De-emphasis** = 75 µs standard in FM (transmitter uses pre-emphasis).
- [x] **AGC** essential in AM; **AFC** essential in FM.
- [x] **Block sequence:** Antenna $\rightarrow$ RF Amp $\rightarrow$ Mixer $\rightarrow$ IF Amp $\rightarrow$ Detector $\rightarrow$ Audio Amp $\rightarrow$ Speaker.
- [x] **Tracking error** $\Delta f$ arises from imperfect ganging of $f_{RF}$ and $f_{LO}$ tuned circuits — a 3-gang variable capacitor or varactor tuning is used.
- [x] **Double conversion superheterodyne** uses two IF stages (high 1st IF for image rejection + low 2nd IF for selectivity) — used in microwave and high-performance communication receivers.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In every Part-B answer on superheterodyne receivers, *always* include: **(1) Block diagram, (2) IF value, (3) Formula for LO and image frequency, (4) Worked numerical with units.** This covers ~95% of all marks awarded by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Process, Pulse Modulation Techniques – PAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling Process, Pulse Modulation Techniques – PAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Sampling Process — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Theorem (Shannon-Nyquist):** *A band-limited signal of finite bandwidth $B$ Hz (or $f_m$ Hz maximum frequency) can be completely reconstructed from its discrete-time samples without any loss of information, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition:*
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
> *where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component present in the original analog signal.*

The minimum theoretical sampling rate is called the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$), and any sampling frequency lower than this threshold causes **Aliasing** (irreversible spectral overlap).

### 1.2 Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM):** *An analog pulse modulation technique in which the amplitude of a periodic train of rectangular pulses is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$, while the pulse width and pulse position are held constant.*

Mathematically, a sampled PAM signal is expressed as:
$$s_{PAM}(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s) \right]$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period and $p(t)$ is the pulse shaping function.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Movie Reel":** Imagine a continuous flowing river (your analog message signal). You cannot store or transmit the whole river, but you can capture **dipperfuls of water** at regular time intervals. If you dip frequently enough (sampling rate ≥ 2× the fastest ripple), you can perfectly reconstruct the river's flow later. If you dip too slowly, two distinct ripples blur into one fake ripple (aliasing).
> 
> Now imagine instead of just water levels, you record them as **heights of wooden pegs** driven into a conveyor belt at uniform spacing. The peg heights vary, but the *spacing* between pegs and the *thickness* of each peg stays constant — this is exactly how PAM works.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate** — $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ (Hz) — minimum sampling frequency
- **Sampling Interval** — $T_s = 1/f_s$ (seconds) — time between successive samples
- **Signal-to-Aliasing Margin** — Practical systems oversample at $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $f_s = 4 f_m$
- **Voice Telephony Standard** — $f_m = 3.4 \text{ kHz}$, so $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ (used in PCM)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Sampling Process

**Step 1 — Identification of Bandwidth**
Determine the maximum frequency $f_m$ in the message signal $m(t)$. This is the *bandwidth* of the baseband signal.

**Step 2 — Selection of Sampling Frequency**
Choose $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. In practice, choose $f_s$ comfortably above this (typically $2.5 f_m$ to $4 f_m$) to allow for practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

**Step 3 — Sample Acquisition**
A switch (Sampling Switch / chopper / FET analog gate) closes momentarily every $T_s$ seconds, capturing the instantaneous amplitude of $m(t)$.

**Step 4 — Pulse Generation**
Each sample value modulates a pulse — by amplitude (PAM), width (PWM/PDM), or position (PPM).

**Step 5 — Reconstruction (at receiver)**
A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** smooths the discrete pulses back into the analog waveform.

### 2.2 Types of Sampling

| Sampling Type | Switching Pulse | Output Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Sampling (Chopper Sampling)** | A pulse train with finite pulse width $\tau$ | Pulse top *follows* the analog waveform — i.e., the pulse envelope IS the signal |
| **Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold / PAM)** | A pulse train with rectangular flat tops | Pulse amplitude is *constant* during $\tau$, equal to sample value at start of pulse |

### 2.3 The Nyquist Criterion — "Why" and "How"

- **Why $f_s \geq 2 f_m$?** Because sampling creates spectral replicas of the original spectrum centered at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \dots$. For an ideal brick-wall LPF to isolate the baseband without overlap, the replicas must be separated by at least $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
- **How is aliasing prevented?** A pre-aliasing analog LPF (anti-aliasing filter) restricts $m(t)$ to $f_m$, and the sampler operates at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following high-yield formula sheet consolidates every equation needed for ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Interval | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Number of Samples per cycle | $n = \dfrac{f_s}{f_m}$ | dimensionless integer ≥ 2 |
| 4 | Natural Sampled PAM output | $s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$ | $p(t)$ is rectangular pulse train |
| 5 | Flat-top PAM output | $s(t) = \sum m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)$ | amplitude held constant |
| 6 | Fourier series of pulse train $p(t)$ | $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$ | $C_n = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{n\tau}{T_s}\right)$ |
| 7 | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s}$ | unitless; $\tau$ = pulse width |
| 8 | Bandwidth of PAM signal | $BW_{PAM} = 2 f_m$ (for ideal) | Hz |
| 9 | Aperture Effect (flat-top droop) | $H_a(f) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$ | roll-off distortion |
| 10 | Aliasing Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - n f_s \vert$ | appears in baseband when $f_s < 2 f_m$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PAM forms the **foundational stage** for every digital communication system in existence:

- **Telephony (PCM)** — Voice is sampled at 8 kHz and quantized.
- **CD Audio (PCM Stereo)** — 44.1 kHz sampling of 20 kHz audio bandwidth.
- **Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)** — Multiple PAM signals interleaved share one channel.
- **Biological Signal Acquisition** — ECG (250 Hz), EEG (500 Hz), EMG — all use the sampling theorem.
- **Industrial IoT Sensors** — Temperature, pressure, vibration sensors use natural/flat-top sampling before ADC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Theorem (Spectral Domain Proof)

**Statement:** We prove that sampling in the time domain corresponds to periodic replication in the frequency domain.

**Step 1 — Define the Sampling Switch.**

The sampler is modeled as multiplying the continuous signal $m(t)$ by a periodic impulse train $\delta_{T_s}(t)$:

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 — Write the sampled signal.**

$$m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t)$$

Expanding the impulse train:

$$m_s(t) = m(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Use the sifting property of the impulse.**

By the sifting property, $\int m(t) \delta(t - nT_s) dt = m(nT_s)$, so:

$$m_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 4 — Move to the frequency domain via the Fourier Transform.**

Recall the multiplication-in-time → convolution-in-frequency property. Let $M(f) = \mathcal{F}\{m(t)\}$ and $\Delta(f) = \mathcal{F}\{\delta_{T_s}(t)\}$.

$$\mathcal{F}\{\delta_{T_s}(t)\} = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

Therefore:

$$M_s(f) = M(f) * \left[ \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Convolve with shifted impulses.**

Convolution with $\delta(f - k f_s)$ simply shifts the spectrum by $k f_s$:

$$M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

This is the *spectrum of the sampled signal*: it consists of $M(f)$ replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$, each scaled by $1/T_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition for non-overlapping replicas:** Adjacent replicas are centered at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \dots$ and have bandwidth $2 f_m$. They do not overlap iff $f_s - f_m \geq f_m$, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. This is the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Natural Sampling Output Spectrum

**Step 1 — Define the natural sampler.**

The continuous message $m(t)$ is multiplied by a rectangular pulse train $p(t)$ of pulse width $\tau$ and period $T_s$.

$$s_{nat}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express $p(t)$ via its Fourier Series.**

A periodic rectangular pulse train has the Fourier series:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

with

$$C_n = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n \tau}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n \tau / T_s)}{\pi n \tau / T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $m(t)$.**

$$s_{nat}(t) = m(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j 2\pi n f_s t} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, m(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform.**

Using the frequency-shift property $\mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}\} = X(f - n f_s)$:

$$S_{nat}(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

The output spectrum consists of the baseband $M(f)$ scaled by $C_0$, plus shifted copies $M(f - n f_s)$ scaled by $C_n$. The DC term and the harmonics carry the message spectrum, attenuated by the sinc envelope $\text{sinc}(n\tau / T_s)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) PAM Spectrum

**Step 1 — Define flat-top pulse.**

Each sample $m(nT_s)$ is held for duration $\tau$, producing a rectangular pulse of constant height:

$$s_{flat}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent viewpoint.**

This is equivalent to impulse-sampling $m(t)$ followed by filtering each impulse through a rectangular pulse filter $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$:

$$s_{flat}(t) = \left[ \sum_{n} m(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s) \right] * \text{rect}(t/\tau)$$

**Step 3 — Transform to frequency domain.**

$$S_{flat}(f) = M_s(f) \cdot H(f)$$

where

$$H(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f \tau) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $M_s(f)$.**

$$S_{flat}(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} H(f) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 5 — The Aperture Effect.**

The factor $H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ introduces **droop** (high-frequency attenuation) into the baseband component $M(f)$. At $f = f_m$:

$$H_a(f_m) = \frac{\sin(\pi f_m \tau)}{\pi f_m \tau}$$

This is the **Aperture Effect** — corrected at the receiver by an **equalizer** with gain $1/H_a(f)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A signal $m(t)$ has spectral content from $100 \text{ Hz}$ to $4 \text{ kHz}$. Compute (i) Nyquist rate, (ii) Nyquist interval, (iii) minimum sampling frequency, (iv) bandwidth of the resulting PAM signal.

**Solution:**

Given: $f_m = 4 \text{ kHz}$ (highest frequency component).

(i) **Nyquist Rate:**

$$f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

(ii) **Nyquist Interval:**

$$T_{Nyq} = \frac{1}{f_{Nyq}} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \, \mu s$$

(iii) **Minimum Sampling Frequency:**

$$f_{s, \min} = f_{Nyq} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

(iv) **PAM Bandwidth:**

For an ideal PAM system,

$$BW_{PAM} = 2 f_m = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Natural Sampling PAM System

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Message Signal m t] --> multA[Multiplier Chopper Switch]
    clkA[Pulse Train Generator f s] --> multA
    multA --> chanA[Channel]
    chanA --> recA[Receiver LPF]
    recA --> outA[Reconstructed m hat t]
    clkA -.synchronization.-> recA
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Message Signal** $m(t)$ — band-limited analog input with $f_m \le f_s / 2$.
- **Pulse Train Generator** — generates the sampling pulse train at $f_s$.
- **Multiplier / Chopper** — the actual sampling switch (FET or diode bridge).
- **Channel** — transmission medium (copper / fiber / RF link).
- **Receiver LPF** — bandwidth $f_m$, recovers the original waveform.
- **Sync line** — synchronization between TX and RX.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Flat-Top (Sample & Hold) PAM System

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcB[Message m t] --> samB[Sample and Hold Switch]
    clkB[Pulse Generator f s] --> samB
    samB --> holdB[Hold Capacitor]
    holdB --> chanB[Channel]
    chanB --> eqB[Aperture Equalizer]
    eqB --> lpfB[Reconstruction LPF]
    lpfB --> outB[Output m hat t]
```

**Why Sample-and-Hold?** It gives a flat amplitude during each pulse width $\tau$, making the PAM signal robust to channel noise and easier to quantize for PCM conversion.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Aliasing & Recovery

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Analog Message m t] --> A2[Anti Aliasing LPF cutoff f m]
    A2 --> A3[Sampler f s greater than 2 f m]
    A3 --> A4[PAM Waveform s t]
    A4 --> A5[Quantizer optional]
    A5 --> A6[Channel]
    A6 --> A7[Reconstruction LPF cutoff f m]
    A7 --> A8[Reconstructed m hat t]
    A9[f s less than 2 f m] -.CAUSES.-> A10[Aliasing Spectrum Overlap]
    A10 --> A11[Distorted Recovery]
```

---

### 4.4 Time-Domain Waveform Sketch (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TIME_DOMAIN_WAVEFORMS
        W1[Message m t - Smooth Sinusoid]
        W2[Sampling Pulse Train p t - Narrow Rectangular Pulses at f s]
        W3[Natural Sampled PAM - Pulses whose tops follow m t]
        W4[Flat Top PAM - Pulses with horizontal tops equal to sample value]
    end
    W1 --> W3
    W2 --> W3
    W1 --> W4
    W2 --> W4
```

---

### 4.5 Spectral (Frequency-Domain) Replication Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FREQUENCY_DOMAIN_REPLICATION
        F0[Baseband M f centered at 0 bandwidth 2 f m]
        F1[Replica M f minus f s centered at f s]
        F2[Replica M f minus 2 f s centered at 2 f s]
        F3[Replica M f minus 3 f s centered at 3 f s]
    end
    F0 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F4[If f s less than 2 f m then Replicas Overlap = Aliasing] -.WARNING.-> F0
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1. KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Nyquist sampling theorem states that a band-limited continuous-time signal $m(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples, provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum sampling rate $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ is called the Nyquist rate.
> 
> **Aliasing** is the distortion that occurs when a signal is sampled at a rate *below* the Nyquist rate. Spectral replicas overlap, and high-frequency components fold back into the baseband, irreversibly corrupting the signal.

---

> **[Q2. KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between natural sampling and flat-top sampling.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Feature | Natural Sampling | Flat-Top Sampling |
> |---|---|---|
> | Pulse Top Shape | Follows the analog waveform | Constant / horizontal |
> | Sample-and-Hold | No | Yes (holds sample for $\tau$) |
> | Aperture Effect | Absent | Present ($H_a(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau)$) |
> | Spectral Roll-off | Smooth (sinc-weighted) | Sharper, droop-correctable |
> | Use Case | Older systems | Modern PCM / data acquisition |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the sampling theorem. Derive the spectrum of a naturally sampled PAM signal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A signal $m(t)$ with bandwidth $5 \text{ kHz}$ is sampled at twice the Nyquist rate. Find the sampling frequency, sampling interval, and the bandwidth of the resulting PAM signal. If the pulse width is $\tau = 25 \, \mu s$, calculate the duty cycle. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Sampling Theorem:**
As stated in Section 1.1, a band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be reconstructed exactly if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Derivation of Natural Sampled PAM Spectrum:**

Let $p(t)$ be a rectangular pulse train of width $\tau$ and period $T_s = 1/f_s$:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

$$C_n = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n \tau / T_s)}{\pi n \tau / T_s}$$

The natural-sampled PAM is $s_{nat}(t) = m(t) p(t)$:

$$s_{nat}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, m(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

Fourier transforming both sides, and using the frequency-shift property:

$$\boxed{S_{nat}(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, M(f - n f_s)}$$

The baseband ($n=0$) has amplitude $C_0 = \tau/T_s$. The replicas at $n = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ are weighted by the sinc envelope $C_n = (\tau/T_s) \text{sinc}(n\tau/T_s)$. (5 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: $f_m = 5 \text{ kHz}$, $f_s = 2 \times f_{Nyq} = 2 \times 2 f_m = 4 f_m$.

$$f_s = 4 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50 \, \mu s$$

$$BW_{PAM} = 2 f_m = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

Duty Cycle:

$$D = \frac{\tau}{T_s} = \frac{25 \, \mu s}{50 \, \mu s} = 0.5 \;\; (50\%)$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of flat-top (sample-and-hold) PAM. Derive its spectrum and explain the aperture effect. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A voice signal occupies $300 \text{ Hz}$ to $3.4 \text{ kHz}$. The signal is sampled at $8 \text{ kHz}$ and pulse-width modulated with $\tau = 50 \, \mu s$. Determine (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) the duty cycle, (iii) the bandwidth of the PAM signal, and (iv) whether aliasing occurs. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Generation:** (1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for description)
A sample-and-hold circuit (FET switch + capacitor) takes an instantaneous sample of $m(t)$ at each clock pulse and holds it for $\tau$ seconds. This produces flat-top pulses whose amplitude equals the message value at the start of each pulse.

**Detection:** (1 mark)
At the receiver, a synchronous LPF of cutoff $f_m$ reconstructs the message. Because the flat-top introduces the sinc-shaped droop $H_a(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau)$, an **equalizer** with gain $1/H_a(f)$ precedes the LPF to compensate.

**Spectrum Derivation:** (3 marks)

The flat-top PAM is modeled as impulse sampling followed by rectangular pulse filtering:

$$s_{flat}(t) = \left[ \sum_n m(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s) \right] * \text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

In frequency:

$$S_{flat}(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} H(f) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

with

$$H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Aperture Effect:** (1 mark)
$H(f)$ attenuates higher baseband frequencies (droop). At $f = f_m$:

$$\text{Aperture Loss} = \frac{\sin(\pi f_m \tau)}{\pi f_m \tau}$$

This must be equalized at the receiver.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: $f_m = 3.4 \text{ kHz}$, $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$, $\tau = 50 \, \mu s$.

(i) **Nyquist Rate:**

$$f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.4 = 6.8 \text{ kHz}$$

(ii) **Sampling Interval and Duty Cycle:**

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \, \mu s$$

$$D = \frac{\tau}{T_s} = \frac{50}{125} = 0.4 \;\; (40\%)$$

(iii) **PAM Bandwidth:**

$$BW_{PAM} = 2 f_m = 6.8 \text{ kHz}$$

(iv) **Aliasing Check:**

Since $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz} > 2 f_m = 6.8 \text{ kHz}$, the condition $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is satisfied. **No aliasing occurs.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget that the **bandwidth of PAM = $2 f_m$**, NOT $f_m$ alone. The spectrum has both positive and negative frequency components, hence the factor of 2.
> - In flat-top PAM problems, students commonly omit the **equalizer block** when drawing the receiver — this loses 2 marks.
> - In aliasing questions, students must explicitly verify $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ AND show numerical values to earn full credit.
> - The **Aperture Effect** must always be mentioned alongside the flat-top spectrum — examiners specifically look for the sinc droop term.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE:**

- ⭐ **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s = 2 f_m$ — *the single most tested concept*.
- ⭐ **Nyquist Interval:** $T_s = 1/(2 f_m)$.
- ⭐ **Aliasing occurs when** $f_s < 2 f_m$; **prevented by** pre-filtering + sampling at $\geq 2 f_m$.
- ⭐ **Natural sampling** preserves pulse-top shape (no aperture effect); **Flat-top** holds constant (aperture effect present).
- ⭐ **PAM bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ (ideal).
- ⭐ **Fourier coefficients of pulse train:** $C_n = (\tau/T_s) \cdot \text{sinc}(n\tau/T_s)$.
- ⭐ **Aperture equalizer** is mandatory in flat-top PAM receivers.
- ⭐ **Duty cycle:** $D = \tau/T_s$.
- ⭐ **Sampled signal time-domain form:** $\sum_n m(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)$.
- ⭐ **Sampled signal frequency-domain form:** $(1/T_s) \sum_n M(f - n f_s)$ — spectral replication.
- ⭐ **Practical rule:** Always oversample ($f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $4 f_m$) to allow anti-aliasing filter roll-off.
- ⭐ **Voice telephony:** $f_m = 3.4 \text{ kHz}$, $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ — standard exam value.
- ⭐ **CD audio:** $f_m = 20 \text{ kHz}$, $f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz}$.
