- ⭐ **PAM is the first step in PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)** — every digital comm. system builds on it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PWM and PPM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** PWM and PPM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, also known as **Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)**, is a pulse modulation technique in which the **width (duration) of each rectangular pulse** is made proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal $m(t)$, while the **amplitude and the starting instant (position) of the pulses remain constant**.

$$s_{PWM}(t) = A \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\left(\frac{t - nT}{\tau(t)}\right)$$

where the time-varying pulse width is governed by:

$$\tau(t) = \tau_0 \left[1 + k_p \, m(t)\right]$$

- $A$ = Constant pulse amplitude (in **Volts**)
- $T$ = Sampling period (in **seconds**)
- $\tau_0$ = Unmodulated pulse width (in **seconds**)
- $k_p$ = Modulation sensitivity / PWM sensitivity constant (in **V⁻¹**)
- $m(t)$ = Normalized message signal (dimensionless, $\vert m(t) \vert \leq 1$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PWM, the parameter that carries the information is **width**, *not* amplitude. The amplitude is held fixed to a constant DC value $A$, and the position of the leading edge of every pulse coincides with the sampling instant $nT$.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Door Open-Closed" Concept
Imagine a machine operator standing at a checkpoint every minute (sampling period $T = 60$ s). The gate (the pulse) snaps open and then closes. The **height of the gate never changes** (constant amplitude $A$). However, the **time the gate stays open depends on the size of the truck passing through** (the message signal $m(t)$). A small truck → gate open for 5 s; a large truck → gate open for 25 s. A passing observer at the next checkpoint sees the **same gate height** but **different open durations** — that is PWM. In PPM, by contrast, the gate stays open for a *fixed* duration, but the *timing* of when it opens shifts to the right (or left) based on the truck size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** Many textbooks (Haykin, Lathi) call this technique **PDM (Pulse Duration Modulation)**, while older KTU reference materials frequently use **PWM**. Both refer to the same technique.

---

## 1.2 Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)** is a pulse modulation technique in which the **temporal position (time of occurrence) of each pulse** is made proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal $m(t)$, while the **amplitude and the width of the pulses remain constant**.

$$t_n = nT + k_{pp} \, m(nT)$$

- $t_n$ = Absolute time position of the $n^{th}$ pulse (in **seconds**)
- $k_{pp}$ = PPM modulation constant (in **seconds per volt**)
- The pulse width is a fixed constant $\tau_{PPM}$.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Fixed-Duration Door" Concept
Returning to the gate analogy: now the gate is **spring-loaded** to stay open for a **fixed 3 seconds** (constant width). But the **operator triggers the gate to open later or earlier** based on the truck size. A small truck triggers the gate 1 s late; a large truck triggers it 10 s late. The observer sees a **3-second window** that **slides left or right** within each 60-second cycle — that sliding is PPM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept Link:** PWM and PPM are **mathematically related**. PPM can be generated by **differentiating the PWM signal and then applying a threshold detector** (or by using a monostable multivibrator triggered on the trailing edge of PWM). This derivation is one of the most common 14-mark KTU questions.

---

## 1.3 Classification Summary

| Parameter | PWM | PPM |
|---|---|---|
| Carries Information in | Pulse Width $\tau$ | Pulse Position $t_n$ |
| Pulse Amplitude | Constant $A$ | Constant $A$ |
| Pulse Width | Varies $\tau(t) = \tau_0[1+k_p m(t)]$ | Constant $\tau_{PPM}$ |
| Pulse Position | Fixed at $nT$ | Varies $t_n = nT + k_{pp}m(nT)$ |
| Bandwidth | Higher than PAM | Higher than PWM (theoretically infinite) |
| Noise Immunity | Better than PAM | **Best** among pulse modulation |
| Generation | Comparator (INT-CMP) | PWM + Differentiator + Monostable |
| Power Efficiency | Better (constant $A$) | Best (narrow pulses) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Generation of PWM

The most common PWM generator used in KTU exam questions is the **"Integrate-and-Dump" comparator circuit** (also called the **INT-CMP method**):

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The message signal $m(t)$ is **added to a sawtooth (or ramp) carrier wave** $c(t) = (A_c/T)t$ at the input of a summing amplifier.
2. The summed signal $v(t) = m(t) + c(t)$ is fed into a **threshold comparator** with reference level $V_{ref}$.
3. The comparator output is $s_{PWM}(t) = +A$ whenever $v(t) > V_{ref}$, and $0$ otherwise.
4. As $m(t)$ rises, $v(t)$ crosses the threshold **earlier** in each cycle → the pulse becomes **wider**.
5. As $m(t)$ falls, $v(t)$ crosses the threshold **later** in each cycle → the pulse becomes **narrower**.

**Why this works:** The leading edge of the PWM pulse is fixed (it occurs when the ramp starts), but the **trailing edge moves** because the comparator switches off when $v(t)$ finally falls below $V_{ref}$ due to the sawtooth descending (or when the next cycle resets).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Derivation of PPM from PWM

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Take the time derivative of the PWM signal $s_{PWM}(t)$.
2. Differentiation of a rectangular pulse produces a **positive impulse** at the **leading edge** and a **negative impulse** at the **trailing edge**.
3. The position of the **trailing edge impulse** varies in exact synchronism with the message signal $m(t)$.
4. Apply a **half-wave rectifier** (or use a monostable multivibrator triggered on the negative-going edge) to remove the leading-edge impulses.
5. The resulting train of uniformly-spaced impulses, whose *positions* track $m(t)$, is the **PPM signal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** PPM is widely used in **optical communication (Li-Fi, IR remote control systems)**, **RF telemetry**, and **deep-space communication** (e.g., the Apollo Spacecraft telemetry used PPM for its superior noise immunity and constant-amplitude property). The constant amplitude makes PPM immune to amplitude-fading noise, which is why it is preferred over PAM and PWM in fading channels.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU Module-4 derivations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau(t) = \tau_0 [1 + k_p m(t)]$ | Instantaneous PWM pulse width | seconds |
| 2 | $t_n = nT + k_{pp} m(nT)$ | Position of $n^{th}$ PPM pulse | seconds |
| 3 | $k_p m(t) \leq 1$ | Anti-overmodulation condition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\mu_{PWM} = k_p \cdot m_{max}$ | Modulation index of PWM | dimensionless |
| 5 | $B_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{min}}$ | Minimum transmission bandwidth of PWM | Hz |
| 6 | $B_{PPM} \rightarrow \infty$ | Theoretical bandwidth of ideal impulse PPM | Hz |
| 7 | $\dfrac{S_o}{N_o}\bigg\vert_{PPM} = \dfrac{A^2 P}{2N_0 B}$ | Output SNR for PPM (matched filter) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{S_o}{N_o}\bigg\vert_{PWM} = \dfrac{A^2 P}{2N_0 B}$ | Output SNR for PWM (matched filter) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{PWM} = \dfrac{\bar{\tau}}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T \dfrac{\tau(t)}{T} dt$ | Duty cycle / Power efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{B_{PWM}}{B_{PPM}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Bandwidth ratio (PWM narrower) | dimensionless |

where $P$ is the average transmitted power, $N_0$ is the noise PSD, and $B$ is the receiver bandwidth.

## 2.4 Noise Performance and Trade-offs (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

- **PWM** is more **bandwidth-efficient** than PPM (it requires roughly half the bandwidth).
- **PPM** is the **most noise-robust** pulse modulation scheme because information is encoded in *time*, which is preserved by hard-limiting receivers.
- **PAM** is the **least noise-robust** because information is in amplitude, directly corrupted by additive noise.
- **Power efficiency ranking:** PPM > PWM > PAM (for the same average power, PPM concentrates energy into short, high-amplitude impulses).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series of a Uniform PWM Pulse Train

Consider an unmodulated pulse train with amplitude $A$, period $T$, and constant width $\tau_0$. The signal over one period is:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A, & 0 \leq t \leq \tau_0 \\ 0, & \tau_0 \leq t \leq T \end{cases}$$

The **fundamental angular frequency** is:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

The trigonometric Fourier series is:

$$s(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$

**Computing $a_0$ (DC component):**

$$a_0 = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^{\tau_0} A \, dt = \frac{2A\tau_0}{T}$$

**Computing $a_n$ (cosine coefficient):**

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^{\tau_0} A \cos(n\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

$$a_n = \frac{2A}{T} \cdot \left[\frac{\sin(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_0^{\tau_0}$$

$$a_n = \frac{2A}{T \cdot n\omega_0}\left[\sin(n\omega_0 \tau_0) - 0\right]$$

Since $n\omega_0 = 2\pi n / T$, we substitute:

$$a_n = \frac{2A}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2\pi n}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi n \tau_0}{T}\right)$$

$$\boxed{a_n = \frac{A}{\pi n}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi n \tau_0}{T}\right)}$$

**Computing $b_n$ (sine coefficient):**

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^{\tau_0} A \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

$$b_n = \frac{2A}{T}\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_0^{\tau_0}$$

$$b_n = \frac{2A}{T \cdot n\omega_0}\left[1 - \cos(n\omega_0 \tau_0)\right]$$

$$b_n = \frac{A}{\pi n}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n \tau_0}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Combined spectrum of the unmodulated pulse train:**

$$s(t) = \frac{A\tau_0}{T} + \frac{A}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\left\{\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi n \tau_0}{T}\right)\cos(n\omega_0 t) + \left[1-\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n \tau_0}{T}\right)\right]\sin(n\omega_0 t)\right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pulse train contains a **DC component** $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}$ and an **infinite set of harmonics** at $n\omega_0$. The *envelope* of these harmonics falls off as $1/n$, which means the spectrum is *not* band-limited — this is the key reason pulse modulation schemes require large transmission bandwidth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PWM Modulation Mathematics

Now let the pulse width vary as $\tau(t) = \tau_0[1 + k_p m(t)]$. The PWM signal is no longer periodic in the strict sense, but it can be modeled as a **multiplied** signal:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

where $p(t)$ is a **unit-amplitude rectangular pulse train** of period $T$ and average width $\tau_0$. The Fourier expansion of $p(t)$ is:

$$p(t) = \frac{\tau_0}{T} + \frac{2}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi\tau_0}{T}\right)\cos(n\omega_0 t)$$

When $p(t)$ is *modulated* (its width tracks $m(t)$), the spectrum **shifts** and a **baseband replica** of $m(t)$ appears around each harmonic of the pulse train. The resulting spectrum is:

$$S_{PWM}(f) = \frac{\tau_0}{T}M(f) + \frac{2}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(n\pi\tau_0/T)}{n}\left[M(f-nf_0) + M(f+nf_0)\right]$$

where $M(f)$ is the Fourier transform of $m(t)$ and $f_0 = 1/T$ is the sampling rate.

**Message recovery:** Passing $s_{PWM}(t)$ through a **low-pass filter** with cutoff slightly above the message bandwidth $W$ recovers $m(t)$, since the baseband term $\frac{\tau_0}{T}M(f)$ is isolated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Insight:** This explains why the **minimum transmission bandwidth of PWM is approximately $\dfrac{1}{\tau_{min}}$** (where $\tau_{min}$ is the *narrowest* pulse width), which can be much larger than the message bandwidth $W$. Hence PWM is **bandwidth-inefficient** compared to baseband PAM.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PPM from PWM via Differentiation

Let the PWM signal in one period $[0, T]$ be:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - t_0 - \tau(t)/2}{\tau(t)}\right)$$

where $t_0 = 0$ (leading edge at sampling instant). Differentiate with respect to time:

$$\frac{ds_{PWM}(t)}{dt} = A\delta(t) - A\delta(t - \tau(t))$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

- $A\delta(t)$ → **Positive impulse at the leading edge** (always at $t = 0$, independent of $m(t)$).
- $A\delta(t - \tau(t))$ → **Negative impulse at the trailing edge** (position varies with $m(t)$).

Setting the leading-edge impulses to zero via a **half-wave rectifier** (or by using the negative-going edge to trigger a monostable) yields:

$$s_{PPM}(t) = A'\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT - k_{pp}m(nT))$$

where $A'$ is the amplitude of the regenerated impulse and:

$$k_{pp} = k_p \tau_0$$

This completes the mathematical link between the two modulation schemes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Mandatory for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Property | PAM (Natural) | PWM | PPM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parameter that varies | Amplitude | Width | Position |
| Parameter held constant | Width, Position | Amplitude, Position | Amplitude, Width |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ (lowest) | $\approx 1/\tau_{min}$ (medium) | Theoretically $\infty$ (highest) |
| Noise Immunity | Poorest | Medium | **Best** (hard-limiting) |
| Power Efficiency | Worst (variable amplitude wastes power) | Medium | **Best** (constant-amplitude impulses) |
| Synchronization Needed | No | No | **Yes** (for demodulation) |
| SNR after matched filter | $\dfrac{A^2 P}{2N_0 B}$ | $\dfrac{A^2 P}{2N_0 B}$ | $\dfrac{A^2 P}{2N_0 B}$ (with 2x gain possible) |
| Generation Complexity | Simple (sample-and-hold) | Medium (INT-CMP) | Complex (PWM + diff + mono) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PWM Signal — Waveform in the Time Domain

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Message Signal m of t: Slowly varying sinusoid centered at zero"]
    nodeB["Sawtooth Carrier c of t: Linear ramp resetting every T seconds"]
    nodeC["Summer: v of t equals m of t plus c of t"]
    nodeD["Threshold Comparator: Reference Vref"]
    nodeE["PWM Output sPWM of t: Constant amplitude A with variable width tau of t"]
    nodeF["Low Pass Filter LPF: Cutoff near W"]
    nodeG["Recovered Message m hat of t"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The summer adds the message and sawtooth; the comparator outputs $+A$ when the sum exceeds $V_{ref}$ and $0$ otherwise. As $m(t)$ rises, the comparator switches off *later* → wider pulse. As $m(t)$ falls, it switches off *earlier* → narrower pulse. This is the canonical **INT-CMP PWM modulator** that KTU expects in answers.

---

## 4.2 PWM and PPM Waveform Comparison (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeH["Input m of t: Sinusoidal message with amplitude 1 V and frequency 1 kHz"]
    nodeI["PWM Modulator: INT CMP method producing pulses of constant A and variable tau"]
    nodeJ["PWM Waveform: Pulses of width 2T, T, 2T, T, ... as m of t varies"]
    nodeK["Differentiator Block: d by dt of sPWM t"]
    nodeL["Impulse Train at Differentiator Output: Positive impulse at leading edge, negative impulse at trailing edge"]
    nodeM["Half Wave Rectifier: Removes leading edge impulses, retains trailing edge impulses"]
    nodeN["Monostable Multivibrator: Regenerates uniform width pulses from each retained impulse"]
    nodeO["PPM Output: Pulses of constant width but variable position tn"]

    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Mapping Explanation:** The architecture flow above is a *functional* representation of the **PWM-to-PPM converter**. In a real KTU lab kit, the differentiator is a high-pass RC differentiator with time constant $\tau_{diff} \ll \tau_{min}$, the rectifier is a diode-bridge clipping circuit, and the monostable is implemented using a 555-timer IC. The output is the desired PPM waveform.

---

## 4.3 Time-Domain Waveform Sketches (Descriptive Schematic)

Although physical waveforms cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following ASCII schematic represents the canonical KTU Module-4 figure:

```
  m(t) :    /\        /\        /\
          /    \    /    \    /    \          ← Message signal (sinusoid)
         /      \  /      \  /      \
   ─────/────────\/────────\/────────\────────→ t
         |<────>|  |<───>|  |<────>|
              τ_max    τ_min   τ_max           ← Pulse widths in PWM

  PWM :   ┌──┐         ┌┐       ┌──┐
          │  │         ││       │  │           ← Constant amplitude A,
          │  │         ││       │  │             variable width τ(t)
   ───────┘  └─────────┘└───────┘  └──────────→ t
          ↑        ↑        ↑        ↑
        nT      (n+1)T   (n+2)T   (n+3)T       ← Fixed leading edges

  PPM :            ┌┐                ┌┐
                  ││                ││           ← Constant width τ_PPM,
                  ││                ││             variable position t_n
   ───────────────┘└────────────────┘└──────────→ t
                ↑           ↑
              t_n        t_(n+2)
              (shifted   (shifted
               right)     less right)
```

**Caption for the above:** The message $m(t)$ is a sinusoid. In PWM, the leading edges of all pulses align with sampling instants $nT$, but the trailing edges shift in proportion to $m(nT)$. In PPM, both width and amplitude are constant, but the *position* of each pulse slides along the time axis in proportion to $m(nT)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Pulse Width Modulation. Why is it preferred over PAM in noisy environments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a pulse modulation technique in which the **width of each rectangular pulse** is made proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal $m(t)$, while the **amplitude of the pulse remains constant** at $A$ volts and the **leading edge is fixed** at the sampling instant $nT$.

**Why preferred over PAM (1.5 marks):**

1. In PWM, the message information is carried in the **time domain (width)**, not in amplitude. Hence additive noise (which primarily corrupts amplitude) has a much smaller effect.
2. The constant-amplitude pulses allow the use of **hard-limiting receivers** that clip amplitude noise without distorting the information.
3. The **average transmitted power is constant** (since amplitude is fixed), simplifying power-amplifier design and improving power efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "PWM has variable width" and forget to mention *which two properties are held constant*. Both **amplitude and leading-edge position** must be stated. Marks lost: **1 mark** per missing property.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare PWM and PPM in terms of bandwidth, noise immunity, and synchronization requirement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PWM | PPM |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | $\approx 1/\tau_{min}$ — moderate | Theoretically infinite — largest |
| Noise Immunity | Better than PAM | **Best** of all pulse modulation schemes |
| Synchronization | Not required at the receiver (information is in width) | **Required** — receiver must know the unmodulated position to detect shifts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The synchronization point is *frequently* missed. PPM *demodulation* needs a stable reference clock to determine the position shift. Marks lost: **1 mark** for omitting synchronization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice A]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a uniform PWM pulse train, and show that it contains a baseband replica of the modulating signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 2\cos(2000\pi t)$ is used to PWM-modulate a pulse train of amplitude $A = 5$ V, sampling period $T = 0.5$ ms, and unmodulated width $\tau_0 = 0.1$ ms. If the PWM sensitivity is $k_p = 0.3$ V⁻¹, determine:
   (i) The maximum and minimum pulse widths.
   (ii) The modulation index.
   (iii) The minimum transmission bandwidth.
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Spectrum Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a uniform pulse train $p(t)$ with amplitude $A$, period $T$, and width $\tau_0$. Its Fourier series is:

$$p(t) = \frac{A\tau_0}{T} + \frac{2A}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi\tau_0}{T}\right)\cos(n\omega_0 t)$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$. When the pulse width is modulated, $p(t)$ becomes a *time-varying* function $p(t,\tau(t))$ where $\tau(t) = \tau_0[1+k_p m(t)]$. Substituting:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau_0[1+k_p m(t)]}\right) \cdot \text{periodic}(t)$$

Using the dual representation (PWM as product of message and pulse train):

$$s_{PWM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

Taking the Fourier transform and using the modulation theorem:

$$S_{PWM}(f) = M(f) * P(f)$$

Since $P(f)$ is a line spectrum at $n f_0$, the convolution places a **replica of $M(f)$** at every harmonic $nf_0$. In particular, the DC term $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}$ multiplied by $m(t)$ produces a **baseband replica** $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}m(t)$. This is recoverable using a low-pass filter.

The complete spectrum:

$$S_{PWM}(f) = \frac{A\tau_0}{T}M(f) + \frac{2A}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(n\pi\tau_0/T)}{n}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\left[M(f-nf_0) + M(f+nf_0)\right]$$

A low-pass filter with cutoff $W$ (message bandwidth) extracts the baseband term $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}M(f)$, from which $m(t)$ is recovered up to a constant scale factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the baseband replica appears:** The DC term of the pulse train acts as a "carrier" that does not shift the spectrum — multiplication by a constant $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}$ simply scales $M(f)$ in place. This is the *same mechanism* that allows synchronous detection of AM.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $m(t) = 2\cos(2000\pi t)$, $A = 5$ V, $T = 0.5$ ms $= 5\times 10^{-4}$ s, $\tau_0 = 0.1$ ms $= 10^{-4}$ s, $k_p = 0.3$ V⁻¹.

**(i) Maximum and minimum pulse widths (3 marks):**

The instantaneous pulse width is:

$$\tau(t) = \tau_0[1 + k_p m(t)] = 10^{-4}\left[1 + 0.3 \cdot 2\cos(2000\pi t)\right]$$

$$\tau(t) = 10^{-4}\left[1 + 0.6\cos(2000\pi t)\right] \text{ seconds}$$

The cosine term ranges from $-1$ to $+1$. Therefore:

$$\tau_{max} = 10^{-4}[1 + 0.6] = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.16 \text{ ms}$$

$$\tau_{min} = 10^{-4}[1 - 0.6] = 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.04 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Modulation index (2 marks):**

$$\mu_{PWM} = k_p \cdot m_{max} = 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{PWM} = 0.6 \text{ (or 60\%)}}$$

**(iii) Minimum transmission bandwidth (2 marks):**

The narrowest pulse determines the highest frequency component (Fourier analysis of a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ has its first spectral null at $f = 1/\tau$).

$$B_{min} = \frac{1}{\tau_{min}} = \frac{1}{0.4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{B_{min} = 25 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Note:** The message bandwidth itself is $W = 1$ kHz, so PWM has expanded the bandwidth by a factor of **25** in this case.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $B = 1/(2\tau_{min})$ with $B = 1/\tau_{min}$. The correct formula uses the full width $\tau$, not the half-width. Marks lost: **2 marks** for this mistake.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how PPM can be generated from a PWM signal. Derive the mathematical relationship between the PWM pulse width and the PPM pulse position. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of PPM over PWM. For a telemetry link transmitting a 4 kHz message with sampling rate $f_s = 20$ kHz and PWM constant $k_p = 0.1$ V⁻¹, compute the maximum PPM time shift when $m_{max} = 5$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Generation of PPM from PWM (7 marks):**

**Block diagram description (3.5 marks):**

```
   PWM Signal  →  Differentiator  →  Impulse Train (with ± impulses)
                                       ↓
                              Half-Wave Rectifier
                                       ↓
                          (Removes leading-edge impulses)
                                       ↓
                       Monostable Multivibrator
                                       ↓
                              PPM Output
```

**Mathematical derivation (3.5 marks):**

Let the PWM signal in the $n^{th}$ sampling interval be:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{\tau_n}\right), \quad nT \leq t \leq (n+1)T$$

where $\tau_n = \tau_0[1 + k_p m(nT)]$ is the pulse width in the $n^{th}$ interval.

Differentiating:

$$\frac{ds_{PWM}(t)}{dt} = A\delta(t - nT) - A\delta(t - nT - \tau_n)$$

The second term represents a **negative impulse at time $t = nT + \tau_n$**. Using a half-wave rectifier that retains only the negative impulses (or a monostable triggered on the trailing edge of the PWM pulse):

$$s_{PPM}(t) = A'\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta\!\left(t - nT - \tau_0[1 + k_p m(nT)]\right)$$

Defining $k_{pp} = k_p \tau_0$ and dropping the constant offset $\tau_0$ (which is absorbed into the receiver's reference clock):

$$\boxed{s_{PPM}(t) = A'\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta\!\left(t - nT - k_{pp} m(nT)\right)}$$

This shows the position shift is linearly proportional to the sampled message value.

**Part (b) — PPM vs PWM and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Advantages of PPM over PWM (3 marks):**

1. **Superior noise immunity** — information is in *time*, not width, allowing hard-limiting.
2. **Higher power efficiency** — energy is concentrated in narrow impulses.
3. **Constant-amplitude pulses** — the transmitter amplifier operates in saturation mode, giving maximum power efficiency.
4. **Less effect of amplitude-fading** in multipath channels.

**Disadvantages of PPM over PWM (1 mark):**

1. **Larger bandwidth** requirement (theoretically infinite for ideal impulses).
2. **Synchronization** is required at the receiver to know the reference position.
3. **More complex transmitter** (needs PWM + differentiator + monostable).

**Numerical Problem (3 marks):**

Given: $W = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 20$ kHz, $k_p = 0.1$ V⁻¹, $m_{max} = 5$ V.

The PPM modulation constant:

$$k_{pp} = k_p \cdot \tau_0 = k_p \cdot \frac{T}{2} = 0.1 \times \frac{1}{2 \times 20 \times 10^3}$$

$$k_{pp} = 0.1 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s/V}$$

The maximum PPM time shift:

$$\Delta t_{max} = k_{pp} \cdot m_{max} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 5$$

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{max} = 12.5 \text{ μs}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to use $\tau_0 = T/2$ as the unmodulated width. KTU typically assumes a 50% duty cycle in the unmodulated state for such problems. Marks lost: **2 marks** for assuming arbitrary $\tau_0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **PWM** = information in **width**; **PPM** = information in **position**.
- In PWM, **leading edge is fixed at $nT$**; in PPM, **position shifts by $k_{pp}m(nT)$**.
- Generation chain: **INT → CMP → PWM**; **PWM → Differentiator → Half-Wave Rectifier → Monostable → PPM**.
- The PWM spectrum is given by: $S_{PWM}(f) = \dfrac{A\tau_0}{T}M(f) + \dfrac{2A}{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sin(n\pi\tau_0/T)}{n}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}[M(f-nf_0) + M(f+nf_0)]$.
- **Minimum PWM bandwidth** = $1/\tau_{min}$ (not $1/(2\tau_{min})$).
- **PPM bandwidth is theoretically infinite** for ideal impulses.
- **Anti-overmodulation condition:** $k_p m(t) \leq 1$ for all $t$.
- **Noise-immunity ranking:** PPM > PWM > PAM.
- **Power-efficiency ranking:** PPM > PWM > PAM.
- **Synchronization required:** PPM (yes), PWM (no), PAM (no).
- **PPM modulation constant:** $k_{pp} = k_p \tau_0$.
- **Modulation index of PWM:** $\mu = k_p \cdot m_{max}$, must be $\leq 1$ to avoid overmodulation.
- **Demodulation of PWM:** Low-pass filtering of the pulse train (since the baseband term is $\frac{A\tau_0}{T}m(t)$).
- **Demodulation of PPM:** Requires a stable reference clock and a time-discriminator circuit (or a comparator against a delayed copy of the pulse train).
- **Application trivia:** PWM is used in **servo motor control, Class-D audio amplifiers, dimmer switches**; PPM is used in **IR remote controls, optical fiber communication, deep-space telemetry**.
- **KTU-favorite trick question:** "Why is PWM bandwidth-dependent on the narrowest pulse?" — because the Fourier spectrum of a rectangular pulse has its first null at $f = 1/\tau$, and the narrowest pulse dominates the highest required frequency component.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Digital Communication and Advantages, PCM Encoder and Decoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Digital Communication

**Digital Communication** is the process of transmitting information in the form of discrete, quantized symbols (binary bits, 0s and 1s) from a source to a destination over a communication channel. The continuous-time analog message signal is first converted into a discrete digital bit-stream through the operations of sampling, quantization, and encoding, then modulated onto a carrier for transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A digital communication system is one in which the information-bearing signal is represented in **discrete amplitude levels** (typically binary) and processed/transmitted as a sequence of **coded symbols** rather than continuously varying waveforms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of sending a letter through the postal service:

- **Analog Communication** is like *singing a song* over a long-distance telephone line. Any noise, distortion, or interference along the wire permanently corrupts the song — once lost, those nuances can never be recovered at the receiving end.
- **Digital Communication** is like *writing the same song's lyrics on paper* and sending the letter. Even if the paper gets a few smudges, the words can still be perfectly read because each letter is either clearly an 'A' or clearly a 'B' — there are no in-between fuzzy states.

This **discrete, regenerative nature** is the single most powerful advantage of digital communication: a noisy channel can be cleansed at every repeater, restoring the original digital waveform almost perfectly.

### 1.3 Definition of Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)

**Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the fundamental and most widely used technique for converting an analog baseband signal into a digital bit-stream. PCM performs three sequential, distinct operations on the input message:

1. **Sampling** — discretizing time (converting continuous-time → discrete-time).
2. **Quantization** — discretizing amplitude (mapping continuous amplitudes to a finite set of levels).
3. **Encoding** — translating each quantized level into a unique binary codeword.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Conversions in PCM:**
> 
> Analog Signal → (Sampling) → **PAM Signal** → (Quantization) → **Quantized PAM** → (Encoding) → **Binary PCM Bit-Stream**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Digital Communication

- **Bit Rate ($R_b$)** — bits per second (bps).
- **Baud Rate ($R_s$)** — symbols per second.
- **Channel Bandwidth ($B$)** — measured in **Hz**.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** — probability of bit error, dimensionless.
- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** — measured in **dB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Digital Communication — Block Diagram Description

A **digital communication system** consists of the following functional blocks in sequence:

- **Source (Information Source):** Generates the analog message $m(t)$ (voice, video, data).
- **Source Encoder:** Removes redundancy from the message; for analog sources, this is where the PCM encoder resides.
- **Channel Encoder:** Adds controlled redundancy (parity bits, convolutional codes) for error detection/correction.
- **Digital Modulator:** Maps the bit-stream to analog waveforms suitable for the channel (ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM).
- **Channel:** The physical medium (copper wire, optical fiber, RF wireless link) — introduces **AWGN, fading, ISI**.
- **Digital Demodulator:** Recovers the bit-stream from the received waveform.
- **Channel Decoder:** Detects and corrects errors using the added redundancy.
- **Source Decoder (PCM Decoder):** Reconstructs the analog message from the bit-stream.
- **Destination / Sink:** The final user (loudspeaker, display, storage).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of PCM Encoding

The PCM encoder performs three sub-operations in series:

**Step 1 — Sampling (Anti-Aliasing Filter → Sampler):**
- The input analog signal $m(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ is multiplied by a periodic impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$.
- Output: **PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulated) signal** $m_s(t)$ — discrete in time, continuous in amplitude.
- **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Step 2 — Quantization (Uniform/Non-Uniform Quantizer):**
- The continuous-amplitude PAM samples are rounded-off to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
- Introduces an **irreversible quantization error** $e_q = m - m_q$.
- **Uniform Quantization:** All step sizes are equal: $\Delta = (V_{pp})/L$ where $V_{pp}$ is the peak-to-peak range.
- **Non-Uniform Quantization:** Uses **companding** (A-law / $\mu$-law) to give finer resolution for weak signals and coarser resolution for loud signals — mimics human ear logarithmic response.

**Step 3 — Encoding (Binary Coder + Parallel-to-Serial Converter):**
- Each quantized level index is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary word (e.g., Natural Binary Code, Gray Code, Folded Binary Code).
- Bits are serialized into a single bit-stream for transmission.

### 2.3 PCM Decoder — Inverse Operations

The PCM decoder reverses the encoder's three steps:

- **Serial-to-Parallel + Binary Decoder:** Converts serial bit-stream back to $n$-bit parallel codewords; maps each codeword to a quantized PAM amplitude.
- **Reconstruction Filter (Low-Pass Filter):** Smooths the quantized PAM staircase into an analog waveform $\hat{m}(t)$.

### 2.4 Advantages of Digital Communication (KTU High-Yield List)

1. **Regenerative Repeaters:** Digital signals can be perfectly regenerated, eliminating cumulative noise — analog signals cannot.
2. **Immunity to Channel Noise:** Only two decision thresholds (0 or 1) are required; small noise does not flip bits.
3. **Ease of Encryption / Security:** Bit-streams are easily encrypted (AES, RSA).
4. **Error Detection & Correction:** Powerful coding (Hamming, Reed-Solomon, Turbo, LDPC) is naturally compatible.
5. **Flexibility:** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) and Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) are simpler.
6. **Integration with Digital Systems:** Direct interfacing with computers, microcontrollers, DSPs, and the internet.
7. **Long-Term Storage:** Digital signals can be stored indefinitely without degradation.
8. **Cheap Hardware:** VLSI ICs are inexpensive, reliable, and low-power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off (Examiner's Favourite):** Digital communication requires a **larger bandwidth** than analog communication for the same source — e.g., a **4 kHz** voice signal needs roughly **64 kbps** in PCM (≈ 32 kHz bandwidth), whereas analog FM needs only **~20 kHz**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to preserve table formatting.**

| **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless | $n$ = number of bits per sample |
| 2 | Quantization step size (uniform) | $\Delta = \dfrac{2 V_{pp}}{L}$ or $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{pp}$ = peak-to-peak voltage |
| 3 | Max quantization error (uniform) | $\vert e_q \vert \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) | Half the step size |
| 4 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | W (Watts) | Assumes uniform distribution |
| 5 | Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | **Uniform quantization, full-scale sinusoid** |
| 6 | Output bit rate of PCM | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bps (bits/sec) | $f_s$ = sampling frequency |
| 7 | Minimum transmission bandwidth | $B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | Hz | For NRZ line coding (baseband) |
| 8 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz | $f_m$ = max message frequency |
| 9 | Sampling period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds | — |
| 10 | A-law compander output (linear) | $y = \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$, $\vert x \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{A}$ | — | $A = 87.6$ standard (Europe) |
| 11 | $\mu$-law compander output | $y = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$ | — | $\mu = 255$ standard (USA/Japan) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCM is the **backbone of the global Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**, the **Internet (VoIP)**, **CD audio (44.1 kHz, 16-bit)**, and **digital telephony (G.711 standard: 8 kHz, 8-bit → 64 kbps)**. Without PCM, modern digital audio, mobile networks (4G/5G), and audio streaming would not exist.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Step Size for Uniform Quantizer

**Problem:** An analog signal $m(t)$ lies in the range $-V \leq m \leq +V$. It is uniformly quantized into $L = 2^n$ levels. Determine the quantization step size $\Delta$.

**Step 1:** The total amplitude range (peak-to-peak) is:

$$
V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min} = (+V) - (-V) = 2V
$$

**Step 2:** The total range is divided into $L$ equal steps. By definition of uniform quantization:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{2V}{L} = \frac{2V}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3:** The maximum quantization error is half a step (the sample is at most $\Delta/2$ away from its true value):

$$
\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{V}{2^n} = \frac{V}{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the problem states the signal range as $\pm V$ (symmetric bipolar), use $2V$. When it states the range as $0$ to $V_{max}$ (unipolar), use $V_{max}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Noise Power (Assumes Uniform Distribution)

**Step 1:** The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

**Step 2:** Mean square value of a uniformly distributed random variable on $[a, b]$ is $\sigma^2 = (b-a)^2 / 12$.

**Step 3:** Substituting $a = -\Delta/2$ and $b = +\Delta/2$:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the limits:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \frac{\Delta^3}{8} - \left(-\frac{\Delta^3}{8}\right) \right] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{4} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}}$ — this is the **average quantization noise power**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) for Sinusoidal Input

**Step 1:** Assume the input is a full-amplitude sinusoid $m(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$. Average signal power:

$$
P_s = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** For a full-scale sinusoid, peak-to-peak is $2A$, so the step size becomes:

$$
\Delta = \frac{2A}{L} = \frac{2A}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into quantization noise power:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(2A)^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2n}} = \frac{A^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute SQNR (linear):

$$
SQNR = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{A^2/2}{A^2/(3 \cdot 2^{2n})} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to decibels using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$:

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3/2) + 20 n \log_{10}(2)
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the constants:

$$
10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.7609 \approx 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.0206 \approx 6.02 \text{ dB/bit}
$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\, n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$ — **the famous 6-dB-per-bit rule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each additional bit** in the PCM quantizer **increases SQNR by exactly 6.02 dB**. This is a board-exam favourite numerical result.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Output Bit Rate of a PCM System

**Problem:** A signal with $f_m = 4$ kHz is sampled at Nyquist rate and quantized with $n = 8$ bits. Calculate the bit rate and minimum transmission bandwidth.

**Step 1:** Nyquist sampling rate: $f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8000$ samples/sec.

**Step 2:** Bit rate:

$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3:** Minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ baseband, raised-cosine with roll-off $\alpha = 0$):

$$
B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64{,}000}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the **G.711 PCM standard** used in **ISDN and VoIP telephony**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| **Parameter** | **Analog Communication** | **Digital Communication (PCM)** |
|---|---|---|
| Signal form | Continuously varying | Discrete bits (0, 1) |
| Bandwidth requirement | Lower | Higher (≈ $n \cdot f_m$) |
| Noise immunity | Poor (cumulative) | Excellent (regenerative) |
| Repeater operation | Amplifies signal + noise | Restores clean bit-stream |
| Hardware cost | Moderate | Cheaper (VLSI integration) |
| Storage | Difficult (magnetic degradation) | Easy (CD, SSD, cloud) |
| Encryption | Very difficult | Straightforward |
| SNR over long haul | Degrades with distance | Approximately constant |
| Example system | AM/FM radio broadcast | 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, PCM telephone |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Digital Communication System

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcNode[INFORMATION SOURCE] --> srcEncNode[SOURCE ENCODER]
    srcEncNode --> chEncNode[CHANNEL ENCODER]
    chEncNode --> modNode[DIGITAL MODULATOR]
    modNode --> chNode[CHANNEL WITH NOISE]
    chNode --> demodNode[DIGITAL DEMODULATOR]
    demodNode --> chDecNode[CHANNEL DECODER]
    chDecNode --> srcDecNode[SOURCE DECODER]
    srcDecNode --> dstNode[DESTINATION SINK]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — PCM Encoder

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpNode[ANALOG INPUT M OF T] --> lpfNode[ANTI ALIASING LOW PASS FILTER]
    lpfNode --> sampNode[SAMPLER]
    sampNode --> quantNode[QUANTIZER]
    quantNode --> binEncNode[BINARY ENCODER]
    binEncNode --> p2sNode[PARALLEL TO SERIAL CONVERTER]
    p2sNode --> outNode[PCM BIT STREAM OUTPUT]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — PCM Decoder

```mermaid
graph LR
    pcmInNode[PCM BIT STREAM INPUT] --> s2pNode[SERIAL TO PARALLEL CONVERTER]
    s2pNode --> binDecNode[BINARY DECODER]
    binDecNode --> reconNode[RECONSTRUCTION LOW PASS FILTER]
    reconNode --> anaOutNode[RECONSTRUCTED ANALOG OUTPUT M HAT OF T]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — End-to-End PCM Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    micNode[MICROPHONE SOURCE] --> filtA[LPF F MAX]
    filtA --> sampA[SAMPLER F S]
    sampA --> qntA[QUANTIZER L LEVELS]
    qntA --> encA[BINARY ENCODER N BITS]
    encA --> lineEnc[LINE ENCODER NRZ OR RZ]
    lineEnc --> chLink[CHANNEL COPPER OR FIBER OR RF]
    chLink --> lineDec[LINE DECODER]
    lineDec --> decB[BINARY DECODER]
    decB --> lpfB[RECONSTRUCTION LPF]
    lpfB --> speakNode[LOUDSPEAKER DESTINATION]
```

### 4.5 Functional Stages of Quantization (Mid-Rise Uniform Quantizer)

```mermaid
graph TD
    contAmpNode[CONTINUOUS AMPLITUDE PAM SAMPLE] --> cmpNode{COMPARE WITH THRESHOLDS}
    cmpNode --> lvl0[LEVEL 0 IF RANGE IS MINUS DELTA TO MINUS DELTA OVER 2]
    cmpNode --> lvl1[LEVEL 1]
    cmpNode --> lvl2[LEVEL 2]
    cmpNode --> lvlK[LEVEL L MINUS 1]
    lvl0 --> outNode[QUANTIZED PAM SAMPLE]
    lvl1 --> outNode
    lvl2 --> outNode
    lvlK --> outNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node-Safety Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`micNode`, `lpfA`, etc.) and contain no reserved keywords. All labels are clean uppercase text without markdown bold or italics, ensuring reliable rendering across all Mermaid engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. List any four advantages of digital communication over analog communication.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Noise Immunity & Regenerative Repeaters:** Digital signals are restored to clean bit form at every repeater; analog signals amplify noise cumulatively.
2. **Error Detection and Correction:** Channel coding (parity, Hamming, CRC, convolution) is easily implemented.
3. **Ease of Encryption and Security:** Bit-streams are easily encrypted using AES, RSA.
4. **Flexibility with TDM/FDM and Digital Storage:** Easy multiplexing and long-term stable storage on CD/SSD/cloud.

*(Any four valid points, 1 mark each — concise wording expected.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Define Pulse Code Modulation (PCM). What are its three functional stages?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** PCM is a method of converting an analog signal into a digital bit-stream by sampling, quantizing, and binary-encoding the message.

**Three Stages:**

1. **Sampling** — discretizes time (continuous-time → discrete-time PAM signal).
2. **Quantization** — discretizes amplitude (continuous amplitudes → finite set of $L$ levels, introduces quantization error).
3. **Encoding** — converts each quantized level into a unique $n$-bit binary codeword for transmission.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 4 — 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a PCM encoder and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A band-limited analog signal in the range $-4$ V to $+4$ V is sampled at Nyquist rate. The signal has maximum frequency $f_m = 5$ kHz and is uniformly quantized using $n = 8$ bits. Calculate: (i) sampling frequency, (ii) number of quantization levels, (iii) quantization step size, (iv) bit rate, and (v) SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (a) — Block Diagram of PCM Encoder

Refer to **Section 4.2** above for the block diagram. Explanation of each block:

- **Anti-Aliasing LPF:** Restricts the input $m(t)$ to bandwidth $f_m$ to satisfy the sampling theorem and prevent spectral aliasing.
- **Sampler:** Multiplies the bandlimited message by a periodic impulse train of frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$, producing a discrete-time PAM signal.
- **Quantizer:** Maps each PAM sample amplitude to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, producing quantized PAM. Introduces irreversible quantization error.
- **Binary Encoder:** Assigns a unique $n$-bit binary word (typically Natural Binary Code) to each of the $L$ levels.
- **Parallel-to-Serial Converter:** Serializes the $n$ parallel bits into a single bit-stream suitable for transmission over a single channel.

*(5 blocks × 1.4 marks ≈ 7 marks with neat block diagram and 2-line explanation per block.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $V_{min} = -4$ V, $V_{max} = +4$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits.

**(i) Sampling frequency (Nyquist rate):**

$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Number of quantization levels:**

$$
L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}
$$

**(iii) Quantization step size:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L} = \frac{4 - (-4)}{256} = \frac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V} = 31.25 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iv) Output bit rate:**

$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 10{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(v) Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio:**

Using the standard formula (full-scale sinusoid assumption):

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the three functional stages of PCM with the help of neat waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare analog and digital communication systems. An audio signal has bandwidth 20 kHz. It is sampled at 1.25 times the Nyquist rate and quantized into 1024 levels. Find the bit rate and minimum transmission bandwidth using NRZ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (a) — Three Functional Stages

**Stage 1 — Sampling:** The continuous-time analog message $m(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train $\sum_k \delta(t - kT_s)$ of period $T_s = 1/f_s$. Output is PAM signal $m_s(t)$ — continuous amplitude, discrete time. **Waveform:** impulses whose heights follow the input envelope.

**Stage 2 — Quantization:** Each PAM sample is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels. The quantizer characteristic is a staircase non-linear function. **Waveform:** PAM pulses take only $L$ distinct heights; the "staircase error" between actual and quantized is the quantization noise.

**Stage 3 — Encoding:** Each quantized level index is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword (e.g., 0 V → `000`, $\Delta$ → `001`, …). The binary sequence is the final PCM output. **Waveform:** a digital NRZ bit-stream whose pattern mirrors the quantized PAM.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (b) — Comparison + Numerical

**Comparison:** (4 key points, 1.5 marks each)

| **Analog** | **Digital** |
|---|---|
| Continuously varying amplitudes | Discrete binary levels |
| Cannot eliminate accumulated noise | Regenerated at every repeater |
| Lower bandwidth required | Higher bandwidth (≈ $n \cdot f_m$) |
| Difficult to encrypt and store | Easy to encrypt, store, and process |

**Numerical:**

**Given:** $B_{audio} = f_m = 20$ kHz, sampling at $f_s = 1.25 \times 2 f_m = 1.25 \times 40 = 50$ kHz, $L = 1024$.

**Step 1 — Bits per sample:**

$$
n = \log_2(L) = \log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ bits/sample}
$$

**Step 2 — Bit rate:**

$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 10 \times 50{,}000 = 500{,}000 \text{ bps} = 500 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ baseband, Nyquist criterion):**

$$
B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{500{,}000}{2} = 250 \text{ kHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $V_{pp}$ with $V$:** When the range is $\pm V$, the total peak-to-peak range is $2V$, not $V$. This single mistake shifts $\Delta$ by a factor of 2 and is worth **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1.76$ dB offset:** Writing $SQNR = 6.02\, n$ alone loses **1 mark**; the correct form is $6.02\, n + 1.76$ dB.
> 3. **Mixing $R_b$ and $B$:** $R_b$ is in **bps**; $B$ is in **Hz**. Conflating them is a classic unit-mismatch error.
> 4. **Sampling below Nyquist:** Always write the inequality $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, never just $f_s = 2 f_m$ unless explicitly told to sample at Nyquist rate.
> 5. **Block diagrams:** Omitting the **Anti-Aliasing Filter** before the sampler loses 1 mark — it is mandatory in KTU marking schemes.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Digital communication** = transmission of **discrete symbols (bits)**; PCM is the most common **source-encoding** method.
- **PCM three steps:** **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding** (irreversible at the quantization stage).
- **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — non-negotiable; aliasing otherwise.
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$, where $n$ = bits per sample.
- **Step size (uniform):** $\Delta = (V_{max} - V_{min})/L$.
- **Maximum quantization error:** $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta / 2$.
- **Quantization noise power:** $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- **6-dB-per-bit rule:** $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\, n + 1.76$ dB (uniform, full-scale sinusoid).
- **Output bit rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$.
- **Minimum transmission BW (NRZ):** $B_{min} = R_b/2$.
- **Standard PCM telephony (G.711):** $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits → $R_b = 64$ kbps.
- **Standard CD audio:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, $n = 16$ bits → $R_b = 705.6$ kbps per mono channel.
- **Companding** (A-law: $A = 87.6$; $\mu$-law: $\mu = 255$) improves perceptual SQNR for speech by compressing dynamic range before quantization.
- **Key advantages to memorize:** regenerative repeaters, noise immunity, error correction, encryption, TDM/FDM ease, low-cost VLSI.
- **Key disadvantage:** requires **larger bandwidth** than analog for the same source — almost always a board-exam question.
- **PCM decoder sequence:** Serial-to-Parallel → Binary Decoder → Reconstruction LPF (the **exact reverse** of the encoder).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
