topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of Data Communication, Digital Modulation Schemes – ASK # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Data Communication — Foundational Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition** > **Data Communication** is the process of transferring digital information (binary data) from one point to another through a transmission medium, using a well-defined set of rules called **protocols**. The data is represented as discrete symbols (typically bits `0` and `1`) and is converted into a form suitable for propagation across the channel. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, data communication is treated as the backbone of every modern embedded and biomedical system, from wearable ECG monitors to satellite telemetry. The subject **PBMRT504 – Signal Processing & Communication** emphasizes both the **physical-layer signal generation** and the **application layer deployment** of these systems. ### Components of a Data Communication System A generic link consists of **five canonical blocks**: | # | Block | Function | |---|-------|----------| | 1 | **Source** | Generates the message (sensor, voice, video, biosignal). | | 2 | **Transmitter** | Converts source bits into a transmittable signal (modulation + coding). | | 3 | **Channel** | Physical medium (copper, fiber, RF, optical, biological tissue). | | 4 | **Receiver** | Recovers original bits from the corrupted received signal (demodulation). | | 5 | **Destination** | Sink of the recovered information (display, processor, actuator). | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **modulator** (within the transmitter) is the focus of this module. It maps a low-frequency baseband signal (containing the data) onto a high-frequency carrier so that the signal can propagate efficiently through the channel. --- ## 1.2 Applications of Data Communication > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight** — These applications are frequently asked as **2-mark / 3-mark short questions** in ESE. ### 1.2.1 Biomedical & Healthcare - **Telemedicine** — remote patient monitoring using wireless body-area networks (WBAN). - **Pacemaker Telemetry** — bi-directional data exchange between implant and external programmer. - **Hospital Information Systems (HIS)** — sharing of EHR (Electronic Health Records) over LAN/WAN. - **Ambulance ECG Transmission** — real-time 12-lead ECG streaming to hospital before arrival. ### 1.2.2 Industrial & Process Control - **SCADA Systems** — Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition in power plants. - **IoT-based Smart Sensors** — Industry 4.0 data acquisition nodes. - **PLC Networks** — programmable logic controllers communicating over fieldbuses (Modbus, Profibus). ### 1.2.3 Consumer Electronics & Telecom - **Mobile communication** (2G/3G/4G/5G) — digital voice and data. - **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth / Zigbee** — short-range wireless personal area networks. - **Satellite Communication** — DTH, GPS, deep-space probes. ### 1.2.4 Defense & Aerospace - **Radar signal transmission**, **drone swarms**, **SONAR links** for submarines. ### 1.2.5 Instrumentation (Direct KTU Relevance) - Remote calibration of biomedical instruments. - Multi-parameter patient monitors connected to central nursing stations. - Wireless capsule endoscopy (data transmitted from inside the GI tract). > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** > Think of data communication as **sending a letter through the postal service**. The **letter (data)** is placed inside an **envelope (modulation onto a carrier)** so it can travel across the **postal network (channel)**. At the destination, the **recipient (receiver/demodulator)** opens the envelope to read the original letter. Modulation is essentially the "envelope" that protects the message during transit. --- ## 1.3 Digital Modulation — Core Concept > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** > **Digital Modulation** is the process by which one or more characteristics (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency **carrier wave** are varied in discrete steps in accordance with a digital baseband message signal. The general carrier is written as: $$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$$ A digital modulator can vary any of the **three parameters** $A_c$, $f_c$, or $\phi_c$: | Parameter Varied | Modulation Scheme | |------------------|-------------------| | **Amplitude** $A_c$ | **ASK** — Amplitude Shift Keying | | **Frequency** $f_c$ | **FSK** — Frequency Shift Keying | | **Phase** $\phi_c$ | **PSK** — Phase Shift Keying | This module specifically covers **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**. --- ## 1.4 Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) — Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition:** > **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, also called **On-Off Keying (OOK)**, is a form of amplitude modulation in which the amplitude of the sinusoidal carrier is switched between two discrete levels — typically **ON (carrier present)** to represent binary `1`, and **OFF (no carrier)** to represent binary `0`, while the **frequency and phase remain constant**. Mathematically, the ASK signal is expressed as: $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for binary } 1 \\ 0, & \text{for binary } 0 \end{cases}$$ where the binary input $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ modulates the carrier. > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy — ASK = "Light Switch Telegraphy":** > Imagine you are signaling your friend across a dark field using a **flashlight**. When the **light is ON**, you are sending a `1`. When the light is **OFF**, you are sending a `0`. Your friend, sitting far away, can clearly see the ON/OFF pattern and decode the message. The light intensity (amplitude) is what carries the data, while the bulb's color (frequency) and position (phase) stay constant. **This is exactly how ASK works in RF communication** — e.g., a garage-door opener remote and old-style optical-fiber links. ### Why ASK is Important in the KTU Curriculum - It is the **simplest** digital modulation scheme — hence the **starting point** for all higher-order schemes. - Forms the conceptual foundation for **OOK, BPSK, QAM, and OFDM subcarrier mapping**. - Widely used in **low-cost, low-power biomedical implants** and **RFID tags**. > [!WARNING] > Common KTU Student Mistake: > Students often confuse **ASK** with **AM (Analog Modulation)**. The key difference — **AM is continuous** (amplitude varies smoothly with an analog message), whereas **ASK is discrete** (amplitude takes only **two fixed values** corresponding to binary symbols). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Binary Data Representation A binary message $b(t)$ is a **baseband NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero)** waveform: $$b(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n \, p(t - nT_b)$$ where: - $a_n \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $n^{th}$ bit. - $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of duration $T_b$ (bit period). - $T_b$ is the **bit duration** in **seconds**. The **bit rate** $R_b$ (in **bits per second, bps**) is: $$R_b = \frac{1}{T_b} \text{ bps}$$ --- ## 2.2 Mathematical Model of ASK The ASK modulated signal is the **product** of the binary data $b(t)$ and the carrier $c(t)$: $$\boxed{s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$ ### Step-by-Step Logic - **Why the product?** Multiplying by a binary signal (0 or 1) either **passes** the carrier (when bit = 1) or **blocks** it (when bit = 0). This is the simplest form of amplitude switching. - **Why a high-frequency carrier?** Low-frequency baseband signals cannot propagate efficiently through antennas; the carrier translates the spectrum to a bandpass region suitable for transmission. - **Condition for proper operation:** The carrier frequency must satisfy $f_c \gg R_b$ to avoid spectral overlap. A common design rule is $f_c \geq 10 R_b$. ### Special Case: ON-OFF Keying (OOK) When $a_n \in \{0, 1\}$ (unipolar), the scheme is called **OOK**, and the modulated signal becomes: $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & a_n = 1 \\ 0, & a_n = 0 \end{cases}$$ --- ## 2.3 Power Spectral Density (PSD) of ASK The PSD of an ASK signal with equiprobable bits is: $$S_{ASK}(f) = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} \, \text{sinc}^2\!\left((f - f_c) T_b\right) + \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} \, \text{sinc}^2\!\left((f + f_c) T_b\right) + \frac{A_c^2}{4} \, \delta(f - f_c) + \frac{A_c^2}{4} \, \delta(f + f_c)$$ where $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The presence of the **discrete carrier component** (delta functions) makes ASK a **line-coded modulation** — it carries significant power in the carrier itself, which is one reason it is **power-inefficient** compared to PSK. --- ## 2.4 Bandwidth of ASK The transmission bandwidth of an ASK signal is: $$B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = \frac{2}{T_b} \text{ Hz}$$ > [!TIP] > **Intuition:** ASK uses a **double-sideband** spectrum centered on $f_c$, with each sideband extending to $R_b/2$ on either side. Hence the **null-to-null bandwidth is $2 R_b$**. --- ## 2.5 Probability of Bit Error (BER) of ASK under AWGN In an **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$, the **coherent** (synchronous) detector achieves a bit error probability of: $$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ where: - $E_b$ is the **energy per bit** = $A_c^2 T_b / 4$ for OOK. - $N_0$ is the **noise PSD** (one-sided) in **W/Hz**. - $Q(\cdot)$ is the **Gaussian Q-function**. - $\text{erfc}(\cdot)$ is the **complementary error function**. For **non-coherent (envelope) detection** of ASK: $$P_{e,\text{non-coh}} = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Coherent ASK needs **3 dB less SNR** than non-coherent detection for the same $P_e$, but it requires **phase synchronization** at the receiver — a complexity trade-off. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bookmark this table** — these formulas cover 80% of all KTU ESE numerical questions on ASK. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|-----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Carrier wave | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | | 2 | Bit rate | $R_b = 1 / T_b$ | **bits/sec (bps)** | | 3 | ASK signal | $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | | 4 | OOK signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ for `1`; $0$ for `0` | Volts | | 5 | Carrier power | $P_c = A_c^2 / 2$ | **Watts** | | 6 | Bit energy (OOK) | $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4$ | **Joules** | | 7 | Bandwidth | $B_{ASK} = 2 R_b$ | **Hz** | | 8 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b / B = 0.5$ | **bits/sec/Hz** | | 9 | Coherent BER | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | Non-coherent BER | $P_e = \frac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b / 2 N_0)$ | Dimensionless | | 11 | Carrier frequency rule | $f_c \geq 10 R_b$ | Hz | | 12 | Noise PSD (one-sided) | $N_0$ | **W/Hz** | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Utility of ASK | Domain | Application | Why ASK? | |--------|-------------|----------| | **Biomedical implants** | Pacemaker telemetry, capsule endoscopy | Simple, low-power, low data rate. | | **RFID systems** | Toll collection, inventory tags | Coherent detection unnecessary — envelope detector suffices. | | **Optical communication** | IR remote controls (TV, AC) | Light ON/OFF is literally amplitude switching. | | **Morse-code-style RF** | Garage openers, simple alarms | Cost-sensitive; no need for phase/frequency stability. | | **Modem legacy systems** | 1200-bps Bell 202 modem | Early telecommunication standard. | --- ## 2.8 Advantages and Disadvantages of ASK | Advantages | Disadvantages | |------------|---------------| | **Simplest** digital modulation to implement. | **Highly susceptible to noise** — noise directly adds to amplitude. | | Low **circuit complexity** — multiplier + filter suffice. | **Power-inefficient** — most power is wasted in the carrier. | | Suitable for **low-cost, low-data-rate** links. | **Bandwidth inefficient** — spectral efficiency only **0.5 bps/Hz**. | | Easy to detect with **envelope detector** (non-coherent). | **DC component** in the spectrum (unsuitable for AC-coupled channels). | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ASK as a Product Modulator > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** — both the modulator and the demodulator blocks are often asked. ### Step 1 — Start with the Baseband Binary Signal The incoming bit stream is represented as a **unipolar NRZ** waveform: $$b(t) = \sum_{n} a_n \, p(t - nT_b), \quad a_n \in \{0, 1\}$$ The amplitude levels are **either 0 V (for bit 0) or +V (for bit 1)**. ### Step 2 — Generate the High-Frequency Carrier A stable local oscillator (crystal-controlled) produces: $$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ ### Step 3 — Multiply Carrier by Data The transmitter contains a **balanced multiplier** (or an analog switch). Its output is: $$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) = b(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Expanding: $$\begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \sum_{n} a_n p(t - nT_b) \\ &= A_c \sum_{n} a_n \, p(t - nT_b) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpret the Result During the interval $nT_b \leq t < (n+1)T_b$: - If $a_n = 1$: $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ → a **full-amplitude sinusoid**. - If $a_n = 0$: $s(t) = 0$ → **silence** (the carrier is "switched off"). This is the **ON-OFF Keying waveform**. > [!NOTE] > The bit rate $R_b$ must be much smaller than $f_c$ to ensure the spectral lobes of the modulated signal do not overlap with adjacent channels. The **Nyquist criterion** is automatically satisfied because the data bandwidth is $R_b/2$ (NRZ baseband) and the carrier is at $f_c \gg R_b$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Coherent (Synchronous) Detection of ASK A **coherent detector** multiplies the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ by a **locally generated carrier** synchronized in frequency and phase with the transmitter, then low-pass filters. ### Step 1 — Multiplication at the Receiver Let the local oscillator be $c_{\text{LO}}(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (assuming perfect sync). Then: $$\begin{aligned} r(t) \cdot c_{\text{LO}}(t) &= \left[s(t) + n(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) \\ &= s(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Expand the Signal Term For the `1` symbol, $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$: $$\begin{aligned} s(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) &= A_c \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \\ &= \frac{A_c}{2} \left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)\right] \end{aligned}$$ using the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$. For the `0` symbol, $s(t) = 0$, the product is **zero**. ### Step 3 — Apply the Low-Pass Filter (LPF) The LPF removes the high-frequency term $\cos(4\pi f_c t)$, leaving: $$v(t) = \begin{cases} A_c / 2, & \text{bit} = 1 \\ 0, & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ This is a **DC level** proportional to the original bit. A threshold detector at $A_c/4$ then decides between `0` and `1`. ### Step 4 — Add Noise Analysis After LPF, the noise term has variance $\sigma_n^2 = N_0 B / 2 = N_0 R_b$ (for matched filter). With signal amplitude $A_c/2$ and noise variance, the SNR at the decision device is: $$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{(A_c/2)^2}{N_0 R_b} = \frac{A_c^2}{4 N_0 R_b} = \frac{E_b}{N_0}$$ since $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4 = A_c^2 / (4 R_b)$. ### Step 5 — Compute the Bit Error Probability For an equiprobable binary signal, the optimum threshold is $A_c/4$. The probability of error is the probability that the noise exceeds half the signal level: $$P_e = P(\text{noise} > A_c/4) = Q\!\left(\frac{A_c/4}{\sigma_n}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ This is the **coherent ASK BER formula** in the KTU Formula Sheet. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Non-Coherent (Envelope) Detection of ASK A non-coherent detector is **far simpler** — it does not require a synchronized local oscillator. It uses an **envelope detector** (diode + RC filter) followed by a threshold comparator. ### Step 1 — Received Signal For a `1` bit: $$r(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Envelope of $r(t)$ The envelope detector output is: $$y(t) = \sqrt{\left[A_c + n_I(t)\right]^2 + n_Q^2(t)}$$ where $n_I(t)$ and $n_Q(t)$ are the in-phase and quadrature noise components, each Gaussian with zero mean and variance $N_0 B$. - If a `1` is sent: $y(t)$ has **Rician distribution** with mean $\approx A_c$. - If a `0` is sent: $y(t)$ has **Rayleigh distribution** (no signal). ### Step 3 — Threshold Decision A threshold $\gamma$ is set (typically $A_c/2$). The BER is: $$P_e = \frac{1}{2} P(y > \gamma \vert 0) + \frac{1}{2} P(y < \gamma \vert 1) = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\gamma^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - Q\!\left(\frac{A_c - \gamma}{\sigma_n}\right)\right]$$ Optimizing $\gamma = A_c/2$ yields: $$\boxed{P_{e,\text{non-coh}} = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}\right)}$$ > [!TIP] > **Comparison Insight:** At high SNR ($E_b/N_0 \gg 1$), the non-coherent $P_e$ is about **3 dB worse** than the coherent $P_e$ — but the receiver is **much simpler** and cheaper. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem) > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem (KTU 2019 ESE Style):** > A binary message `10110010` is to be transmitted using ASK over a channel of carrier frequency $1$ MHz and bit rate $200$ kbps. The carrier amplitude is $5$ V. Sketch the ASK waveform, calculate the transmission bandwidth, and determine the bit energy. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** - Bit stream: `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0` (8 bits). - $T_b = 1 / R_b = 1 / (200 \times 10^3) = 5\ \mu s$ per bit. - Total signal duration $= 8 \times 5 = 40\ \mu s$. - $A_c = 5\ \text{V}$, $f_c = 1\ \text{MHz}$. **Step 2 — Sketch the waveform:** The waveform consists of 8 segments, each of width $5\ \mu s$. Carrier is present (5 V sinusoid at 1 MHz) for `1` and zero for `0`. **Step 3 — Bandwidth:** $$B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = 2 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 400\ \text{kHz}$$ **Step 4 — Bit energy:** $$E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} = \frac{(5)^2 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})}{4} = \frac{25 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 31.25\ \mu\text{J}$$ **Step 5 — Total transmitted energy:** $$E_{\text{total}} = 8 \times E_b = 250\ \mu\text{J}$$ (Note: the four `0` bits carry no energy; only four `1` bits contribute.) > [!NOTE] > **Final Answers:** > - Bandwidth $= 400$ kHz > - Bit energy $E_b = 31.25\ \mu$J > - Waveform: carrier ON for `1`, OFF for `0`. --- ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: ASK vs. FSK vs. PSK | Parameter | **ASK** | **FSK** | **BPSK** | |-----------|---------|---------|----------| | Parameter varied | Amplitude | Frequency | Phase | | Bandwidth | $2 R_b$ | $\geq 2 R_b$ (wider) | $2 R_b$ | | Noise immunity | Poor | Moderate | **Best** | | Power efficiency | Poor | Moderate | **Best** | | Circuit complexity | **Lowest** | Moderate | Moderate-High | | Coherent detection needed? | Optional (non-coh is common) | Optional | Yes | | Spectral efficiency | 0.5 bps/Hz | $< 0.5$ bps/Hz | 0.5 bps/Hz | | BER at same $E_b/N_0$ | Worst | Middle | **Best** | | Typical use | Remote control, RFID, optical | Modems, paging | Satellite, Wi-Fi, cellular | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ASK Transmitter Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Binary Data Source
b(t) NRZ Pulse Train"] --> nodeB["Balanced Multiplier
or Analog Switch"] nodeC["Local Oscillator
Carrier c(t) A_c cos 2 pi f_c t"] --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD["Bandpass Filter
Centered at f_c"] nodeD --> nodeE["Channel
AWGN n(t) Added"] nodeE --> nodeF["ASK Modulated Output
s(t) Transmitted to Antenna"] ``` **Interpretation:** - The **binary data source** produces the unipolar NRZ waveform. - The **local oscillator** provides the high-frequency carrier. - The **multiplier** performs the switching — this is the heart of ASK modulation. - The **bandpass filter** removes any DC offset and out-of-band harmonics generated by the switching action. --- ## 4.2 Coherent (Synchronous) ASK Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Received Signal
r(t) s(t) plus n(t)"] --> nodeB["Multiplier"] nodeC["Synchronous Local
Oscillator cos 2 pi f_c t"] --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD["Low Pass Filter
Cutoff approx R_b slash 2"] nodeD --> nodeE["Sampler
at bit rate R_b"] nodeE --> nodeF["Threshold Comparator
Threshold equals A_c divided by 4"] nodeF --> nodeG["Recovered Binary
Output b hat t"] ``` **Interpretation:** - The **local oscillator must be phase-locked** with the transmitter's carrier — this is achieved using a **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** or a **Costas loop** in practice. - The **LPF** extracts the baseband, and the **sampler + comparator** make the bit decision. --- ## 4.3 Non-Coherent (Envelope) ASK Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Received Signal
r(t) s(t) plus n(t)"] --> nodeB["Bandpass Filter
Centered at f_c"] nodeB --> nodeC["Envelope Detector
Diode plus RC"] nodeC --> nodeD["Sample and Hold
at Rate R_b"] nodeD --> nodeE["Threshold Decision
Threshold equals A_c divided by 2"] nodeE --> nodeF["Recovered Binary
Output b hat t"] ``` **Interpretation:** - **No local oscillator** is needed — this is the simplest, cheapest receiver. - The **envelope detector** outputs a DC level proportional to the carrier amplitude. - **Limitation:** Cannot distinguish phase, and is slightly noisier than the coherent detector. --- ## 4.4 ASK Modulation — Time-Domain Waveform Sketch ```mermaid graph TB subgraph timeline["Time-Domain Representation of ASK for Data 1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0"] T1["Bit 1 equals 1
Carrier ON
Five cycles of 1 MHz sinusoid"] T2["Bit 2 equals 0
Carrier OFF
Zero amplitude"] T3["Bit 3 equals 1
Carrier ON
Five cycles of 1 MHz sinusoid"] T4["Bit 4 equals 1
Carrier ON
Five cycles of 1 MHz sinusoid"] T5["Bit 5 equals 0
Carrier OFF
Zero amplitude"] T6["Bit 6 equals 0
Carrier OFF
Zero amplitude"] T7["Bit 7 equals 1
Carrier ON
Five cycles of 1 MHz sinusoid"] T8["Bit 8 equals 0
Carrier OFF
Zero amplitude"] T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7 --> T8 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each bit occupies $5\ \mu s$ (= $1 / 200$ kbps). Within each `1` bit, **5 complete cycles** of the 1 MHz carrier fit, since $5\ \mu s \times 1\ \text{MHz} = 5$ cycles. --- ## 4.5 Frequency-Domain Spectrum of ASK ```mermaid graph TB subgraph spectrum["Power Spectral Density of ASK Signal"] S1["Main Lobe at plus f_c
Width equals 2 R_b"] S2["Discrete Carrier Spike
Delta Function at plus f_c"] S3["Main Lobe at minus f_c
Width equals 2 R_b"] S4["Discrete Carrier Spike
Delta Function at minus f_c"] S1 --> S2 S3 --> S4 end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Spectral Features:** > - The ASK spectrum consists of a **continuous baseband spectrum** (sinc-shaped) translated to $\pm f_c$. > - A **discrete carrier spike** (Dirac delta) exists at $\pm f_c$ because the average value of $b(t)$ is non-zero (unipolar, mean = 0.5). > - The **null-to-null bandwidth** is $2 R_b$. > - For **BPSK** (bipolar data), the delta spikes **vanish** because the mean of $b(t)$ is zero — this is why BPSK is more power-efficient than ASK. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are short-answer, definition, or one-line derivations. Answer in **2–3 sentences** with a key equation. --- ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). Mention any two applications.** **Model Answer:** Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of a sinusoidal carrier is varied in discrete levels in accordance with a binary data signal, while frequency and phase remain constant. Mathematically: $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, where $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$. **Applications:** 1. Optical-fiber and IR remote control links (e.g., TV remotes — light ON/OFF). 2. RFID tag-to-reader communication in toll-collection systems. 3. Biomedical telemetry from low-power implants (e.g., pacemakers). --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **What is meant by ON-OFF Keying (OOK)? How is it related to ASK?** **Model Answer:** On-Off Keying (OOK) is a special binary form of ASK in which the carrier is either **fully transmitted** (representing binary `1`) or **completely suppressed** (representing binary `0`). In OOK, the modulated signal is: $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit} = 1 \\ 0, & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ **Relationship:** OOK is the **binary special case** of ASK. In general, ASK can have **M-level** amplitude variations (e.g., 4-ASK with four amplitude levels), but OOK is restricted to just two levels (ON and OFF). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Internal Choice Format > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] #### **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of ASK signal. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Generation:** A binary data stream $b(t)$ (unipolar NRZ) is multiplied with a high-frequency carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a balanced modulator. The output is the ASK signal: $$s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Block diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["Binary Data b(t)"] --> Q2["Multiplier"] Q3["Carrier c(t)"] --> Q2 Q2 --> Q4["Bandpass Filter"] Q4 --> Q5["ASK Output s(t)"] ``` **Detection (Coherent):** The received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ synchronized with the transmitter. The product contains a low-frequency term $A_c b(t)/2$ and a high-frequency term at $2f_c$. A low-pass filter removes the $2f_c$ component, leaving $v(t) = A_c b(t)/2$. A sampler and threshold detector recover the binary data. #### **(b) For an ASK system operating over an AWGN channel, derive the expression for the probability of error. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The received signal in an AWGN channel is: $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 0, 1$$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean white Gaussian noise with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. After coherent demodulation, the decision variable at the sampler output is: $$v = \begin{cases} +A_c/2 + n_0, & \text{bit} = 1 \\ 0 + n_0, & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ where $n_0$ is the noise sample with variance $\sigma_n^2 = N_0 / (2 T_b)$. The optimal threshold is $A_c/4$. The probability of error is the probability that the noise pushes the decision variable across the threshold: $$P_e = P(v > A_c/4 \vert 0)\,P(0) + P(v < A_c/4 \vert 1)\,P(1)$$ Assuming equiprobable bits $P(0) = P(1) = 1/2$: $$P_e = \frac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\frac{A_c/4}{\sigma_n}\right) + \frac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\frac{A_c/4}{\sigma_n}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{A_c/4}{\sigma_n}\right)$$ Substituting $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4$ and $\sigma_n^2 = N_0 / (2 T_b)$: $$\frac{A_c/4}{\sigma_n} = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU Model] #### **(a) Explain the applications of data communication in biomedical and industrial fields. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Biomedical Applications:** 1. **Telemedicine:** Real-time transmission of patient vitals (ECG, SpO2, BP) from remote locations to hospitals. 2. **Implantable devices:** Pacemakers, cochlear implants, and insulin pumps communicate wirelessly with external programmers using ASK/FSK. 3. **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** Patient records, lab reports, and imaging data shared across departments over LAN/WAN. 4. **Ambulance telemetry:** 12-lead ECG streamed to the receiving hospital during patient transit, allowing pre-arrival diagnosis. **Industrial Applications:** 1. **SCADA systems:** Used in power plants and water-treatment facilities for remote monitoring and control. 2. **IoT-based smart sensors:** Industry 4.0 deployments where temperature, pressure, and humidity sensors transmit data wirelessly. 3. **PLC networks:** Modbus, Profibus, and CAN protocols used in factory automation. 4. **Predictive maintenance:** Vibration and current sensors on motors send data to cloud servers for ML-based failure prediction. **Conclusion:** Data communication forms the nervous system of both modern healthcare delivery and Industry 4.0, enabling real-time, distributed decision-making. #### **(b) For the binary data `10110010` transmitted using ASK with carrier frequency 500 kHz and bit rate 50 kbps, calculate the (i) bandwidth, (ii) bit duration, and (iii) bit energy if carrier amplitude is 4 V. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Bit stream: `10110010` (8 bits). - $f_c = 500$ kHz, $R_b = 50$ kbps, $A_c = 4$ V. **(i) Bandwidth:** $$B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = 2 \times 50 \times 10^3 = 100\ \text{kHz}$$ **(ii) Bit Duration:** $$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^3} = 20\ \mu\text{s}$$ **Total signal duration** $= 8 \times T_b = 160\ \mu\text{s}$. **Number of carrier cycles per `1` bit** $= f_c \times T_b = 500 \times 10^3 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 10$ cycles. **(iii) Bit Energy:** $$E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} = \frac{(4)^2 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = \frac{16 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 80\ \mu\text{J}$$ **Total transmitted energy** (only `1` bits contribute; there are four `1`s): $$E_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 80\ \mu\text{J} = 320\ \mu\text{J}$$ **Carrier power:** $$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8\ \text{W}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on ASK questions:** > 1. **Confusing the bandwidth formula:** Students write $B = R_b$ instead of $B = 2 R_b$. The factor of 2 arises because ASK is a **double-sideband** modulation. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/4 in $E_b$:** For OOK, the energy is $A_c^2 T_b / 4$, not $A_c^2 T_b / 2$. (–1 mark) > 3. **Drawing the ASK waveform as bipolar:** OOK is **unipolar** (carrier or zero), not $\pm A_c$. (–1 to –2 marks) > 4. **Mixing up ASK with AM:** ASK is **discrete** (two levels), AM is **continuous**. (–1 mark) > 5. **Not mentioning phase/frequency constancy:** A complete ASK definition must state that **only amplitude varies** while $f_c$ and $\phi_c$ are constant. (–1 mark) > 6. **Confusing coherent vs. non-coherent detection:** Many students write the wrong $P_e$ formula. Coherent: $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$; Non-coherent: $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. (–2 marks) > 7. **No diagrams:** KTU examiners **expect** at least one block diagram and one waveform sketch. Skipping them costs 1–2 marks per question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 (ASK)** ### A. Data Communication Basics - Data communication involves **source → transmitter → channel → receiver → destination**. - Modulation is essential for **efficient propagation** (small antenna, low noise). - Applications span **biomedical, industrial, telecom, defense, instrumentation**. ### B. Digital Modulation Family - **ASK** → amplitude varies. - **FSK** → frequency varies. - **PSK** → phase varies. - All share the general form: $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))$. ### C. ASK Key Facts - ASK = **ON-OFF Keying (OOK)** in its binary form. - Mathematical form: $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$. - **Bandwidth:** $B_{ASK} = 2 R_b$. - **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = 0.5$ bps/Hz. - **Bit energy (OOK):** $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4$. - **Coherent BER:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. - **Non-coherent BER:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. - **Carrier spike** in the spectrum → power-inefficient. ### D. Generation & Detection - **Generation:** Binary data × Carrier (balanced multiplier). - **Coherent detection:** Multiply by synchronized LO + LPF + threshold. - **Non-coherent detection:** Envelope detector + sample + threshold. - Coherent is **3 dB better** in SNR but more complex. ### E. Pros & Cons - ✅ Simple, cheap, low-power. - ❌ Noise-sensitive, bandwidth-inefficient, power-inefficient. ### F. Common KTU Mistakes - Forgetting factor of 2 in bandwidth. - Drawing bipolar waveform for OOK. - Confusing $E_b = A_c^2 T_b/4$ with $A_c^2 T_b/2$. - Confusing coherent vs. non-coherent $P_e$. ### G. Memory Hooks (KTU Mnemonics) - **"ASK = Amplitude Switch Key"** → only amplitude changes. - **"OOK = On-Off = 0/1 = light switch"** → simple, low-power, common in remotes. - **"Bandwidth = 2 R_b"** → double sideband, just like AM. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** > For a 14-mark ASK question, always include: > 1. Definition with equation (2 marks). > 2. Block diagram of modulator (2 marks). > 3. Block diagram of demodulator (2 marks). > 4. Waveform sketch (2 marks). > 5. Derivation of $P_e$ **or** bandwidth and energy calculation (4 marks). > 6. Two applications (2 marks).

topics.title: PSK, Suggestions on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT504 - SIGNAL PROCESSING& COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** PSK, Suggestions on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Phase Shift Keying (PSK) — Formal Definition

**Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a digital modulation scheme in which the **phase** of a constant-amplitude carrier signal is varied discretely in accordance with the digital baseband message signal, while its **amplitude and frequency are held constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> PSK is a *constant-envelope* digital modulation technique where information is encoded in the phase transitions of the carrier. The general form of the PSK signal is:
>
> $$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{T}} \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i), \quad 0 \le t \le T$$
>
> where $\phi_i$ is one of $M$ discrete phase states and $E$ is the symbol energy.

The most commonly studied variants in the KTU syllabus are:

| Variant | M-ary Order | Bits per Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **BPSK** (Binary PSK) | $M = 2$ | 1 bit/symbol |
| **QPSK** (Quadrature PSK) | $M = 4$ | 2 bits/symbol |
| **8-PSK** | $M = 8$ | 3 bits/symbol |
| **M-ary PSK** | $M$ | $\log_2 M$ bits/symbol |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Clock Hands of Information

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Compass on a Hill":**
> Imagine a compass needle on top of a hill. The wind (carrier wave) always blows with the **same strength (amplitude)** and from the **same direction (frequency)**. But every second, the compass needle is *rotated* to point in one of two (or four, or eight) allowed directions. A distant observer only needs to know *where the needle points* — not how hard the wind blows — to read the message. Each "pointing direction" is a **phase state**, and reading the direction recovers the original digital bits.

This is exactly what PSK does: it changes the **phase of a sinusoidal carrier** to encode binary data, leaving amplitude untouched. The receiver measures the angle of the received vector to recover the bits.

## 1.3 Why PSK is Bandwidth- and Power-Efficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Constant envelope** → immune to non-linear distortion → can use efficient Class-C amplifiers.
> - **Orthogonal signaling** (e.g., QPSK) → doubles the data rate **without doubling bandwidth**.
> - **Reference standard:** BPSK achieves the **lowest bit error rate** of all PSK schemes for a given $E_b/N_0$, making it the **theoretical benchmark** for power efficiency.

## 1.4 Project Topics — High-Level Definition

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a project component in PBMRT504. **Suggestions on Project Topics** refers to the curated list of feasible B.Tech-level final-year project themes that integrate **Signal Processing** and **Communication** theory with hands-on implementation (MATLAB/Python/Simulink/hardware).

These project topics are designed to map directly to the **Course Outcomes (COs)** of PBMRT504 — particularly CO4 (Design of communication systems) and CO5 (Modern communication trends).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General M-ary PSK Signal Representation

For an **M-ary PSK** scheme, the transmitted signal during the $k$-th symbol interval is:

$$s_k(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_k), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

where:
- $A_c$ = carrier amplitude
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency
- $\theta_k \in \left\{0, \frac{2\pi}{M}, \frac{4\pi}{M}, \ldots, \frac{2\pi(M-1)}{M}\right\}$ = phase state for symbol $k$
- $T_s$ = symbol duration

Using the trigonometric identity, $s_k(t)$ can be **decomposed** into its in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components:

$$s_k(t) = \underbrace{A_c \cos(\theta_k)}_{\text{I-component}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \underbrace{A_c \sin(\theta_k)}_{\text{Q-component}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

This leads directly to the **constellation diagram** representation on the I-Q plane.

## 2.2 BPSK — Binary Phase Shift Keying

In **BPSK**, only two phase states are used:
- Phase $0$ for binary '**0**' (or '**+1**')
- Phase $\pi$ for binary '**1**' (or '**−1**')

The two transmitted signals are:

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \text{for bit } 0$$

$$s_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \text{for bit } 1$$

**Key Properties of BPSK:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The two signals are **antipodal** (opposite in sign).
> - BPSK is the **most power-efficient** PSK scheme.
> - Minimum Euclidean distance between constellation points is **maximum**.
> - Bandwidth efficiency: $\eta = 0.5 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$ (with raised-cosine filtering $\alpha = 0$).

## 2.3 QPSK — Quadrature Phase Shift Keying

In **QPSK**, the carrier phase takes one of **four equally spaced values**:

$$\theta_k \in \left\{ \frac{\pi}{4},\ \frac{3\pi}{4},\ \frac{5\pi}{4},\ \frac{7\pi}{4} \right\}$$

A QPSK symbol encodes **2 bits**. The four possible signals are:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + (2k-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit-to-Phase Mapping (Gray coding preferred):**
> 
> | Dibit | Phase |
> |---|---|
> | 00 | $\pi/4$ |
> | 01 | $3\pi/4$ |
> | 11 | $5\pi/4$ |
> | 10 | $7\pi/4$ |
> 
> Gray coding ensures that adjacent constellation points differ in **only one bit**, minimizing bit errors.

**QPSK Spectral Efficiency:** Because 2 bits are transmitted per symbol, the **bandwidth efficiency doubles** to $\eta = 1 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$ compared to BPSK, at the **same $E_b/N_0$ performance**.

## 2.4 Generation of PSK Signals

### 2.4.1 BPSK Generation — Using a Balanced Modulator

The BPSK modulator consists of:
1. A **NRZ encoder** that maps '0' → +1 V and '1' → −1 V.
2. A **balanced modulator** (ring modulator) that multiplies the carrier with the NRZ signal.
3. The output is the BPSK waveform.

### 2.4.2 QPSK Generation — Using I/Q Modulator

The QPSK modulator uses:
1. **Bit-splitter:** separates input bitstream into **odd (I)** and **even (Q)** bits.
2. Two **NRZ encoders** produce the $I(t)$ and $Q(t)$ bipolar signals.
3. Two **balanced modulators** multiply the I and Q streams with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
4. The two BPSK outputs are **summed** to form the QPSK waveform.

## 2.5 Detection (Demodulation) of PSK

### 2.5.1 Coherent Detection (for BPSK)

1. The received BPSK signal is **multiplied** by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. The product is passed through a **low-pass filter (LPF)**.
3. A **threshold detector** decides '0' if the integrator output is positive, and '1' if negative.

### 2.5.2 Coherent Detection (for QPSK)

The received QPSK signal is split into **two arms**:
- The **I-arm** is multiplied by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrated.
- The **Q-arm** is multiplied by $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrated.
- A **decision circuit** decodes the pair of outputs back into 2 bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Recovery:** Coherent detection requires a **phase-coherent local oscillator**. In practice, a **Costas loop** or **squaring loop** is used for carrier recovery from the suppressed-carrier PSK signal.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the exam-critical equations. Memorize these.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symbol energy vs bit energy | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ | Joules |
| 2 | Bit rate vs symbol rate | $R_b = R_s \log_2 M$ | bits/s |
| 3 | Bandwidth (BPSK, raised-cosine) | $B = \dfrac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha)$ | Hz, $\alpha$ = roll-off |
| 4 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B} = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{1+\alpha}$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 5 | BPSK Bit Error Rate (BER) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | QPSK Bit Error Rate (BER) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | **Same as BPSK!** |
| 7 | M-ary PSK Symbol Error Rate (SER) | $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)$ | Approx. for large $E_s/N_0$ |
| 8 | M-ary PSK BER (approx.) | $P_b \approx \dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M}$ | For Gray coding |
| 9 | Minimum phase separation | $\Delta\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{2\pi}{M}$ | Radians |
| 10 | Q-function relation | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Useful identity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Q-function:** $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$ — the area under the tail of a unit-variance Gaussian PDF. **Always tabulate $Q(x)$ values** for $x = 0, 1, 2, 3$ in the exam.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of PSK

- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Uses BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM adaptive modulation.
- **Satellite communication:** BPSK/QPSK are standard for telemetry.
- **GSM cellular:** Uses GMSK (a variant) but the underlying principles are PSK-derived.
- **Deep-space communication (NASA):** BPSK remains the modulation of choice due to its **maximum power efficiency**.
- **5G NR:** Uses $\pi/2$-BPSK for uplink control channels to reduce PAPR.

## 2.8 Suggestions on Project Topics — Engineering Domains

The following project topics align with PBMRT504 outcomes. Students are expected to select, simulate, and validate these.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Categorized Project Topic Bank (KTU-Aligned):**

**Category A — Pure Simulation (MATLAB/Simulink/Python)**
1. BER performance comparison of BPSK, QPSK, and 8-PSK over AWGN channel.
2. Design and simulation of a QPSK transceiver with carrier synchronization using a Costas loop.
3. Implementation of an M-ary PSK modem with adaptive modulation under varying SNR.
4. Effect of Rayleigh fading on PSK schemes — comparative BER analysis.

**Category B — Software-Defined Radio (SDR) Implementation**
5. Real-time BPSK transmitter-receiver using USRP/ADALM-PLUTO SDR.
6. QPSK communication link with channel coding (convolutional code + Viterbi decoder).

**Category C — Signal Processing Integrated Projects**
7. Speech transmission over a QPSK link with LPC-based source coding.
8. OFDM-based PSK transmission to combat multipath fading.
9. Image transmission over PSK-modulated wireless link (bit error resilience study).
10. Real-time ECG signal transmission over a BPSK link with Manchester encoding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The most **scored** projects are those that include both **theoretical analysis** (BER curves, mathematical derivations) and **hardware/simulation verification** (constellation diagrams, eye diagrams, spectrum plots).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BPSK Bit Error Probability over AWGN

### Problem Setup
A BPSK signal $s(t) = \pm \sqrt{2E_b/T_b} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is transmitted over an **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. The received signal is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 1, 2$$

where $n(t)$ is zero-mean white Gaussian noise.

### Step-by-Step Coherent Detection

**Step 1:** Multiply the received signal by the locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = s_i(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $2\cos^2(\theta) = 1 + \cos(2\theta)$ to simplify:

$$s_1(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} \left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Pass through the integrate-and-dump (low-pass filter) over one bit duration $T_b$. The high-frequency $2f_c$ component is removed:

$$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + n_0$$

where $n_0$ is the noise sample at the decision instant, which is **zero-mean Gaussian** with variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$.

**Step 4:** Decision rule:

$$\hat{b} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } y > 0 \\ 1 & \text{if } y < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5:** Probability of error. The decision variable $y$ is Gaussian with:
- Mean $= +\sqrt{E_b}$ when bit '0' is sent
- Mean $= -\sqrt{E_b}$ when bit '1' is sent
- Variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$ in both cases

An error occurs when the sign of $y$ contradicts the transmitted bit. By symmetry:

$$P_b = P(y < 0 \mid \text{bit } 0 \text{ sent}) = P(\sqrt{E_b} + n_0 < 0)$$

**Step 6:** Standardize using the substitution $u = n_0/\sigma$:

$$P_b = P\!\left(n_0 < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) = P\!\left(\frac{n_0}{\sigma} < -\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sigma}\right)$$

Substituting $\sigma = \sqrt{N_0/2}$:

$$P_b = P\!\left(u < -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

This is the **single most important formula** in the entire PBMRT504 digital communication module.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: QPSK Bit Error Rate from BPSK Equivalence

### Step-by-Step Justification

**Step 1:** Express the QPSK signal in I/Q form:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \left[I_k \cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k \sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]$$

where $I_k, Q_k \in \{\pm 1/\sqrt{2}\}$ are the I and Q components for the $k$-th symbol.

**Step 2:** The QPSK signal can be **viewed as two independent BPSK signals** transmitted on orthogonal carriers:
- One BPSK on $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with energy $E_s/2$ per bit
- One BPSK on $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ with energy $E_s/2$ per bit

**Step 3:** Since the I and Q components are **orthogonal** and noise is **independent** in the two arms, the bit error on the I-arm and Q-arm are **statistically independent**.

**Step 4:** Total bit error probability:

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = P(\text{I-bit error}) + P(\text{Q-bit error}) - P(\text{I-bit error})P(\text{Q-bit error})$$

**Step 5:** For each BPSK arm, the energy per bit is $E_b = E_s/2$. By the BPSK result:

$$P(\text{I-bit error}) = P(\text{Q-bit error}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{2N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Let $p = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)$. Then:

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = 2p - p^2 \approx 2p \quad \text{(for small } p\text{)}$$

Wait — let me re-examine this more carefully:

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = 2p - p^2$$

For $E_s/N_0 \gg 1$, $p \ll 1$, so $p^2 \ll 2p$. Hence:

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

But the bit error rate is the **probability of bit error**, not symbol error. The probability of symbol error is $P_s = 1 - (1-p)^2 = 2p - p^2$, and the BER (for Gray coding) is:

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = \frac{P_s}{2} \approx \frac{2p}{2} = p = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

**Key Insight:** QPSK achieves **the same BER as BPSK** but uses **half the bandwidth** — a clear engineering trade-off win.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth Efficiency Comparison

### BPSK Spectral Efficiency

Bit duration $T_b$, symbol duration $T_s = T_b$ (1 bit per symbol).

Using a raised-cosine filter with roll-off $\alpha$:

$$B_{\text{BPSK}} = \frac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha) = \frac{1}{2T_b}(1+\alpha)$$

Spectral efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{BPSK}} = \frac{R_b}{B_{\text{BPSK}}} = \frac{1}{(1+\alpha)/2} = \frac{2}{1+\alpha}$$

For $\alpha = 0$: $\eta_{\text{BPSK}} = 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ (often cited as **0.5** when $\alpha = 1$, the practical case).

### QPSK Spectral Efficiency

Bit duration $T_b$, symbol duration $T_s = 2T_b$ (2 bits per symbol).

$$B_{\text{QPSK}} = \frac{R_s}{2}(1+\alpha) = \frac{R_b/2}{2}(1+\alpha) = \frac{R_b}{4}(1+\alpha)$$

Spectral efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{QPSK}} = \frac{R_b}{B_{\text{QPSK}}} = \frac{4}{1+\alpha}$$

For $\alpha = 0$: $\eta_{\text{QPSK}} = 2 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$ — **double BPSK's efficiency**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU Exam Style):**
> A QPSK system operates with a bit rate of $R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps}$ over an AWGN channel with PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-9} \text{ W/Hz}$. The received signal power is $P_r = 1 \text{ mW}$. Find:
> (a) the symbol rate
> (b) the bit error rate
> (c) the minimum bandwidth (raised-cosine, $\alpha = 0.5$)

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Symbol rate for QPSK:

$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2} = 10^6 \text{ symbols/s} = 1 \text{ Msps}$$

**Part (b):** Calculate $E_b/N_0$:

$$E_b = \frac{P_r}{R_b} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^6} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J}$$

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{E_b}{2 \cdot (N_0/2)} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.25$$

$$\frac{2E_b}{N_0} = 0.5$$

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{0.5}\right) = Q(0.7071)$$

From the standard Q-function table: $Q(0.7071) \approx 0.2398$.

**Part (c):** Minimum bandwidth:

$$B = \frac{R_s}{2}(1+\alpha) = \frac{10^6}{2}(1+0.5) = 750 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Using $B = \frac{R_b}{2\log_2 M}(1+\alpha) = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 2}(1.5) = 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 = 750 \text{ kHz}$ ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PSK Schemes

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | 8-PSK | 16-PSK |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bits/symbol | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Bandwidth efficiency $\eta$ (bits/s/Hz, $\alpha=0$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Minimum $E_b/N_0$ for $P_b = 10^{-5}$ | 9.6 dB | 9.6 dB | ~13 dB | ~17 dB |
| Power efficiency | **Best** | **Best (equal to BPSK)** | Lower | **Worst** |
| Susceptibility to phase error | Low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Amplitude fluctuations | None | None | None | None |
| Receiver complexity | Low | Medium | High | Very high |
| KTU Exam relevance | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** As $M$ increases, **bandwidth efficiency improves** but **power efficiency degrades** — a fundamental trade-off in digital communication.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
**Q: Compare BPSK and QPSK in terms of bandwidth efficiency and power efficiency. Justify why QPSK is preferred in bandwidth-limited systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK |
|---|---|---|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth efficiency (raised cosine, $\alpha = 0$) | $1$ bit/s/Hz | $2$ bits/s/Hz |
| Power efficiency (BER for given $E_b/N_0$) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical** |
| Minimum $E_b/N_0$ for $P_b = 10^{-5}$ | ~9.6 dB | ~9.6 dB |

**Justification:** QPSK carries **twice the information rate** within the **same bandwidth** as BPSK while requiring the **same transmitted power per bit** to achieve the same BER. In bandwidth-limited channels (e.g., satellite transponders, mobile cellular), QPSK is therefore preferred because it doubles the throughput without sacrificing power efficiency or BER performance.

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Q: What is meant by "constant envelope" in PSK? Why is it advantageous in non-linear amplification?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **constant envelope** signal is one whose **amplitude remains constant** regardless of the modulating data. In PSK, only the **phase** of the carrier changes; the **amplitude and frequency remain fixed**.

**Mathematical condition:** $|s(t)| = A_c = \text{constant for all } t$.

**Advantages in non-linear amplification:**
1. **Immunity to AM-AM and AM-PM distortion:** Non-linear amplifiers (e.g., Class-C, traveling wave tubes) introduce amplitude-dependent phase shifts. Since PSK has no amplitude variation, these distortions are avoided.
2. **Higher power efficiency:** Non-linear amplifiers can operate in **saturation** without distorting the signal, achieving efficiencies of 60–80% (vs. ~30% for linear Class-A).
3. **No spectral regrowth:** The output spectrum is clean, with minimal out-of-band emissions, reducing adjacent-channel interference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **constant envelope** with **constant power spectral density**. They are *not* the same. Constant envelope means constant *instantaneous* amplitude, while PSD can still vary with frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — BPSK & QPSK Detailed Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the bit error probability of BPSK over an AWGN channel. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of QPSK. Show that the BER of QPSK is the same as BPSK. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions:**
- Coherent carrier recovery at the receiver (perfect phase synchronization).
- AWGN channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.
- Equi-probable binary symbols.

**Transmitted signals:**

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \text{bit } 0$$

$$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \text{bit } 1$$

**Received signal:** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Detection — Step 1:** Multiply by the local carrier:

$$r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = s_i(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate over one bit period $T_b$:

$$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_0$$

where $n_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ is the noise at the decision instant.

**Step 3:** Decision rule: decide '0' if $y > 0$; decide '1' if $y < 0$.

**Step 4:** Probability of error (by symmetry, conditioned on bit '0' sent):

$$P_b = P(y < 0 \mid s_1 \text{ sent}) = P\!\left(\sqrt{E_b} + n_0 < 0\right)$$

$$P_b = P\!\left(n_0 < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) = P\!\left(\frac{n_0}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} < -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

**Asymptotic behavior:** For large $E_b/N_0$, BER decreases exponentially.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**QPSK Generation (refer to Diagram 4.6 above for block diagram):**

1. The input bitstream $b(t)$ of rate $R_b$ is **split** into two streams:
   - $I(t)$: odd-indexed bits at rate $R_b/2$
   - $Q(t)$: even-indexed bits at rate $R_b/2$

2. Each stream is **NRZ-encoded** into bipolar signals ($\pm 1$ V).

3. The I-channel is **modulated** by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the Q-channel by $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (90° phase shift).

4. The two BPSK outputs are **summed**:

$$s(t) = I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

**QPSK Detection:**
1. The received signal is **split** into two arms.
2. The I-arm multiplies by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$; the Q-arm multiplies by $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
3. Each arm passes through an **integrate-and-dump** circuit.
4. A **threshold detector** decides the polarity of each integrator output.
5. A **parallel-to-serial converter** recombines the two bitstreams.

**Proof of BER equivalence to BPSK:**

The I and Q channels are **independent** BPSK modulations. The energy per bit in each channel is $E_b = E_s/2$ (since total symbol energy $E_s$ is split equally).

Each channel has BER:

$$P(\text{bit error on I}) = P(\text{bit error on Q}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

Total symbol error rate: $P_s = 1 - (1-p)^2 = 2p - p^2$, where $p = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

For Gray-coded mapping, **symbol errors result in only one bit error** (since adjacent points differ in one bit). Hence:

$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{2} \approx p = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = P_{b,\text{BPSK}}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: M-ary PSK & Project Topic

**(a)** Explain M-ary PSK with constellation diagram for $M = 8$. Derive an approximate expression for the symbol error probability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Suggest and justify a suitable project topic in the area of digital modulation. Outline the methodology, tools, and expected outcomes. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

In **M-ary PSK**, the carrier phase takes one of $M$ equally spaced values:

$$\theta_k = \frac{2\pi k}{M}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$$

For $M = 8$, the eight phases are $\{0, \pi/4, \pi/2, 3\pi/4, \pi, 5\pi/4, 3\pi/2, 7\pi/4\}$, all on the unit circle.

**General transmitted signal:**

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi k}{M}\right)$$

**Constellation for 8-PSK (textual description):**
- Eight points equally spaced on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$.
- Each point is at $45°$ intervals.
- Minimum angular separation: $\Delta\theta = 2\pi/M = \pi/4 = 45°$.

**Symbol Error Probability Derivation:**

Consider two adjacent points at angular separation $2\pi/M$. The **minimum Euclidean distance** between them is:

$$d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

For an AWGN channel, the probability that the noise vector takes the received point across the **perpendicular bisector** between two adjacent constellation points is:

$$P(\text{error} \mid k) = Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min}/2}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)$$

Accounting for **two adjacent neighbors** per symbol and using the **union bound**:

$$\boxed{P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)}$$

This is valid for large $E_s/N_0$ where the error contributions from non-adjacent symbols are negligible.

**Bit error rate** (for Gray coding, $P_b \approx P_s/\log_2 M$):

$$P_b \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Recommended Project Topic:**
**"BER Performance Analysis and Real-Time Implementation of QPSK Transceiver over AWGN and Rayleigh Fading Channels using Software Defined Radio (SDR)"**

**Justification (Why this topic?):**
1. Directly maps to **CO4** (Design of communication systems) and **CO5** (Modern communication trends).
2. Integrates **theory** (BER math, QPSK modulator/demodulator) with **practical implementation** (SDR hardware).
3. Demonstrates understanding of **channel effects** (AWGN vs. Rayleigh) — a high-weightage KTU topic.

**Methodology:**
1. **Phase 1 — Theoretical analysis:** Derive BER expressions for QPSK in AWGN and Rayleigh fading.
2. **Phase 2 — Simulation:** Implement the QPSK transceiver in **MATLAB/Simulink** using the Communications Toolbox. Plot BER vs. $E_b/N_0$ curves.
3. **Phase 3 — Hardware implementation:** Use an **ADALM-PLUTO** or **USRP B200** SDR to transmit and receive QPSK over the air. Compare measured BER with theoretical predictions.

**Tools Required:**
- MATLAB R2023a with Communications Toolbox
- ADALM-PLUTO SDR (or USRP)
- Python (NumPy, SciPy) for offline data analysis
- Standard PC with MATLAB-compatible hardware

**Expected Outcomes:**
1. BER vs. $E_b/N_0$ plots for AWGN and Rayleigh channels (comparison with theoretical curves).
2. Constellation diagrams at the receiver showing noise-induced spreading.
3. Eye diagrams demonstrating the effect of channel impairments.
4. Live over-the-air QPSK link with measured error rates.
5. A formal project report with **at least 40 pages**, validated results, and code listings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common ways students lose marks in PSK questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Q-function** for QPSK symbol error. Remember: QPSK has *two* BPSK channels, so $P_s = 2Q(\sqrt{E_s/N_0})$.
> 2. **Confusing $E_s$ with $E_b$:** $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$. For QPSK, $E_s = 2E_b$. Forgetting this gives wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Not specifying roll-off factor:** A bandwidth question without stating $\alpha$ is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption list** in BER derivations: Examiners specifically look for "coherent detection," "AWGN," and "equi-probable symbols" as assumptions.
> 5. **In project reports:** Failing to compare simulation results with theoretical curves — this is a major CO5 mapping gap.
> 6. **In QPSK block diagrams:** Drawing the modulator and demodulator incorrectly (forgetting the 90° phase shift in the Q-arm). Always label both carriers explicitly.
> 7. **Using degrees instead of radians** in M-ary PSK phase expressions. The KTU convention is **radians**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PSK & Project Topics**

**Core Concept Pillars:**
- ☐ PSK encodes data in the **phase** of a constant-amplitude carrier.
- ☐ BPSK: 2 phases, 1 bit/symbol, BER $= Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
- ☐ QPSK: 4 phases, 2 bits/symbol, BER $= Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **same as BPSK**.
- ☐ M-ary PSK: $M$ phases, $\log_2 M$ bits/symbol, SER $\approx 2Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M))$.
- ☐ Energy relation: $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$.
- ☐ Bandwidth: $B = R_s(1+\alpha)/2$ for raised-cosine filtering.
- ☐ Spectral efficiency: BPSK = 1 bit/s/Hz; QPSK = 2 bit/s/Hz (at $\alpha=0$).

**Numerical Memorization:**
- ☐ $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(1) \approx 0.1587$, $Q(2) \approx 0.0228$, $Q(3) \approx 0.00135$.
- ☐ $Q(x) = 0.5\,\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$.
- ☐ For $P_b = 10^{-5}$, BPSK/QPSK need $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB.

**Conceptual Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ QPSK has the **same power efficiency** as BPSK, not better.
- ☐ Higher-order PSK (8, 16) is **less power-efficient** but **more bandwidth-efficient**.
- ☐ Coherent detection **requires carrier recovery** — PSK is suppressed-carrier.
- ☐ Gray coding reduces BER by ensuring adjacent symbol errors cause single-bit errors.

**Project Topic Selection Strategy:**
- ☐ Choose a topic that combines **theory + simulation** (and optionally hardware).
- ☐ Ensure the topic maps to **at least 2 KTU Course Outcomes**.
- ☐ Use standard tools: MATLAB, Python, Simulink, ADALM-PLUTO, USRP.
- ☐ Always include **theoretical + simulated + (hardware) BER curves** in the final report.
- ☐ Document the **mathematical model**, the **simulation code**, and the **validation results**.

**Quick-Reference Symbol Table:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $E_b$ | Energy per bit |
| $E_s$ | Energy per symbol |
| $N_0$ | Noise PSD (single-sided) |
| $R_b$ | Bit rate (bits/s) |
| $R_s$ | Symbol rate (symbols/s or baud) |
| $T_b$ | Bit duration |
| $T_s$ | Symbol duration |
| $M$ | Number of M-ary symbols |
| $\alpha$ | Roll-off factor of raised-cosine filter |
| $P_b$ | Bit error rate (BER) |
| $P_s$ | Symbol error rate (SER) |
| $Q(x)$ | Gaussian Q-function |
| $\eta$ | Spectral efficiency (bits/s/Hz) |

**Final KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *"PSK = Phase modulation, BPSK = simplest, QPSK = bandwidth-saver, Gray code = BER-saver, coherent detection = synchronization-required, constant envelope = power-amplifier-friendly."*
